## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Mass Balance of Paddy Processing

Let us derive the by-product yield from a paddy processing unit.

**Given:** 
- Input: **M_p** = mass of paddy (kg)
- Milling recovery (head rice yield): **Y_h** = 65%
- Total rice yield (head + broken): **Y_t** = 70%
- Moisture content of paddy: **M_p%** = 12%

**Step 1:** Calculate total rice produced.

$$
M_{rice} = M_p \times Y_t = M_p \times 0.70
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate broken rice (since 5% of total rice is typically broken).

$$
M_{broken} = M_{rice} \times 0.05 = M_p \times 0.70 \times 0.05 = 0.035\, M_p
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate head rice.

$$
M_{head} = M_{rice} - M_{broken} = 0.70 M_p - 0.035 M_p = 0.665\, M_p
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate rice husk yield (typically 20%).

$$
M_{husk} = M_p \times 0.20
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate rice bran yield (typically 8–10%).

$$
M_{bran} = M_p \times 0.09
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate total by-products.

$$
M_{by\text{-}products} = M_{husk} + M_{bran} + M_{broken} = 0.20\,M_p + 0.09\,M_p + 0.035\,M_p
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{by\text{-}products} = 0.325\,M_p}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every **1000 kg** of paddy processed, **325 kg** of by-products are generated — a huge secondary resource pool.

### 3.2 Compositional Mass Balance (Protein Recovery)

**Given:** Soybean meal composition: **Protein (P_s) = 48%**, **Moisture (M_s) = 10%**, **Oil (O_s) = 2%**.

**Step 1:** Calculate protein in 1 tonne meal.

$$
P_{mass} = 1000 \times 0.48 = 480 \text{ kg protein}
$$

**Step 2:** Determine moisture-free protein content.

$$
P_{dry} = \frac{P_{mass}}{1 - M_s} = \frac{480}{1 - 0.10} = \frac{480}{0.90}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{dry} \approx 533.33 \text{ kg protein/tonne}}
$$

### 3.3 Bioactives Yield Calculation from Rice Bran

**Given:** Rice bran contains **γ-oryzanol = 20,000 ppm** (i.e., **20,000 mg/kg = 20 g/kg**).

**Step 1:** Calculate oryzanol in 1 kg bran.

$$
O_{mass} = 1 \text{ kg} \times 20 \text{ g/kg} = 20 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2:** If bran yield is **90 kg/tonne paddy**, total oryzanol per tonne paddy:

$$
O_{total} = 90 \times 20 = 1800 \text{ g} = 1.8 \text{ kg/tonne paddy}
$$

**Step 3:** At commercial value of **₹5000/kg** oryzanol (approximate nutraceutical market price):

$$
\text{Revenue} = 1.8 \text{ kg} \times ₹5000/\text{kg} = ₹9000 \text{ per tonne of paddy}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This derivation is a KTU favorite.** The examiner tests the student's ability to *quantify the economic value of by-product utilization* using basic mass balance.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for By-Product Selection

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **select appropriate utilization pathways** based on compositional analysis. The following decision matrix is a high-yield KTU framework:

| Decision Parameter | High-Protein By-Product (>30% protein) | High-Carbohydrate By-Product (>50% carb) | High-Lipid By-Product (>15% lipid) | High-Fiber By-Product (>30% fiber) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Utilization** | Protein isolate / Animal feed | Bioethanol / Fermentation | Lipid extraction / Biodiesel | Dietary fiber / Biopolymer |
| **Processing Route** | Alkaline extraction, isoelectric precipitation | Enzymatic hydrolysis, SSF (Solid-State Fermentation) | Solvent extraction, Transesterification | Alkali/acid hydrolysis, Bleaching |
| **Value Addition** | Functional ingredient, Meat analog | Biogas, Biofuel, Organic acids | Oryzanol, Squalene, Nutraceutical | Prebiotic, Composite flour |
| **Example By-Product** | Soy meal, Groundnut cake | Rice husk (after cellulose extraction), Spent grain | Rice bran, Coconut meal | Wheat bran, Pulse husk, Sunflower hull |
| **Target Market** | Food/Feed industry | Energy/Fuel industry | Nutraceutical/Pharma | Bakery/Health food industry |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation: Biodiesel Yield from Soap Stock

**Given:** Soap stock contains **40% FFA** (free fatty acid), with molecular weight of oleic acid = **282 g/mol**.

**Step 1:** Calculate FFA in 1000 kg soap stock.

$$
\text{FFA mass} = 1000 \times 0.40 = 400 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply acid-catalyzed esterification reaction:

$$
\text{FFA} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{FAME (Biodiesel)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Stoichiometry:** 1 mole FFA → 1 mole FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester).

**Step 3:** Calculate moles of FFA (assuming pure oleic acid).

$$
n_{FFA} = \frac{400{,}000 \text{ g}}{282 \text{ g/mol}} \approx 1418 \text{ moles}
$$

**Step 4:** Theoretical biodiesel (FAME) mass.

$$
m_{FAME} = 1418 \times (282 + 14) = 1418 \times 296
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{FAME} \approx 419{,}728 \text{ g} \approx 419.7 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Interpretation:** 1 tonne of soap stock (40% FFA) → ~**420 kg biodiesel**, with **5–10% loss** considered for real-world efficiency.

### 3.6 Process Sequence for Soy Protein Isolate (SPI)

A frequently tested KTU process diagram in written form:

$$
\text{Soybean meal} \xrightarrow{\text{Defatting (hexane)}} \text{Defatted meal} \xrightarrow{\text{Alkaline extraction (pH 9–10, 50°C)}} \text{Soluble proteins}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Centrifugation}} \text{Clear extract} \xrightarrow{\text{Isoelectric precipitation (pH 4.5)}} \text{Curd (SPI)}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Neutralization, Drying}} \text{SPI powder (}\geq 90\%\text{ protein)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Explain utilization of soybean meal as a value-added product,"* this 5-step flow with **pH 4.5** as the isoelectric point of glycinin is the complete model answer.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**"Differentiate between rice husk and rice bran with respect to composition and utilization."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Rice Husk | Rice Bran |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Outer siliceous layer of paddy | Inner pericarp + aleurone + germ |
| **Yield (% paddy)** | 18–22% | 8–10% |
| **Major Component** | Silica (15–22%), Cellulose (35–40%) | Lipid (15–22%), Protein (12–17%) |
| **Calorific Value** | ~14 MJ/kg | ~19 MJ/kg |
| **Key Utilization** | Fuel, Silica, Xylanase, Activated carbon | Edible oil, Oryzanol, Animal feed |

Rice husk is **fibrous-siliceous**, while rice bran is **lipid-proteinaceous**, dictating completely different downstream utilization pathways.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition: 1 mark; Comparison: 1.5 marks; Utilization link: 0.5 mark.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**"List any four anti-nutritional factors (ANFs) commonly found in oilseed meals and state one method to detoxify each."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Phytic Acid** (Soybean, Mustard, Sesame) → Detoxified by **Phytase enzyme treatment** or **Soaking in 0.5% HCl**.
2. **Trypsin Inhibitors** (Soybean) → Destroyed by **Autoclaving at 121°C for 15 min**.
3. **Glucosinolates** (Mustard, Rapeseed meal) → Reduced by **Myrosinase inactivation via heat** and **aqueous leaching**.
4. **Gossypol** (Cottonseed) → Detoxified by **solvent extraction (hexane)** or **iron sulfate treatment**.
5. **Aflatoxins** (Groundnut) → Reduced by **Ammoniation** or **UV irradiation**.

**Mark Distribution:** 4 ANFs × 0.5 mark; 4 methods × 0.25 mark = 2 + 1 = 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Q.A(a). [7 Marks]** Discuss the various by-products generated during the processing of cereals. Explain the characterization and effective utilization of rice bran and wheat bran in detail.

**Q.A(b). [7 Marks]** Describe the steps involved in the production of rice bran oil. Discuss the nutraceutical significance of γ-oryzanol and the various commercial applications.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: List the cereal by-products (2 marks)**
- Rice: husk, bran, germ, broken rice
- Wheat: bran, germ, middlings
- Maize: bran, germ, steep liquor, gluten meal
- Barley (Brewery): spent grain

**Step 2: Characterization of Rice Bran (2 marks)**
- **Composition (% dry basis):** Protein 12–17%, Lipid 15–22%, Ash 8–12%, Fiber 10–14%, Carbohydrate 35–45%
- **Bioactives:** γ-oryzanol (10,000–20,000 ppm), Tocopherols (200–400 mg/kg), Phytosterols (~10,000 ppm)
- **Characterization Parameters:** Acid value, Peroxide value, Saponification value, Iodine value, Free fatty acid %, Color (Lovibond)
- **Anti-nutritional factors:** Phytic acid (4–5%), Trypsin inhibitor (low)

**Step 3: Characterization of Wheat Bran (1 mark)**
- **Composition:** Protein 14–17%, Arabinoxylan 25–30%, Cellulose 10–15%, Starch 10–15%, Phytate 4–5%
- **Characterization:** Soluble/Insoluble fiber ratio, AX content, Color, Particle size

**Step 4: Effective Utilization (2 marks)**
- **Rice Bran:** 
  1. **Rice Bran Oil (RBO)** extraction via solvent (hexane) or expeller → refining → nutraceutical-grade oil.
  2. **Defatted bran** → Animal feed, Protein concentrate, Fermentation substrate.
  3. **Oryzanol extraction** → Nutraceutical capsules, Cosmetics, Sunscreens.
- **Wheat Bran:**
  1. **Bakery fortification** (5–15% in atta, biscuits, bread).
  2. **Arabinoxylan extraction** → Prebiotic, Functional food.
  3. **Phytase production** via Solid-State Fermentation (SSF).
  4. **Wheat Bran Oil** (rich in Vitamin E).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Rice Bran Oil (RBO) Production Process (3 marks)**

The process flow is:

$$
\text{Rice Bran} \xrightarrow{\text{Stabilization (steam/heat)}} \text{Inactive Lipase}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Solvent Extraction (hexane)}} \text{Mixed Miscella}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Distillation}} \text{Crude RBO} \xrightarrow{\text{Refining (degumming, neutralization, bleaching, deodorization)}} \text{Refined RBO}
$$

- **Stabilization:** Heating to 90–100°C for 10–15 min to inactivate **lipase** enzyme (which would otherwise hydrolyze triglycerides into FFA, increasing acid value).
- **Solvent extraction:** Hexane at 55–65°C, oil recovery ~95–98%.
- **Refining:** Four stages as above. The deodorizer distillate is a valuable by-product containing tocopherols.

**Step 2: γ-Oryzanol — Chemistry and Nutraceutical Significance (2.5 marks)**

- **Chemical Nature:** γ-Oryzanol is a **mixture of steryl ferulates**, primarily **cycloartenyl ferulate, 24-methylenecycloartanyl ferulate, and campesteryl ferulate**.
- **Properties:** 
  - **Antioxidant** — 4× more potent than Vitamin E (α-tocopherol).
  - **Hypocholesterolemic** — Lowers total and LDL cholesterol.
  - **Anti-inflammatory** and **anti-aging** properties.
  - **UV protection** — Used in sunscreen formulations.
- **Dosage:** 300 mg/day in clinical studies for cholesterol management.

**Step 3: Commercial Applications of RBO (1.5 marks)**
1. **Edible oil** — Premium cooking medium (high smoke point ~250°C, balanced fatty acid profile).
2. **Cosmetics** — Hair oils, skincare (oryzanol as UV protectant).
3. **Pharmaceuticals** — Soft gel capsules for dyslipidemia.
4. **Oryzanol extraction** — Sell to nutraceutical industry.
5. **Soap making** (using soap stock from refining).
6. **Biodiesel** (from soap stock and degraded oil).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B(a). [7 Marks]** Explain the by-products of the oilseed processing industry. Describe the production, characterization, and utilization of soybean meal and mustard cake.

**Q.B(b). [7 Marks]** Discuss the various by-products generated during pulse milling. Explain the characterization and effective utilization of pulse husk and dal powder.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Oilseed By-Products List (2 marks)**
- **Oil Cake** (mechanical pressing, 4–8% residual oil)
- **Oil Meal** (solvent extraction, <2% oil)
- **Hulls/Shells** (fibrous, low protein)
- **Soap Stock** (FFA, phospholipids)
- **Gums/Lecithin** (phosphatidylcholine source)
- **Deodorizer Distillate (DOD)** (tocopherols, sterols, squalene)
- **Spent Bleaching Earth** (oil-laden clay)

**Step 2: Soybean Meal — Characterization (2 marks)**
- **Composition:** Protein 44–48%, Carbohydrate 30–35%, Fiber 5–7%, Moisture 10–12%
- **Critical Parameters:** 
  - **Trypsin Inhibitor Activity (TIA):** < 4 mg/g (post-heat treatment)
  - **Urease Activity:** pH rise < 0.1 (confirms adequate heat treatment)
  - **KOH Solubility:** 78–85%
  - **PDI (Protein Dispersibility Index):** >40% for feed grade
- **Anti-nutritional factors:** Trypsin inhibitors, Lectins, Phytate, Oligosaccharides (raffinose, stachyose)

**Step 3: Soybean Meal — Utilization (1.5 marks)**
1. **Poultry and swine feed** (primary global use).
2. **Soy Protein Isolate (SPI)** — 5-step process: Defatting → Alkaline extraction (pH 9–10) → Centrifugation → Isoelectric precipitation (pH 4.5) → Drying.
3. **Textured Soy Protein (TSP)** — Meat analog.
4. **Fermentation substrate** for probiotics, tempeh, miso.

**Step 4: Mustard Cake — Characterization and Utilization (1.5 marks)**
- **Composition:** Protein 36–40%, Glucosinolates 4–8% (allyl isothiocyanate), Sinapine 1–2%
- **Characterization:** Glucosinolate HPLC, Allyl isothiocyanate (AITC) by GC-MS
- **Utilization:**
  1. **Bio-pesticide** (AITC is nematicidal and insecticidal).
  2. **Biofumigant** for soil-borne pathogens.
  3. **Protein isolate** (after detoxification).
  4. **Organic fertilizer** (N-rich).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Pulse Milling By-Products (2 marks)**
- Pulse milling sequence: Cleaning → Conditioning → Dehusking → Splitting → Polishing
- **By-products:**
  1. **Husk** (~10–15% of seed weight)
  2. **Dal Powder** (~2–5%)
  3. **Broken Dal** (~3–6%)
  4. **Soaking/conditioning water** (with soluble sugars and anti-nutritional factors)

**Step 2: Pulse Husk — Characterization (2 marks)**
- **Composition:** Cellulose 35–45%, Hemicellulose 20–25%, Lignin 10–15%, Crude fiber 50–60%, Protein 3–5%, Tannins 2–5%, Ash 3–5%
- **Characterization Parameters:**
  - **Tannin content** (Folin-Ciocalteu method)
  - **Crude fiber** (AOAC method)
  - **Total Dietary Fiber (TDF)** — Soluble + Insoluble
  - **Color, Bulk density, Water Absorption Capacity (WAC)**, **Oil Absorption Capacity (OAC)**

**Step 3: Pulse Husk — Utilization (1.5 marks)**
1. **Dietary fiber supplement** — Incorporated into biscuits, bread, pasta.
2. **Composting / Vermicomposting** — N-rich organic manure.
3. **Tannin extraction** — Leather industry, Pharmaceutical applications.
4. **Mushroom cultivation substrate** (especially oyster mushroom).
5. **Biofuel** — Pellets, briquettes.

**Step 4: Dal Powder — Utilization (1.5 marks)**
- **Composition:** Protein 20–25%, Starch 50–60%, Fiber 3–5%
- **Utilization:**
  1. **Soup mixes and ready-to-eat products.**
  2. **Snack fortification** (extruded products, namkeen).
  3. **Bakery fortification** (breads, cookies).
  4. **Animal feed** (with amino acid fortification).
  5. **Bengal gram dal powder** (besan) → Traditional sweets, pakoras.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up "by-product" and "waste":** By-products have *economic value*; waste does not. Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** if this distinction is missing.
> 
> 2. **Listing without compositional data:** Just writing "rice bran is used as animal feed" scores **only 1 mark out of 3**. Always support with **composition (%)** and **specific compound (e.g., γ-oryzanol, 20,000 ppm)**.
> 
> 3. **Missing process flow in Part B:** For 7-mark questions, examiners expect **3–4 sequential process steps**. A bullet list of utilization is insufficient — a **flow diagram with conditions (pH, temperature, time)** is mandatory.
> 
> 4. **No mention of anti-nutritional factors (ANFs):** When discussing oilseed meal or pulse by-products, the examiner expects ANFs and their detoxification. **Skipping this loses 2–3 marks.**
> 
> 5. **Not linking characterization to utilization:** "Brute-force memorization" of utilization lists is penalized. Always show the **logic chain**: Composition → Property → Process → Product.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring mass balance:** A numerical question on by-product yield requires an **explicit calculation**, not a verbal estimate. Use the mass balance derivations from Section 3.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ✅ **Cereal by-products** include rice husk, rice bran, wheat bran, wheat germ, broken rice, spent grain, corn steep liquor, corn gluten meal.
- ✅ **Pulse by-products** include husk (chaff), dal powder, broken dal, process effluent.
- ✅ **Oilseed by-products** include oil cake, oil meal, hulls, soap stock, gums/lecithin, deodorizer distillate, spent earth.
- ✅ **Characterization involves:** Proximate composition (moisture, ash, protein, fat, fiber, carbohydrates), Bioactive profile (phenolics, oryzanol, tocopherols), Anti-nutritional factors (phytate, tannin, trypsin inhibitor, glucosinolate, gossypol, aflatoxin), Functional properties (WAC, OAC, emulsification, foaming).
- ✅ **Rice bran** contains **15–22% lipid** and **20,000 ppm γ-oryzanol**; **Rice husk** contains **15–22% silica** and **~20% yield**.
- ✅ **Soybean meal** is **44–48% protein** with **lysine (6.2%)**; **Mustard cake** contains **4–8% glucosinolates**.
- ✅ **Isoelectric point of soy protein:** **pH 4.5** (critical for SPI production).
- ✅ **Heat treatment of soybean meal:** **121°C, 15 min** autoclaving to destroy trypsin inhibitors.
- ✅ **Soap stock to biodiesel:** Acid esterification of **FFA + methanol → FAME** (biodiesel).
- ✅ **Aflatoxin B1** is the most toxic aflatoxin; detoxification includes **ammoniation and UV treatment**.
- ✅ **Mass balance of paddy:** ~**20% husk + 9% bran + 3.5% broken = 32.5% by-products**.
- ✅ **Calorific value of rice husk:** **14–16 MJ/kg** (suitable for boiler fuel).
- ✅ **Conversion to value:** RBO, SPI, biodiesel, γ-oryzanol, dietary fiber, organic acids (lactic acid from CSL), prebiotics, bioplastics.
- ✅ **Circular bioeconomy principle:** Every by-product is a *misallocated resource* — characterization unlocks its value.
- ✅ **Diagram-based questions:** Always draw **flow diagrams with mass percentages** for full marks in 7-mark questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked a 14-mark question, structure your answer as: **(i) List (2 marks) → (ii) Characterization with composition table (3 marks) → (iii) Process flow with conditions (4 marks) → (iv) Utilization with specific products (3 marks) → (v) Diagram/Flowchart (2 marks).** This is the proven KTU scoring template.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fruits, Vegetables and Plantation Products
Fermented Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fermented Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fermented Foods (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *Fermented foods are food substrates that have been subjected to the controlled action of microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, or molds) and their associated enzymes, resulting in desirable biochemical transformations and the formation of characteristic organic acids, alcohols, esters, gases, and other metabolites. From a food-industry-waste-management perspective, fermentation processes are critical intervention points where raw agricultural substrates are partially converted into a primary food product while simultaneously generating a parallel stream of solid residues, liquid effluents, and spent biomass.*

### 1.2 Classification of Fermentation Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four (4) principal fermentation pathways in the food industry determine the **type**, **volume**, and **composition** of waste generated per unit of finished product. KTU examiners frequently test this classification because each pathway yields a different waste profile.

| S.No | Fermentation Type | Key Microbe | Primary Metabolite | Industrial Waste Signature |
|------|-------------------|-------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Lactic Acid Fermentation (LAF) | *Lactobacillus*, *Streptococcus* | Lactic acid | Whey, brine effluent |
| 2 | Alcoholic Fermentation (AF) | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | Ethanol + CO₂ | Spent wash, spent grain |
| 3 | Acetic Acid Fermentation (AAF) | *Acetobacter*, *Gluconobacter* | Acetic acid | Mother liquor, sludge |
| 4 | Alkaline / Mold Fermentation | *Aspergillus*, *Rhizopus*, *Bacillus* | Ammonia / proteases | Solid residue (okara, koji waste) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **The "Kitchen Composting Jar" Analogy:**
> Imagine you leave a jar of chopped cabbage on a kitchen counter for a few days with some salt sprinkled on it. Natural bacteria already living on the cabbage leaves begin multiplying. They feed on the cabbage sugars and release lactic acid as a by-product. The acid drops the pH, the cabbage softens, develops a tangy flavor, and the food is preserved. That tangy, softened cabbage is **sauerkraut** — a classical lactic acid fermented food. From a waste perspective, the salt-water that drains out of the jar is **brine effluent**, and any leftover stalks/leaves are **solid waste**. Industrial-scale fermentation simply does the same thing inside enormous stainless-steel tanks, but produces tons of both food *and* waste that must be managed responsibly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:**
> - Typical **BOD of distillery spent wash** = **40,000 – 50,000 mg/L** (highly polluting)
> - Typical **BOD of cheese whey** = **30,000 – 50,000 mg/L**
> - Typical **BOD of bakery/brewery effluent** = **1,500 – 4,000 mg/L**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Biochemistry of the Major Fermentations

#### A. Lactic Acid Fermentation (Homofermentative Pathway)

- **Step 1 — Glycolysis:** Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is split via the Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) pathway.
- **Step 2 — Pyruvate Reduction:** Pyruvate is reduced by the enzyme **lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)** using NADH as a cofactor.
- **Step 3 — Acid Build-Up:** Lactic acid accumulates, pH drops to **~3.5 – 4.5**, inhibiting spoilage organisms.

#### B. Alcoholic Fermentation

- **Step 1 — Glycolysis (EMP Pathway):** One mole of glucose yields 2 moles of pyruvate + 2 ATP + 2 NADH.
- **Step 2 — Decarboxylation:** Pyruvate decarboxylase converts pyruvate → acetaldehyde + CO₂.
- **Step 3 — Reduction:** Alcohol dehydrogenase reduces acetaldehyde → ethanol; NADH is regenerated.

#### C. Acetic Acid Fermentation

- **Step 1 — Pre-existing Ethanol:** Ethanol from prior alcoholic fermentation is the substrate.
- **Step 2 — Oxidation:** *Acetobacter* oxidizes ethanol → acetaldehyde (via alcohol dehydrogenase).
- **Step 3 — Hydration & Oxidation:** Acetaldehyde → acetic acid via aldehyde dehydrogenase, fixing **O₂**.

#### D. Solid-State Fermentation (SSF)

- **Step 1 — Substrate Preparation:** Moist solid substrate (e.g., steamed rice, soybean) is inoculated.
- **Step 2 — Fungal/Bacterial Growth:** *Aspergillus oryzae* (koji mold) secretes proteases, amylases, lipases.
- **Step 3 — Brining/Aging:** Salt-tolerant LAB and yeasts continue fermentation for weeks–months.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value symbols in table entries (never the bare pipe `\vert` character that breaks markdown tables).

| S.No | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Unit |
|------|---------------------|-------------|--------------|
| F-1 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_3H_6O_3$ | Homofermentative lactic acid pathway (glucose → lactic acid) | Stoichiometric |
| F-2 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$ | Alcoholic fermentation (Gay-Lussac equation) | Stoichiometric |
| F-3 | $C_2H_5OH + O_2 \rightarrow CH_3COOH + H_2O$ | Acetic acid fermentation | Stoichiometric |
| F-4 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{V}$ | Standard 5-day BOD for fermented effluent (Winkler method) | mg/L |
| F-5 | $Y_{p/s} = \dfrac{\text{Product formed (g/L)}}{\text{Substrate consumed (g/L)}}$ | Product yield coefficient | g/g |
| F-6 | $\eta_{fermentation} = \dfrac{\text{Actual ethanol produced}}{\text{Theoretical ethanol}} \times 100\%$ | Fermentation efficiency | % |
| F-7 | $P_{\text{biogas}} = Q_{eff} \times (COD_{in} - COD_{out}) \times 0.35$ | Biogas potential from fermented food effluent (Q = flow, 0.35 = m³ CH₄/kg COD removed) | m³/day |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Waste Management

- **Spent grains from brewing** are valorised as cattle feed, bran substitutes, or feedstock for biogas.
- **Cheese whey** (≈ 9 kg per 1 kg cheese) is a high-BOD effluent; recovered for **lactose**, **whey protein concentrates**, or anaerobically digested.
- **Spent wash from distilleries** is the single most polluting liquid waste stream in the Indian food sector; treated via biomethanation, biocomposting, or concentration into **Pharma/CO₂-grade potash**.
- **Okara (soy pulp)** from soy-milk/tofu production is dried and used as dietary fibre or as a substrate for SSF.
- **Vegetable brine waste** (from pickle/kimchi industries) is a salty, acidic effluent requiring dilution + biological treatment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac Stoichiometry)

The complete derivation is shown below — every algebraic and biochemical step is written out explicitly as required by the KTU 2024 valuation scheme.

**Starting from the Gay-Lussac equation for alcoholic fermentation:**

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the molecular masses involved**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{glucose}} &= 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180 \text{ g/mol} \\
M_{\text{ethanol}} &= 2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46 \text{ g/mol} \\
M_{\text{CO}_2} &= 12 + 2(16) = 44 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* We tabulate molecular weights because stoichiometric yield must be expressed on a mass basis for industrial calculations.

**Step 2 — Apply stoichiometric ratios**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From 180 g glucose} &\rightarrow 2 \times 46 \text{ g ethanol} = 92 \text{ g ethanol} \\
\text{Hence, theoretical yield} &= \dfrac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \text{ g ethanol / g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the **Gay-Lussac constant (0.511 g/g)** — the maximum possible ethanol yield, ignoring cell-mass and by-product formation.

**Step 3 — Convert to volumetric yield (percent alcohol by volume, ABV)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Density of ethanol} &= 0.789 \text{ g/mL} \\
\text{Volume of ethanol per g} &= \dfrac{1}{0.789} = 1.267 \text{ mL/g} \\
\therefore \text{Volume yield} &= 0.5111 \times 1.267 = 0.6476 \text{ mL ethanol / g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* For every gram of glucose fermented, the theoretical maximum is **0.6476 mL of pure ethanol**, or equivalently **64.76 %** of the substrate mass as ethanol volume.

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage fermentation efficiency**

$$
\eta_{fermentation} = \dfrac{\text{Actual ethanol produced}}{\text{Theoretical ethanol}} \times 100\%
$$

For a typical industrial brewery that achieves **8 % w/v ethanol** from a **20 % w/v glucose** substrate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual} &= 8 \text{ g ethanol per 100 mL} = 0.08 \text{ g/mL} \\
\text{Theoretical} &= 0.20 \text{ g glucose/mL} \times 0.5111 = 0.1022 \text{ g ethanol/mL} \\
\eta_{fermentation} &= \dfrac{0.08}{0.1022} \times 100\% = 78.27\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Industrial efficiencies of **80–95 %** are acceptable; the gap is due to cell-mass synthesis, glycerol, higher alcohols, and unfermented sugars.

### 3.2 Derivation: BOD of a Spent Wash Sample (Winkler Method)

The **5-day Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** is the most-cited parameter in KTU exam questions on fermented food waste. The standard formula is:

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}
$$

Where:
- $D_1$ = DO of diluted sample on Day 0 (mg/L)
- $D_2$ = DO of diluted sample on Day 5 (mg/L)
- $B_1$ = DO of dilution water blank on Day 0 (mg/L)
- $B_2$ = DO of dilution water blank on Day 5 (mg/L)
- $f$ = ratio of dilution water to sample in the diluted sample
- $P$ = decimal volumetric fraction of sample in the diluted sample

**Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**

A distillery spent wash sample is diluted such that **$f = 0.02$** (i.e., 2 % sample, 98 % dilution water). The recorded values are:

$$
D_1 = 8.4 \text{ mg/L}, \quad D_2 = 3.0 \text{ mg/L}, \quad B_1 = 8.6 \text{ mg/L}, \quad B_2 = 8.2 \text{ mg/L}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BOD}_5 &= \dfrac{(8.4 - 3.0) - (8.6 - 8.2) \times 0.02}{0.02} \\
&= \dfrac{(5.4) - (0.4 \times 0.02)}{0.02} \\
&= \dfrac{5.4 - 0.008}{0.02} \\
&= \dfrac{5.392}{0.02} \\
&= 269.6 \text{ mg/L (of diluted sample)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the BOD of the *diluted* sample. To express it as BOD of the *original* undiluted spent wash, we must divide by the dilution factor:

$$
\text{BOD}_5^{\text{original}} = 269.6 \times \dfrac{100}{2} = 13{,}480 \text{ mg/L}
$$

A value of ~13,480 mg/L is consistent with typical molasses-based distillery spent wash (literature range: **40,000 – 50,000 mg/L** for untreated wash — confirming the need for further dilution in the test).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Fermented Food → Waste Stream → Management Strategy

| Fermented Product | Substrate | Key Waste Stream | Quantity of Waste | Management Strategy |
|-------------------|-----------|------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Beer / Whisky | Barley / Molasses | Spent grain, spent wash | 85 % of raw grain as spent grain; 8–15 L spent wash / L alcohol | Anaerobic digestion (biogas), cattle feed |
| Yogurt / Cheese | Milk | Cheese whey | 9 L whey / 1 kg cheese | Whey protein recovery, lactose extraction |
| Vinegar | Wine / Cider | Acetic acid mother liquor, sludge | 5–10 % of total volume | Neutralization, composting |
| Soy Sauce | Soybean + Wheat | Okara (insoluble pulp) | 1.1 kg okara / kg soy processed | Dried into flour, SSF substrate |
| Sauerkraut / Kimchi | Cabbage | Brine effluent | 30–50 % of cabbage mass | Dilution + biological treatment |
| Bread | Wheat flour | Stale bread, dough waste | 5–10 % of production | Animal feed, ethanol via fermentation |
| Idli / Dosa batter | Rice + Black gram | No major liquid waste, but spent solids | Variable | Composting |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Generic Industrial Fermentation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Agricultural Substrate] --> B1[Cleaning and Sorting]
    B1 --> C1[Size Reduction / Milling]
    C1 --> D1[Cooking / Pasteurization]
    D1 --> E1[Cooling to Inoculation Temperature]
    E1 --> F1[Inoculation with Starter Culture]
    F1 --> G1[Controlled Fermentation Tank]
    G1 --> H1[Maturation / Aging]
    H1 --> I1[Finished Fermented Food Product]
    G1 --> J1[Liquid Effluent Stream]
    H1 --> K1[Solid Spent Biomass]
    J1 --> L1[Wastewater Treatment]
    K1 --> M1[Composting or Drying]
    I1 --> N1[Packaging and Distribution]
    L1 --> O1[Treated Water Discharge]
    M1 --> P1[By-Product Valorisation]
```

*Caption:* A block-level functional architecture flow of a generic industrial fermentation unit. The diagram clearly distinguishes the **product pathway** (A1 → I1 → N1) from the **waste pathway** (J1 + K1 → L1/M1 → O1/P1). This dual-flow concept is central to KTU Module 2.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Waste Management Hierarchy for Fermented Food Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Fermented Food Waste Generated] --> nodeB{Waste Type?}
    nodeB -->|Solid| nodeC[Spent Grain / Okara / Pomace]
    nodeB -->|Liquid| nodeD[Spent Wash / Whey / Brine]
    nodeC --> nodeE{Valorisation Possible?}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF[Animal Feed / Substrate for SSF]
    nodeE -->|No| nodeG[Composting]
    nodeD --> nodeH{BOD Level?}
    nodeH -->|High BOD > 10,000| nodeI[Anaerobic Digestion Biogas]
    nodeH -->|Moderate BOD| nodeJ[Aerobic Biological Treatment]
    nodeH -->|Low BOD| nodeK[Direct Discharge after Polishing]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Biogas Energy Recovery]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Sludge Dewatering]
    nodeG --> nodeN[Organic Fertilizer]
    nodeF --> nodeO[Value-Added Product]
    nodeL --> nodeP[Sustainable Energy]
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeK --> nodeP
```

*Caption:* The hierarchy follows the standard **5 R's** of waste management — Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Residual Disposal. KTU examiners expect students to recognize that **anaerobic digestion** is the *recovery-of-energy* stage for high-BOD fermented effluents.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Microbial Pathways → Waste Products Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX1[Glucose Substrate] --> nodeY1[Yeast Inoculation]
    nodeX1 --> nodeY2[LAB Inoculation]
    nodeX1 --> nodeY3[Acetobacter Inoculation]
    nodeY1 --> nodeZ1[Ethanol and CO2]
    nodeY2 --> nodeZ2[Lactic Acid]
    nodeY3 --> nodeZ3[Acetic Acid]
    nodeZ1 --> nodeW1[Beer Wine Whisky]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeW2[Yogurt Cheese Pickles]
    nodeZ3 --> nodeW3[Vinegar]
    nodeW1 --> nodeS1[Spent Wash Spent Grain]
    nodeW2 --> nodeS2[Whey Brine]
    nodeW3 --> nodeS3[Acetobacter Sludge]
```

*Caption:* A functional mapping of microbial inoculum → fermentation pathway → finished food → characteristic waste stream. Useful for KTU short-answer questions asking *"Identify the waste stream generated by [specific fermented food]."*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
*Define fermented foods. List any four (4) major fermented foods with the microorganism responsible for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Fermented foods are foods produced by the controlled growth of microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, or molds) and the enzymatic conversion of substrates into organic acids, alcohols, CO₂, and other metabolites (1 mark).
>
> Four major fermented foods and their responsible microorganisms (½ mark each):
> 1. **Yogurt** — *Lactobacillus bulgaricus* and *Streptococcus thermophilus*
> 2. **Beer** — *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*
> 3. **Sauerkraut** — *Leuconostoc mesenteroides* (followed by *Lactobacillus* spp.)
> 4. **Soy sauce** — *Aspergillus oryzae* (koji mold) followed by *Tetragenococcus halophilus* and *Zygosaccharomyces rouxii*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
*Mention any three (3) waste streams generated by the fermented food industry and state one management strategy for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Waste Stream (1 mark) | Source | Management Strategy (½ mark each) |
|------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------|
| Spent wash from distilleries | Ethanol fermentation of molasses | Anaerobic digestion for biogas recovery |
| Cheese whey | Lactic acid fermentation of milk | Lactose and whey protein recovery |
| Okara | Soy sauce / tofu production | Drying and use as animal feed / SSF substrate |

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students frequently **omit the chemical equation** when describing fermentation pathways — this alone costs 2 marks.
> - Many confuse *homofermentative* and *heterofermentative* LAB. Homofermenters produce only lactic acid; heterofermenters produce lactic acid + CO₂ + ethanol.
> - When asked about waste management, students name *biogas* but **fail to mention the BOD/COD reduction percentage** — losing the numerical component.
> - In derivation questions, always **show the molecular-weight step** before applying stoichiometry.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of balanced chemical equations, describe the following fermentation pathways and mention one industrial food product for each: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Lactic acid fermentation
   (ii) Alcoholic fermentation
   (iii) Acetic acid fermentation

**(b)** Explain the major waste streams generated by the **dairy fermentation industry** (yogurt, cheese, buttermilk). Discuss the environmental impact of cheese whey and outline any **two (2) sustainable management strategies** with their basic principles. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Lactic Acid Fermentation (2 marks)**

The homofermentative lactic acid pathway converts one mole of glucose into two moles of lactic acid via the EMP pathway. The balanced equation is:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Lactate dehydrogenase}} 2\,C_3H_6O_3
$$

The responsible microbes are homofermentative LAB such as *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus* and *Streptococcus thermophilus*. **Industrial product:** Yogurt.

**(ii) Alcoholic Fermentation (2 marks)**

The Gay-Lussac equation describes the conversion of glucose to ethanol and carbon dioxide, catalysed by the enzyme complex zymase in yeasts.

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2
$$

The principal organism is *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*. **Industrial product:** Beer or wine.

**(iii) Acetic Acid Fermentation (2 marks)**

Acetic acid fermentation is an oxidation of ethanol carried out by *Acetobacter aceti* in the presence of atmospheric oxygen:

$$
C_2H_5OH + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Alcohol dehydrogenase}} CH_3COOH + H_2O
$$

**Industrial product:** Vinegar (1 mark for tying it together).

**(b) — 7 marks**

The dairy fermentation industry produces three principal waste streams (1 mark each for naming):
1. **Cheese whey** (≈ 9 L per kg cheese produced)
2. **Lactose-rich permeate** from whey fractionation
3. **Cleaning-in-Place (CIP) wastewater** containing caustic, acid, and dairy residues

**Environmental Impact of Cheese Whey (2 marks):**
- Cheese whey has a BOD₅ of **30,000 – 50,000 mg/L** and COD of **60,000 – 80,000 mg/L**.
- Discharge into natural water bodies causes severe oxygen depletion, fish kills, and eutrophication.
- Approximately **190 million tonnes** of whey are produced globally per year; mismanagement is a major ecological concern.

**Sustainable Management Strategies (2 marks):**

*Strategy 1 — Whey Protein Recovery via Membrane Filtration:*
Ultrafiltration (UF) with 10 kDa cut-off membranes separates whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin) from lactose and minerals, producing **Whey Protein Concentrate (WPC-80)**. This converts a pollutant into a marketable ingredient valued at **USD 3–8/kg**.

*Strategy 2 — Anaerobic Biogas Production:*
High-strength whey is fed into a UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket) reactor operating at **35 °C mesophilic** conditions. Hydraulic retention time ≈ 5–10 days. Methane yield ≈ **0.30–0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed**, with **80–90 % COD reduction**. The biogas can be co-generated with thermal energy for the dairy plant.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe **solid-state fermentation (SSF)** with reference to *koji* preparation. Mention the substrate, microorganism, and enzymes involved. List two (2) fermented foods produced by SSF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **spent wash** generated by the Indian distillery industry. Explain its characteristic pollutant load (BOD, COD, colour) and elaborate **two (2) waste-management technologies** currently employed in India for its safe disposal and valorisation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Solid-State Fermentation (SSF) Defined (1 mark):**
SSF is a fermentation process in which microorganisms grow on a moist, solid, non-soluble substrate in the **absence (or near-absence) of free-flowing water**. Water activity ($a_w$) is maintained in the range **0.4 – 0.8**, suitable for fungi.

**Koji Preparation (3 marks):**
- **Substrate:** Steamed polished rice (or steamed soybean–wheat mixture for soy sauce).
- **Microorganism:** *Aspergillus oryzae* (also called "koji-kin").
- **Enzymes secreted by *A. oryzae*:**
  - **Amylases** (saccharify starch → maltose + glucose)
  - **Proteases** (hydrolyse proteins → peptides + amino acids)
  - **Lipases** (split fats → free fatty acids)
- The inoculated rice is spread in thin wooden trays (traditionally) and incubated at **28 – 32 °C** for **40 – 48 hours** until the grains are covered with a greenish-yellow mycelium ("bloom").

**Two Fermented Foods Produced by SSF (2 marks):**
1. **Soy sauce (Shoyu)** — produced from koji + soybean + wheat + brine fermentation (18–24 months).
2. **Miso** — produced from koji + cooked soybean + salt (fermented 6 months – 3 years).
3. *(Optional third)* **Tempeh** — Rhizopus oligosporus fermentation of boiled soybeans.

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Spent Wash Overview (2 marks):**
Spent wash (also called *distillery wastewater* or *vinasse*) is the liquid residue left after the distillation of alcohol from fermented molasses. India produces approximately **3.0 – 3.5 billion litres of alcohol** annually, generating **40 – 50 billion litres of spent wash** per year.

**Characteristic Pollutant Load (2 marks):**
- **pH:** 3.5 – 4.5 (strongly acidic)
- **BOD₅:** **40,000 – 50,000 mg/L**
- **COD:** **80,000 – 120,000 mg/L**
- **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** **50,000 – 70,000 mg/L**
- **Colour:** Dark brown due to **melanoidin** pigments (resistant to biological degradation)
- **Temperature:** **80 – 100 °C** (hot discharge)

**Waste-Management Technologies Employed in India (3 marks):**

*Technology 1 — Anaerobic Biomethanation (Biogas Plant):*
Spent wash is cooled to **35 – 40 °C**, neutralized partially with lime, and fed into a **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** or **anaerobic filter** for **15 – 30 days HRT**. Methane yield ranges from **0.30 – 0.45 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed**. The biogas is used in boiler furnaces, displacing fossil fuels. Effluent BOD reduction: **70 – 85 %**.

*Technology 2 — Concentration into Potash (Composting with Bio-compost):*
Biogas-treated spent wash is concentrated by **multiple-effect evaporators** into a syrup of **60 – 65 % solids**, then mixed with **press mud (bagasse filter cake)** and **microbial consortium** to produce bio-compost rich in **K₂O (potassium)** — sold as a soil conditioner. This recovers **potash fertilizer** and solves the disposal problem simultaneously.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density):**

- **Fermented foods** = food + microbes + controlled enzymes → organic acids, alcohols, CO₂.
- **Four major fermentation pathways:** Lactic Acid, Alcoholic, Acetic Acid, Alkaline/Mold (SSF).
- **Homofermenters:** only lactic acid (e.g., *L. bulgaricus*). **Heterofermenters:** lactic acid + CO₂ + ethanol (e.g., *Leuconostoc*).
- **Gay-Lussac equation** — alcohol fermentation stoichiometry — must be memorized verbatim: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$.
- **Theoretical ethanol yield** = **0.511 g/g glucose** OR **0.6476 mL/g glucose** — a board favourite for 2- and 3-mark questions.
- **Koji** = steamed rice + *Aspergillus oryzae*; central to Japanese/Chinese fermented foods (soy sauce, miso, sake).
- **Cheese whey** = ~9 L per kg of cheese; BOD ≈ 30,000–50,000 mg/L.
- **Spent wash** (distillery) — BOD ≈ 40,000–50,000 mg/L — single largest polluting effluent in Indian food industry.
- **BOD₅ formula** (Winkler) must be applied with the dilution factor correction.
- **Anaerobic digestion** is the *primary* treatment technology for both whey and spent wash.
- **Valorisation hierarchy for fermented food waste:** Animal feed → Biogas → Compost → Recovery of compounds (lactose, protein, potash).
- **SSF** uses minimal water; ideal for fungi; produces enzymes (amylase, protease, lipase).
- **Industrial examples to remember:** Beer (yeast), Yogurt (LAB), Vinegar (Acetobacter), Tempeh (Rhizopus), Soy Sauce (Aspergillus + LAB + Yeast).
- **Common KTU pitfall:** not stating the **dilution factor** in BOD problems — leads to zero marks despite correct arithmetic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dairy Industry and Milk Products Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dairy Industry and Milk Products Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is the Dairy Industry?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The dairy industry is a segment of the food processing sector that handles the collection, processing, and distribution of milk and milk-derived products. It encompasses the complete value chain from farm-level raw milk handling to the manufacture of value-added products such as butter, cheese, ghee, yogurt, condensed milk, ice cream, and milk powder.

The dairy industry is one of the **largest food-processing sectors in India** and globally, generating enormous volumes of both **solid waste and liquid effluent** per unit of processed milk. For every **1 litre of milk processed**, approximately **1 to 3 litres of wastewater** are generated, depending on the hygiene regime and the products being made.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** When studying this topic, students must focus on (1) the **nature and characteristics** of waste streams, (2) the **volume** of waste generated, and (3) the **treatment technologies** applicable to each stream.

### 1.2 What is the Milk Products Industry?

The **milk products industry** is the downstream sub-sector of the dairy industry that converts raw or pasteurized milk into manufactured, shelf-stable, or semi-preserved products. The key product categories include:

- **Fermented products** – yogurt, curd, kefir, cheese
- **Concentrated products** – condensed milk, evaporated milk, milk powder (skim milk powder, whole milk powder)
- **Fat-based products** – butter, ghee, cream
- **Frozen products** – ice cream, frozen yogurt
- **Protein products** – casein, whey protein concentrates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for waste management:** Each manufactured product leaves behind a **specific by-product stream** (whey from cheese, buttermilk from butter, permeate from milk powder, etc.). These by-products are themselves highly polluting due to their high organic content but are also rich in valuable nutrients (lactose, proteins, minerals), making **recovery and valorization** the central theme of modern dairy waste management.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Milk Is the Hero, the Whey Is the Sidekick":**
> Think of a cheese factory as a kitchen where you are making paneer. You heat milk, add an acid or enzyme (rennet), and the milk *curdles* into solid curds (which become cheese) and a greenish-yellow liquid called **whey**. The curds are the valuable product; the whey is the waste. But whey is not "garbage" — it contains nearly **half the nutrients of the original milk** (lactose, water-soluble proteins, vitamins, and minerals). Throwing it away is like pouring half your meal down the drain. This is exactly the problem in the **dairy industry at industrial scale**: tons of nutrient-rich effluent, generated daily, that can either pollute rivers (if discharged untreated) or be transformed into biogas, animal feed, or protein supplements (if managed correctly).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following benchmark values are commonly used in KTU-level dairy wastewater problems and are **essential for numerical questions**:

| Parameter | Typical Value for Dairy Effluent | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **pH** | 6.0 – 9.0 | — |
| **Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** | **1,000 – 3,000** | mg/L |
| **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** | **2,000 – 6,000** | mg/L |
| **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** | 500 – 1,500 | mg/L |
| **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | 1,000 – 3,000 | mg/L |
| **Oil & Grease** | 100 – 500 | mg/L |
| **Total Nitrogen** | 50 – 200 | mg/L |
| **Total Phosphorus** | 10 – 50 | mg/L |
| **Volume of wastewater per litre of milk** | **1.0 – 3.0** | L/L |
| **Whey generated per kg of cheese** | **8 – 10** | kg |
| **BOD/COD ratio** | **0.4 – 0.6** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **High BOD/COD ratio (>0.4)** is the signature characteristic of dairy wastewater. It tells us that the organic matter is **highly biodegradable**, which means biological treatment methods (especially anaerobic digestion) are highly effective and economically attractive.

### 1.5 The Composition of Milk — The Source of the Waste

Understanding the composition of milk is fundamental to understanding dairy waste, because the waste streams are simply **milk components that did not end up in the product**.

| Component | % by weight | Where does it end up? |
|---|---|---|
| **Water** | **~87.0 %** | Becomes the bulk of the wastewater |
| **Lactose (milk sugar)** | **~4.8 %** | Mostly lost in **whey** during cheese making |
| **Fat (milk fat)** | **~3.5 %** | Lost in **buttermilk**, **whey**, or as **floatable scum** in effluent |
| **Proteins (casein, whey proteins)** | **~3.2 %** | Casein goes into cheese; whey proteins lost in whey |
| **Minerals (ash)** | **~0.7 %** | Distributed in all streams |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often treat dairy waste as a "single" homogeneous stream. In reality, a dairy plant produces **multiple distinct waste streams**, each requiring a tailored management approach. Treating them as one will cost you marks in KTU ESE questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Dairy Processing Chain & Associated Waste Streams

To understand dairy waste, you must trace the milk through every step of processing. Each step generates a specific waste stream:

**Step 1 — Reception and Chilling of Raw Milk**
- Milk arrives at the dairy in tankers.
- Waste generated: **spilled milk, tanker wash water, dirt, dung residues**.
- Typical loss: **0.1 – 0.5 %** of intake.

**Step 2 — Pasteurization**
- Milk is heated to **72 °C for 15 s (HTST)** or **63 °C for 30 min (LTLT)**.
- Waste generated: **start-up and shut-down milk losses**, water used for heating/cooling.

**Step 3 — Standardization and Separation**
- Cream is separated from skim milk via centrifugation.
- Waste generated: **none significant directly**, but downstream processing creates streams.

**Step 4 — Homogenization**
- Fat globules are broken into smaller uniform sizes.
- Waste: minimal.

**Step 5 — Manufacture of Products** (cheese, butter, etc.) — see Section 2.2.

**Step 6 — Cleaning-In-Place (CIP)**
- After every production run, the entire plant is cleaned using **hot water, caustic soda (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO₃), and sanitizers**.
- Waste generated: **CIP effluent — the largest single waste stream by volume** in most dairies.
- Characteristics: high pH (10–12), high temperature (50–60 °C), contains milk solids, detergents, and sanitizers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIP effluent is typically 70–80 % of the total wastewater volume** in a dairy plant. It is *not* the most polluting stream (milk-rich process water is), but it dominates the hydraulic load on the Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP).

### 2.2 Waste Streams from Specific Milk Products

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite KTU question area**. Examiners love to test whether you know which product produces which waste.

#### A) Cheese Industry Waste

- **Cheese whey** is the greenish liquid left after curds are separated during cheese/casein manufacture.
- Per **100 kg of milk** used for cheese, approximately **80–90 kg of whey** is generated.
- Composition of whey:
  - Water: ~93 %
  - Lactose: **4.5 – 5.0 %**
  - Whey proteins: **0.6 – 0.8 %**
  - Fat: 0.3 – 0.5 %
  - Minerals (ash): **0.5 – 0.7 %**
- **BOD of cheese whey: 30,000 – 50,000 mg/L** (one of the most polluting food-industry effluents).
- **COD: 60,000 – 100,000 mg/L**.

> [!WARNING]
> Cheese whey has a **BOD roughly 10–20 times higher than domestic sewage**. Discharging untreated whey into a river is ecologically catastrophic — it rapidly depletes dissolved oxygen and kills aquatic life.

#### B) Butter Industry Waste

- **Buttermilk** is the aqueous phase left after churning cream into butter.
- Composition: water (~91 %), lactose (4.5 %), proteins (3.4 %), fat (0.5 %), minerals (0.7 %).
- BOD: **5,000 – 8,000 mg/L**.

#### C) Ice Cream Industry Waste

- Waste contains **fats, sugars, milk solids, stabilizers, and emulsifiers**.
- BOD: 2,000 – 4,000 mg/L; high fat makes floatation/DAF necessary.

#### D) Condensed/Dried Milk Industry Waste

- **Milk permeate** is the by-product of ultrafiltration of milk before evaporation.
- Rich in lactose and minerals; BOD: 20,000 – 30,000 mg/L.

### 2.3 Why Dairy Waste is a Special Problem

The dairy industry is unique among food industries for **three reasons**:

1. **High organic load per unit volume** — BOD often exceeds 3,000 mg/L, far beyond domestic sewage (200 mg/L).
2. **High water content of the raw material** — milk is 87 % water, so any spillage carries enormous pollutant load.
3. **Nutritional value of the waste** — unlike some industrial wastes, dairy waste is *itself* a food source for microbes, causing **rapid oxygen depletion** in receiving waters.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important table** for solving numerical questions on dairy waste management in KTU exams.

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wastewater volume per litre of milk** | $Q_{ww} = V_{water} \times f$ | $f$ = factor (1.0 – 3.0) | L/L |
| **BOD load to ETP** | $L_{BOD} = Q \times \text{BOD}_i \times 10^{-3}$ | $Q$ in L/d, BOD in mg/L → kg/d | kg BOD/d |
| **COD load to ETP** | $L_{COD} = Q \times \text{COD}_i \times 10^{-3}$ | Similar to BOD load | kg COD/d |
| **BOD/COD ratio (biodegradability)** | $R = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{\text{COD}}$ | $R \geq 0.4$ indicates good biodegradability | — |
| **Anaerobic biogas yield** | $Y_{bg} = 0.35 \times (\text{COD}_{in} - \text{COD}_{out})$ | Empirical for dairy | m³ CH₄/kg COD removed |
| **Whey generation from cheese** | $W = M_{milk} \times 0.85$ | Per kg of milk used for cheese | kg whey/kg milk |
| **Population equivalent of dairy effluent** | $PE = \dfrac{Q \times \text{BOD}}{60}$ | 1 PE = 60 g BOD/d (WHO) | Persons |
| **Activated sludge F/M ratio** | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V \times X}$ | $S_0$ = influent BOD, $V$ = aeration volume, $X$ = MLVSS | d⁻¹ |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Volume / flow rate | h or d |
| **Solids Retention Time (SRT)** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | For activated sludge | d |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** When computing population equivalent (PE), always use **per-capita BOD = 60 g/d** unless the problem specifies a different standard (e.g., Indian CPCB uses **54 g/d** in some older references; **always read the problem carefully**).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The BOD/COD ratio dictates the **choice of treatment technology**:

- If $R \geq 0.4$ (typical of dairy) → **Biological treatment** (anaerobic + aerobic) is the most cost-effective.
- If $R < 0.3$ → Biological treatment becomes inefficient; **physico-chemical treatment** (coagulation, Fenton oxidation) is preferred.
- High $R$ also means **anaerobic digestion is doubly attractive** because it (a) destroys BOD and (b) produces **biogas**, which is a renewable fuel that can offset the plant's energy costs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem 1 — Population Equivalent of a Dairy Plant

> **Question (KTU style):** A dairy plant processes **50,000 L of milk per day**. The wastewater generated is **2 L per litre of milk** processed. The average BOD₅ of the wastewater is **2,000 mg/L**. Calculate the **population equivalent** of the dairy plant. Assume per-capita BOD generation = **60 g/person/day**.

#### Step 1 — Calculate the total wastewater volume

$$
Q = 50{,}000 \ \text{L milk/d} \times 2 \ \text{L wastewater/L milk}
$$

$$
Q = 100{,}000 \ \text{L/d}
$$

**Explanation:** The factor 2 L/L of milk is a typical mid-range value for a dairy that includes CIP and processing losses. The problem could have given 1.0, 1.5, or 3.0 — always apply the given factor literally.

#### Step 2 — Calculate the total BOD load per day

$$
L_{BOD} = Q \times \text{BOD}_5 = 100{,}000 \ \text{L/d} \times 2{,}000 \ \text{mg/L}
$$

$$
L_{BOD} = 2 \times 10^{8} \ \text{mg/d}
$$

Convert mg to kg:

$$
L_{BOD} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{8}}{10^{6}} = 200 \ \text{kg/d}
$$

**Explanation:** $1 \ \text{kg} = 10^{6} \ \text{mg}$. This is the total mass of BOD being discharged per day.

#### Step 3 — Compute the population equivalent

$$
PE = \dfrac{L_{BOD}}{\text{per-capita BOD}} = \dfrac{200 \ \text{kg/d}}{60 \ \text{g/person/d}}
$$

Convert units so they match:

$$
PE = \dfrac{200{,}000 \ \text{g/d}}{60 \ \text{g/person/d}}
$$

$$
PE \approx 3{,}333 \ \text{persons}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The dairy plant's effluent has a **population equivalent of ≈ 3,333 persons**. This means treating this dairy's waste is equivalent (in terms of organic load) to providing sanitation to a small town of over 3,000 residents.

---

### 3.2 Problem 2 — Anaerobic Biogas Potential of Cheese Whey

> **Question:** A cheese plant processes **10,000 kg of milk/day** into cheese. **85 % of the milk becomes whey** (by mass). The whey has a **COD of 70,000 mg/L** and a **density of approximately 1.03 kg/L**. The anaerobic digester achieves **80 % COD removal**. Calculate the **daily methane (CH₄) production** in m³/d, using the empirical yield **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg of COD removed**.

#### Step 1 — Calculate the volume of whey produced

$$
M_{whey} = 10{,}000 \ \text{kg milk} \times 0.85 = 8{,}500 \ \text{kg whey}
$$

Density of whey ≈ 1.03 kg/L, so:

$$
V_{whey} = \dfrac{8{,}500 \ \text{kg}}{1.03 \ \text{kg/L}} \approx 8{,}252 \ \text{L}
$$

**Explanation:** The factor 0.85 means that 85 % of milk ends up as whey — this is the standard cheese yield ratio (cheese yield is only ~10 % of milk by mass).

#### Step 2 — Calculate the total COD load

$$
L_{COD} = V_{whey} \times \text{COD} = 8{,}252 \ \text{L} \times 70{,}000 \ \text{mg/L}
$$

$$
L_{COD} = 5.776 \times 10^{8} \ \text{mg/d} = 577.6 \ \text{kg COD/d}
$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate the COD removed per day

$$
L_{COD,removed} = 577.6 \times 0.80 = 462.1 \ \text{kg COD/d}
$$

**Explanation:** 80 % removal is a typical performance for a well-designed upflow anaerobic sludge blanket (UASB) reactor treating high-strength dairy waste.

#### Step 4 — Calculate methane production

$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \ \text{m}^3/\text{kg COD removed} \times 462.1 \ \text{kg COD/d}
$$

$$
V_{CH_4} \approx 161.7 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The plant can produce **≈ 162 m³ of methane per day** from whey alone. At a calorific value of ~35 MJ/m³ for methane, this corresponds to about **5.66 GJ/day of renewable energy** — enough to displace a significant portion of the plant's fossil-fuel usage.

---

### 3.3 Problem 3 — Aeration Tank Sizing for Activated Sludge

> **Question:** Design an aeration tank for the secondary treatment of a dairy effluent with the following data: **Flow Q = 500 m³/d; Influent BOD₅ S₀ = 1,500 mg/L; Effluent BOD₅ S = 30 mg/L; MLVSS X = 3,000 mg/L; F/M ratio = 0.25 d⁻¹; Y = 0.6; kd = 0.06 d⁻¹**. Calculate the **aeration tank volume V** and the **hydraulic retention time τ**.

#### Step 1 — Calculate the BOD load

$$
L = Q \times S_0 = 500 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 1{,}500 \ \text{g}/\text{m}^3
$$

Wait — we must be careful with units. Let $S_0 = 1{,}500 \ \text{mg/L} = 1.5 \ \text{kg/m}^3$.

$$
L = 500 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 1.5 \ \text{kg/m}^3 = 750 \ \text{kg BOD/d}
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the F/M ratio definition

$$
\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = 0.25 \ \text{d}^{-1}
$$

Rearrange to find V:

$$
V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}
$$

Convert X to kg/m³: $X = 3{,}000 \ \text{mg/L} = 3.0 \ \text{kg/m}^3$.

$$
V = \dfrac{500 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 1.5 \ \text{kg/m}^3}{3.0 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 0.25 \ \text{d}^{-1}}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{750}{0.75} = 1{,}000 \ \text{m}^3
$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate the hydraulic retention time

$$
\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1{,}000 \ \text{m}^3}{500 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d}} = 2 \ \text{days} = 48 \ \text{hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Aeration tank volume **V = 1,000 m³** and **HRT = 2 days**. This is a long HRT, typical of dairy wastewater because of its high organic load and the need for slow-growing microbes (especially if biological nutrient removal is included).

---

### 3.4 Problem 4 — BOD/COD Ratio Interpretation

> **Question:** A dairy wastewater sample has BOD₅ = 2,400 mg/L and COD = 4,500 mg/L. (a) Calculate the BOD/COD ratio. (b) Comment on the **biodegradability** of the effluent. (c) Recommend a treatment strategy.

#### Step 1 — Calculate the ratio

$$
R = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{\text{COD}} = \dfrac{2{,}400}{4{,}500} = 0.533
$$

#### Step 2 — Interpret

Since $R = 0.533 \geq 0.4$, the wastewater is **highly biodegradable**.

#### Step 3 — Recommend

> [!TIP]
> **Treatment Strategy:**
> 1. **Primary treatment** — screening, grit removal, oil & grease removal (DAF).
> 2. **Equalization** — to handle pH and load fluctuations from CIP and process streams.
> 3. **Anaerobic treatment (UASB/Anaerobic filter)** — for high-strength stream like whey, achieving 70–85 % COD removal with biogas recovery.
> 4. **Aerobic treatment (Activated Sludge / SBR / MBBR)** — to polish the effluent to meet discharge standards.
> 5. **Tertiary treatment** — Nutrient removal (N, P) and disinfection if discharging to surface water.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Treatment Technology

| Treatment | Target Pollutant | Dairy Application | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Screening** | Large solids | All streams | Cheap, simple | Removes only coarse solids |
| **DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation)** | Oils, fats, suspended solids | Butter, ice-cream, milk reception | Very effective for fat | High operating cost |
| **Equalization** | pH, flow, load fluctuations | CIP + process | Stabilizes downstream ETP | Requires large tank |
| **Anaerobic (UASB)** | High-strength organics (COD) | Whey, permeate | Energy recovery, low sludge | Slow startup, sensitive to shocks |
| **Activated Sludge** | BOD, COD, nutrients | Whole plant effluent | Proven, efficient | High aeration cost |
| **SBR (Sequencing Batch Reactor)** | BOD, nitrification, P removal | Small/medium dairies | Compact, flexible | Sophisticated control |
| **MBR (Membrane Bio-Reactor)** | BOD, TSS, bacteria | High-end dairies | Excellent effluent quality | Membrane fouling, cost |
| **Reverse Osmosis** | TDS, recovery | Permeate/whey concentration | Water reuse | Energy intensive |
| **Fenton Oxidation** | Recalcitrant COD | Polish step | Removes persistent colour | Chemical cost |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Dairy Processing & Associated Waste Streams

This Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete flow of milk through a dairy plant** with **each unit operation** and its **associated waste stream** clearly labeled. This is the diagram examiners expect when they ask you to "draw the waste generation pattern in a dairy."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    intake["A RAW MILK INTAKE"] --> reception["B RECEPTION AND CHILLING"]
    reception --> pasteu["C PASTEURIZATION HTST 72C 15s"]
    reception --> sep["C1 SEPARATION CREAM AND SKIM MILK"]
    pasteu --> stand["D STANDARDIZATION"]
    stand --> homo["E HOMOGENIZATION"]
    stand --> pack["F PACKAGING FLUID MILK"]

    sep --> cream["G CREAM"]
    cream --> butter["H BUTTER MANUFACTURE"]
    butter --> buttWaste["I WASTE BUTTERMILK BOD 5000 mg/L"]

    homo --> cheese["J CHEESE MANUFACTURE"]
    cheese --> curd["K CURD CHEESE PRODUCT"]
    cheese --> whey["L WASTE WHEY BOD 40000 mg/L"]

    homo --> mp["M MILK POWDER PLANT"]
    mp --> permeate["N WASTE PERMEATE BOD 25000 mg/L"]

    pack --> cip["O CLEANING IN PLACE CIP EFFLUENT 80 percent of flow"]
    cheese --> cip
    butter --> cip
    mp --> cip

    cip --> etp["P EQUALIZATION TANK"]
    buttWaste --> etp
    whey --> etp
    permeate --> etp
    etp --> anaero["Q ANAEROBIC UASB BIOREACTOR"]
    anaero --> biogas["R BIOGAS METHANE FUEL"]
    anaero --> aero["S AEROBIC ACTIVATED SLUDGE"]
    aero --> clarifier["T SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
    clarifier --> sludge["U WASTE ACTIVATED SLUDGE"]
    clarifier --> dis["V DISINFECTION AND DISCHARGE"]
    sludge --> dewat["W SLUDGE DEWATERING"]
    dewat --> cake["X DEWATERED CAKE BIOFERTILIZER OR LANDFILL"]
    biogas --> util["Y ENERGY RECOVERY BOILER OR CHP"]
```

**How to read this diagram for KTU exams:**
- Trace **every box** from left (intake) to right (effluent).
- Notice the **branching**: solid product lines (curd, butter, milk powder) versus **waste lines** (whey, buttermilk, permeate, CIP).
- All waste streams **converge in the equalization tank** before entering biological treatment.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — ETP for Dairy Wastewater

This diagram shows the **sequence of treatment units** in a typical dairy Effluent Treatment Plant. Examiners frequently ask "Explain with a flow diagram the treatment of dairy wastewater."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw["DAIRY EFFLUENT INLET"] --> screen["1 SCREEN BAR SCREEN 10 mm"]
    screen --> grit["2 GRIT CHAMBER"]
    grit --> daf["3 DAF OIL AND GREASE REMOVAL"]
    daf --> eq["4 EQUALIZATION TANK HRT 8 to 12 h"]
    eq --> neu["5 NEUTRALIZATION pH 6.5 to 7.5"]
    neu --> anaer["6 UASB ANAEROBIC BIOREACTOR HRT 2 to 5 d"]
    anaer --> aer["7 ACTIVATED SLUDGE AERATION TANK HRT 1 to 2 d"]
    aer --> cl["8 SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
    cl --> sludge["SLUDGE RECYCLE TO AER TANK"]
    cl --> dis["9 CHLORINATION DISINFECTION"]
    dis --> out["TREATED EFFLUENT TO DISCHARGE OR REUSE"]
    anaer --> bio["BIOGAS COLLECTION AND USE"]
    sludge --> dewt["SLUDGE THICKENER AND DEWATERING"]
    dewt --> cake["BIOSOLIDS BIOFERTILIZER OR CO-COMBUSTION"]
```

---

### 4.3 Concept Map — Waste Hierarchy Applied to Dairy

The **waste management hierarchy** (most preferred → least preferred) applied specifically to the dairy industry:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    prev["MOST PREFERRED PREVENTION"] --> min["MINIMIZATION SOURCE REDUCTION"]
    min --> reu["REUSE E.G. WHEY TO ANIMAL FEED"]
    reu --> rec["RECOVERY E.G. LACTOSE PROTEIN EXTRACTION"]
    rec --> recy["RECYCLING E.G. WATER RECYCLE IN PLANT"]
    recy --> treat["TREATMENT BIOLOGICAL ETP"]
    treat --> disp["LEAST PREFERRED DISPOSAL TO WATER BODY"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** KTU examiners award **extra marks** if, when asked about dairy waste treatment, you mention **recovery and reuse first** rather than jumping straight to ETP design. The NEP 2020 and KTU 2024 scheme explicitly emphasize the **circular economy** and **zero-liquid-discharge** mindset.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Schematic — Whey Management Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    whey["CHEESE WHEY 85 PERCENT OF MILK"] --> p1["PATHWAY 1 DIRECT BIOTREATMENT UASB"]
    p1 --> bio["BIOGAS AND EFFLUENT"]
    whey --> p2["PATHWAY 2 WHEY POWDER PRODUCTION"]
    p2 --> wp["DRIED WHEY POWDER FOOD INGREDIENT"]
    whey --> p3["PATHWAY 3 PROTEIN RECOVERY ULTRAFILTRATION"]
    p3 --> wpc["WHEY PROTEIN CONCENTRATE WPC 80"]
    p3 --> permeate["PERMEATE RICH IN LACTOSE"]
    permeate --> lact["LACTOSE CRYSTALLIZATION"]
    whey --> p4["PATHWAY 4 ANIMAL FEED LIQUID WHEY"]
    p4 --> feed["PIG AND CATTLE FEED"]
    whey --> p5["PATHWAY 5 BIOETHANOL FERMENTATION"]
    p5 --> eth["BIOETHANOL FUEL ADDITIVE"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for examiners:** A well-organized answer that lists these multiple pathways in a flowchart earns significantly more credit than a one-line statement that "whey is treated biologically."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define "Cheese Whey" and explain why it is considered one of the most polluting dairy effluents. State its typical BOD value.**

**Model Answer:**
Cheese whey is the greenish-yellow liquid by-product obtained after the separation of casein curd from milk during cheese manufacture. For every 100 kg of milk used in cheese making, about 80–90 kg of whey is generated. Whey contains approximately 4.5–5 % lactose, 0.6–0.8 % proteins, 0.5 % fat, and 0.5–0.7 % minerals, dissolved in about 93 % water. The high concentration of dissolved organic matter, particularly lactose, gives whey a typical **BOD₅ of 30,000–50,000 mg/L**, which is roughly **10–20 times that of domestic sewage**. Because of this very high organic load, discharge of untreated whey into water bodies causes rapid oxygen depletion, fish kills, and eutrophication, making it one of the **most polluting effluents in the entire food industry**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is Cleaning-In-Place (CIP) effluent? Why is it significant in the context of dairy waste management?**

**Model Answer:**
Cleaning-In-Place (CIP) is the automated procedure used to clean the interior surfaces of pipes, vessels, pasteurizers, and separators in a dairy plant **without disassembly**, using circulating hot water, caustic soda (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO₃), and sanitizers, typically at 50–60 °C. The resulting spent liquid is called **CIP effluent**. It is significant because:
- It constitutes **70–80 % of the total wastewater volume** in a dairy.
- It is **alkaline (pH 10–12)**, has high temperature, and contains dissolved milk solids, detergents, and sanitizers.
- It causes **large hydraulic and pH fluctuations** in the ETP, so it must be **neutralized and equalized** before biological treatment.
- It is the **single most important stream** that drives the design capacity of the ETP.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]**

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the **various waste streams generated in a dairy industry**, indicating the source, characteristics (BOD, COD, pH, suspended solids), and typical volume of each stream. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **flow diagram**, explain the **various treatment units** in a typical dairy Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP). Justify why **anaerobic treatment (UASB)** is recommended as the first biological step. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

The dairy industry generates **four major waste streams** of differing characteristics. The table below summarizes them — this is the exact kind of structured table KTU examiners reward:

| Waste Stream | Source | Volume | BOD₅ (mg/L) | COD (mg/L) | TSS (mg/L) | pH |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Milk reception waste** | Spillage, tanker washing | Low | 1,000–2,000 | 2,000–4,000 | 500–1,500 | 6–8 |
| **Process wastewater** | Pasteurizer start-up, homogenizer losses, packaging overflows | Moderate | 1,500–3,000 | 3,000–6,000 | 500–1,000 | 6–8 |
| **CIP effluent** | Cleaning of all equipment | **70–80 % of total flow** | 500–1,500 | 1,000–3,000 | 200–500 | **10–12 (alkaline)** |
| **Cheese whey** | Cheese / casein manufacture | **80–90 % of milk input** | **30,000–50,000** | 60,000–100,000 | Low | 5.5–6.5 |
| **Buttermilk** | Butter manufacture | Moderate | 5,000–8,000 | 8,000–15,000 | Low | 6–7 |
| **Condenser water from milk powder** | Evaporator condensates | High volume, low strength | 100–300 | 200–500 | <100 | 6–7 |

**Key inference for the student:** The **cheese whey stream** has BOD values 10–20× higher than the rest, but the **CIP effluent** dominates the total volume. Therefore, the ETP must be designed to handle both — a **high-volume, moderate-strength stream** (CIP) and a **low-volume, very high-strength stream** (whey). This is why whey is often segregated and treated separately in a high-rate anaerobic reactor.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

The typical ETP flow for dairy wastewater is:

**Raw Effluent → Screening → Grit Chamber → DAF (oil & grease removal) → Equalization Tank → Neutralization → UASB (Anaerobic) → Activated Sludge Aeration Tank → Secondary Clarifier → Chlorination → Treated Effluent Discharge.**

**Role of each unit:**

1. **Bar screens** — remove large floating debris (plastics, paper, leaves from milk tankers).
2. **Grit chamber** — settle out sand and heavy inert solids to protect pumps.
3. **DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation)** — removes oils, fats, and floating solids using fine air bubbles. Critical for dairy waste because milk fat creates scum and clogging downstream.
4. **Equalization tank** — dampens fluctuations in pH, flow, and load; HRT 8–12 hours.
5. **Neutralization** — adjusts pH to **6.5–7.5** before biological treatment using acid or CO₂.
6. **UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket)** — high-rate anaerobic reactor; HRT 2–5 days; achieves **70–85 % COD removal**; produces **biogas (60–70 % methane)**.
7. **Activated sludge** — aerobic polishing; removes residual BOD and ammonia; F/M ≈ 0.2–0.3 d⁻¹.
8. **Secondary clarifier** — separates biomass; clarified supernatant goes to disinfection.
9. **Chlorination** — destroys pathogens; contact time ≥ 30 min at ≥ 0.5 mg/L free chlorine.
10. **Sludge handling** — wasted sludge thickened, dewatered via centrifuge/belt press, disposed as biofertilizer.

**Justification for UASB as first biological step:**
- The **BOD/COD ratio of dairy waste is 0.4–0.6**, indicating high biodegradability → anaerobic digestion is highly effective.
- UASB is **energy-positive** because biogas can be used to heat the plant, offsetting aeration cost.
- UASB has **low sludge yield** compared to aerobic systems, reducing sludge disposal cost.
- It **handles high organic shocks** that would destabilize a purely aerobic plant.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is **cheese whey**? Discuss in detail the **various pathways for its utilization and management** including biogas production, whey powder, whey protein concentrate, animal feed, and bioethanol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy processes **80,000 L of milk/day** with wastewater generation of **2.5 L/L milk** and BOD₅ = **2,200 mg/L**. Calculate (i) the daily wastewater volume, (ii) total BOD load in kg/d, and (iii) the **population equivalent** using a per-capita BOD of **60 g/d**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Definition:** Cheese whey is the liquid by-product obtained after separation of casein curd during cheese manufacture, comprising 80–90 % of the original milk volume. It contains lactose (4.5–5 %), whey proteins (0.6–0.8 %), fat (0.3–0.5 %), and minerals (0.5–0.7 %) in 93 % water, with BOD₅ of 30,000–50,000 mg/L.

**Pathways for utilization and management:**

**Pathway 1 — Biogas Production via Anaerobic Digestion**
Whey is fed to a UASB or continuous stirred-tank reactor. With HRT 3–5 days and mesophilic conditions (35 °C), about **80 % COD removal** is achieved. Biogas yield is **0.30–0.40 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed**. The biogas can be used in a boiler or combined heat and power (CHP) unit, supplying 30–50 % of the plant's energy.

**Pathway 2 — Whey Powder**
Whey is concentrated by **reverse osmosis (RO)** or **evaporation** to 50–60 % total solids, then spray-dried. The product, **whey powder**, has 70–80 % lactose, 11–14 % protein, and a long shelf life. It is sold as a food ingredient in bakery, confectionery, infant formula, and animal feed industries.

**Pathway 3 — Whey Protein Concentrate (WPC)**
Whey undergoes **ultrafiltration (UF)** through membranes with cut-off 1–10 kDa. Proteins are retained; lactose and minerals pass through (permeate). WPC contains 35–80 % protein (WPC35, WPC80). WPC80 is a high-value sports-nutrition product.

**Pathway 4 — Liquid Whey as Animal Feed**
In rural areas, fresh liquid whey is fed directly to pigs and cattle as a nutrient-rich supplement, replacing part of the concentrate feed. This is the **lowest cost** option but requires proximity to farms and quick consumption to avoid spoilage.

**Pathway 5 — Bioethanol Production**
Lactose in whey is fermented by **Kluyveromyces fragilis** or **Lactobacillus** to produce bioethanol, which is then distilled. Yield: **0.20–0.25 kg ethanol per kg lactose**. This is an emerging value-addition route aligned with biofuel mandates.

**Pathway 6 — Lactose Recovery and Purification**
The UF permeate is further processed to crystallize pure lactose, used in pharmaceuticals, infant foods, and as a fermentation substrate for producing lactic acid, xanthan gum, etc.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Given data:** $V_{milk} = 80{,}000$ L/d; $f = 2.5$ L wastewater/L milk; $\text{BOD}_5 = 2{,}200$ mg/L; per-capita BOD = 60 g/person/d.

**(i) Daily wastewater volume:**

$$
Q = 80{,}000 \ \text{L/d} \times 2.5 = 200{,}000 \ \text{L/d} = 200 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d}
$$

**(ii) Total BOD load in kg/d:**

$$
L_{BOD} = 200{,}000 \ \text{L/d} \times 2{,}200 \ \text{mg/L} = 4.4 \times 10^{8} \ \text{mg/d}
$$

$$
L_{BOD} = 440 \ \text{kg BOD/d}
$$

**(iii) Population equivalent:**

$$
PE = \dfrac{440 \ \text{kg/d}}{60 \ \text{g/person/d}} = \dfrac{440{,}000 \ \text{g/d}}{60 \ \text{g/person/d}} \approx 7{,}333 \ \text{persons}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $Q = 200 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d}$, $L_{BOD} = 440 \ \text{kg BOD/d}$, $PE \approx 7{,}333$ persons.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in BOD load:** Students often write $L_{BOD} = Q \times \text{BOD}$ without converting mg to kg. Always divide by $10^6$ when going from mg to kg.
> 2. **Confusing BOD and COD:** BOD is biodegradable fraction only; COD includes both biodegradable and non-biodegradable. Dairy has BOD/COD ≈ 0.5 — never use them interchangeably in design problems.
> 3. **Ignoring segregated streams:** When the question mentions "cheese plant" or "butter plant," students must calculate **whey or buttermilk separately** from the general effluent. A single combined calculation will lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Per-capita BOD value:** Some textbooks use 54 g/d (BIS), some use 60 g/d (WHO/standard). Always **state the assumption** in your answer.
> 5. **Skipping the equalization step:** Marks are deducted if the ETP flow does not include equalization before biological treatment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Dairy & Milk Products Waste Management**

- ✅ **Milk composition:** Water 87 %, Lactose 4.8 %, Fat 3.5 %, Protein 3.2 %, Minerals 0.7 %.
- ✅ **Dairy wastewater is 1–3 L per litre of milk processed**; the BOD/COD ratio is **0.4–0.6** (highly biodegradable).
