<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) and Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD). State two differences between them.*

**Model Answer:**
BOD is the amount of dissolved oxygen consumed by microorganisms during biological oxidation of organic matter, measured over **5 days at 20 °C**, expressed in **mg/L**.

COD is the amount of oxygen equivalent consumed when organic matter is chemically oxidized by **potassium dichromate in 50% sulfuric acid under reflux for 2 hours**.

| Aspect | BOD | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidizing agent | Microorganisms | K₂Cr₂O₇ |
| Time required | 5 days | 2 – 3 hours |
| Measures | Biodegradable organics only | Biodegradable + non-biodegradable |
| Magnitude | Always < COD | Higher than BOD |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*State the significance of the BOD/COD ratio in food industry wastewater characterization.*

**Model Answer:**
The BOD/COD ratio indicates the **biodegradability** of the wastewater. A ratio **> 0.5** implies the effluent is readily biodegradable and suited for conventional aerobic biological treatment. A ratio **< 0.3** suggests the presence of toxic or recalcitrant organics, requiring advanced treatment such as ozonation or Fenton oxidation. For food industries: dairy whey ≈ 0.85 (very biodegradable), distillery spent wash ≈ 0.45 (moderately biodegradable), indicating different treatment strategies.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD). Derive the general expression for the ThOD of an organic compound with formula $C_aH_bO_cN_d$ assuming ammonia as the end-product of nitrogen oxidation. Calculate the ThOD of **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)** in g O₂/g. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy industry effluent has BOD₅ = 1200 mg/L at 20 °C. Assuming the first-order deoxygenation constant **k₁ = 0.30 day⁻¹**, compute the **ultimate BOD (L₀)**. If the same waste is incubated at 30 °C, what would be the equivalent BOD exerted in **2 days**? Use $\theta = 1.047$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition:** ThOD is the stoichiometric amount of oxygen required to completely oxidize a unit mass of a pure organic compound to its final oxidation products — CO₂, H₂O, and (for nitrogen) either NH₃ or NO₃⁻ — based on its chemical formula.

**Step 2 — Balanced equation (NH₃ end-product):**

$$C_aH_bO_cN_d + \left(\frac{4a + b - 2c - 3d}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow aCO_2 + \left(\frac{b - 3d}{2}\right)H_2O + dNH_3$$

**Step 3 — Convert to g O₂ per g compound:**

$$ThOD = \frac{\left(\frac{4a+b-2c-3d}{4}\right) \times 32}{12a + b + 16c + 14d} = \frac{32(4a + b - 2c - 3d)}{MW_{compound}}\,(g\,O_2/g)$$

**Step 4 — Calculate for acetic acid, CH₃COOH = C₂H₄O₂, MW = 60, a=2, b=4, c=2, d=0.**

$$ThOD = \frac{32(4 \times 2 + 4 - 2 \times 2 - 0)}{60}$$

$$= \frac{32(8 + 4 - 4)}{60} = \frac{32 \times 8}{60} = \frac{256}{60} = 4.267\,g\,O_2/mol$$

$$ThOD = \frac{4.267}{60} \times 1000 = 1.067\,g\,O_2/g\,acetic\,acid$$

**Final Answer (a):** **ThOD = 1.067 g O₂/g acetic acid**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute L₀ at 20 °C using BOD₅ = L₀(1 − e^(−k₁·5)):**

$$1200 = L_0 (1 - e^{-0.30 \times 5}) = L_0 (1 - e^{-1.5})$$

$$e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$$

$$L_0 = \frac{1200}{1 - 0.2231} = \frac{1200}{0.7769} = 1544.5\,mg/L$$

**Step 2 — Compute k₁ at 30 °C using van't Hoff correction:**

$$k_{1,30} = 0.30 \times 1.047^{10} = 0.30 \times 1.580 = 0.474\,day^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute BOD at 2 days, 30 °C:**

$$BOD_2 = L_0 (1 - e^{-0.474 \times 2}) = 1544.5 (1 - e^{-0.948})$$

$$e^{-0.948} = 0.3877$$

$$BOD_2 = 1544.5 (1 - 0.3877) = 1544.5 \times 0.6123 = 945.7\,mg/L$$

**Final Answer (b):** L₀ ≈ **1545 mg/L**, BOD₂ at 30 °C ≈ **946 mg/L**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Retest]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Total Oxygen Demand (TOD)** and its relationship with **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)**. Why does TOD generally exceed COD for nitrogenous food wastes? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The effluent from a fruit-canning plant shows the following characteristics: BOD₅ = 800 mg/L, COD = 1500 mg/L, TOC = 540 mg/L. Compute (i) BOD/COD ratio, (ii) COD/TOC ratio, (iii) predicted TOD using TOC, and (iv) comment on the biodegradability and choice of treatment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — TOD Definition:** TOD is measured by injecting a known micro-volume of sample into a carrier gas stream containing oxygen, combusting at **900 °C** over a platinum catalyst. The total oxygen consumed by all oxidizable components (organic carbon, ammonia, etc.) is measured electrochemically.

**Step 2 — TOD–TOC relationship:**

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{32}{12} = 2.67$$

$$TOD \approx 2.67 \times TOC$$

**Step 3 — Why TOD > COD for nitrogenous wastes:** COD (dichromate) generally does **not** oxidize ammonia to nitrate efficiently under standard conditions — the end product is typically NO₂⁻ or unoxidized NH₄⁺. TOD, however, oxidizes **all** nitrogen species fully (NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻ → N₂), consuming extra oxygen. Hence the additional oxygen demand from nitrogen is captured by TOD but not by COD, leading to **TOD > COD**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) BOD/COD ratio:**

$$\frac{BOD_5}{COD} = \frac{800}{1500} = 0.533$$

**(ii) COD/TOC ratio:**

$$\frac{COD}{TOC} = \frac{1500}{540} = 2.78$$

(Predicted theoretical = 2.67; higher value indicates some non-carbonaceous oxygen demand, possibly due to sulfites from preservation.)

**(iii) Predicted TOD:**

$$TOD \approx 2.67 \times 540 = 1441.8\,mg/L$$

(Note: TOD ≈ COD, suggesting low nitrogenous fraction — consistent with fruit waste.)

**(iv) Comment on biodegradability and treatment choice:**

- BOD/COD = **0.533** lies in the **0.5 – 0.7** range — moderately to readily biodegradable.
- Suitable for **conventional aerobic biological treatment** (e.g., activated sludge) followed by secondary clarification.
- Because the waste is biodegradable and low in nitrogen, **single-stage aerobic** treatment is recommended over anaerobic (which suits high-strength wastes).
- A polishing stage (sand filtration or constructed wetland) may be needed to meet discharge norms of BOD < 30 mg/L.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse ThOD with COD** — ThOD is theoretical; COD is experimental. They are equal only for pure, fully reactive compounds.
> 2. **Nitrogen end-product assumption matters** — KTU frequently tests both NH₃ and NO₃⁻ end-products. Ammonia → subtract **3d** in numerator; nitrate → subtract **5d**.
> 3. **Forgetting the temperature correction factor** in k₁ leads to 1–2 mark loss in 14-mark numericals.
> 4. **Always include units** (mg/L or g O₂/g) — missing units cause ½ to 1 mark deduction.
> 5. The empirical ratio **BOD₅/BOD_L = 0.68** assumes **k₁ = 0.23/day at 20 °C with no nitrification** — explicitly stating this condition is essential for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BOD₅** = oxygen used by microbes in **5 days at 20 °C**; first-order kinetics: $L_t = L_0(1 - e^{-k_1 t})$.
- **COD** uses **K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄** reflux for 2 h; measures **biodegradable + non-biodegradable** organics.
- **ThOD** is calculated from chemical formula; equals COD only for pure reactive compounds.
- **TOD** is the **combustion-furnace** total oxidant demand at 900 °C; **TOD ≈ 2.67 × TOC**.
- **BOD/COD ratio** is the master indicator of biodegradability: **> 0.5 readily biodegradable**; **< 0.3 toxic/recalcitrant**.
- **Temperature correction** for k₁: $k_{1,T} = k_{1,20} \times 1.047^{(T-20)}$.
- **Food-industry effluents** typically have **high k₁** (0.25 – 0.50) due to readily fermentable sugars/proteins.
- **Nitrogen end-product** affects ThOD calculation: subtract **3d** (ammonia) or **5d** (nitrate) per mole of N.
- **Dairy whey** has the highest BOD/COD (~0.85); **distillery spent wash** the lowest (~0.45) among food wastes.
- Always state **assumptions** (temperature, end-product, k₁ value) before computing in KTU numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Residues: Solids, Fats, Oils and Grease
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Residues: Solids, Fats, Oils and Grease

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Residues in Food Industry Waste Management

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Food Industry Waste Management**, the term **"Residues"** refers to the **by-products, spent raw materials, finished-product rejects, and process streams** that are generated during the receipt, processing, packaging, storage, and distribution of food materials. These residues are broadly classified by their physical state and chemical nature into three primary engineering categories relevant to waste-handling design:

1. **Solid Residues (S)** – Non-fluid, separable matter including trimmings, peels, seeds, bones, husk, sludge, spent grain, pulp, and dewatered biomass.
2. **Fats, Oils and Grease (FOG)** – Hydrophobic, organic, low-density liquids extracted or liberated during cooking, rendering, dairy processing, and oil-refining operations.
3. **Dissolved and Suspended Solids in Liquid Effluents** – Colloidal and particulate organic load carried in wastewater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *Residues are the organic and inorganic matter remaining after the recovery of the principal product from a food raw material, and they constitute the principal substrate for waste-handling, pollution-control, and by-product-recovery engineering in any food processing plant.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **coconut being processed in a Kerala copra unit**. The white kernel is the product. Everything else — the fibrous husk, the brown testa, the shell, and the thin "fry oil" that drips out of the expeller — is **residue**.

| Layer of the Coconut | What it becomes | Residue Category |
|---|---|---|
| Outer green-yellow husk | Coir fibre / fuel | **Solid Residue** |
| Hard brown shell | Charcoal / fuel | **Solid Residue** |
| Milky water inside | Fermented beverage | **Liquid Effluent (with dissolved solids)** |
| Oil pressed from kernel | Cooking oil (product) | — |
| Residual oil cake | Cattle feed | **Solid Residue** |
| Spent oil from fryers / clarifier | Brown grease | **FOG** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor for Students:** If you can **see it with your eyes as a separate phase**, it is a residue. The engineering task is to decide whether to **dispose, treat, or valorise** it.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

The following engineering reference values must be memorised for any KTU calculation:

- **Density of Fats and Oils:** **ρ ≈ 0.91 – 0.93 g/cm³** at 15 °C (lighter than water).
- **Density of Water (reference):** **ρₕ₂ₒ = 1.00 g/cm³**.
- **Flash Point of Food-Grade Oil:** **≥ 250 °C** (used as a safety benchmark).
- **Standard Total Solids Drying Temperature:** **103 – 105 °C** (APHA Standard Methods, 2540 B).
- **Standard Volatile Solids Ignition Temperature:** **550 ± 50 °C** (APHA Standard Methods, 2540 E).
- **Standard Solids Measurement Time:** **1 hour** at the respective temperature.
- **BOD₅/COD Ratio for Food Waste:** typically **0.4 – 0.8** (highly biodegradable).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solid Residues in the Food Industry

Solid residues are categorised by **origin, biodegradability, and moisture content**. The board examiner expects you to be fluent in all three axes.

### A. By Origin of Process Stream
- **Pre-processing residues** – Trimmings, peels, stones, damaged raw material (e.g., fruit peel from juice units).
- **Processing residues** – Press cake, spent slurry, exhausted catalyst/filter aid.
- **Post-processing residues** – Spent packaging, expired product, wash-down solids.

### B. By Biodegradability
- **Putrescible (organic) solids** – Vegetable trimmings, blood, offal. High **Volatile Solids (VS)** content, **VS/TS > 0.7**.
- **Non-putrescible (inorganic) solids** – Glass cullet, metal scraps, salt crystals.

### C. By Moisture Content (Engineering Basis)
| Class | Moisture % (wb) | Handling System |
|---|---|---|
| Dry solids | < 15 % | Conveyors, bulk storage |
| Moist solids | 15 – 50 % | Screw conveyors, covered bins |
| Wet solids | 50 – 85 % | Pumping, dewatering needed |
| Liquid sludge | > 85 % | Centrifuge / filter press |

## 2.2 The Three-Phase Theory of Fats, Oils and Grease (FOG)

FOG exists in any food-processing wastewater as a **three-phase colloidal system**:

1. **Free Oil** — Droplets > 150 μm, rise to surface by gravity within minutes (Stokes regime).
2. **Emulsified Oil** — Droplets 20 – 150 μm, stabilised by surfactants and proteins; rise slowly.
3. **Dissolved/Emulsified Oil** — Micelles < 20 μm; require chemical or biological breakup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Concept:** Only **free oil** can be removed by a simple grease trap. **Emulsified and dissolved oil** require API separators, dissolved air flotation (DAF), or biological treatment.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Treatment Step — Engineering Logic Map

| Step | Why this step exists | KTU buzzword |
|---|---|---|
| Screening | Protect downstream pumps | "Headworks protection" |
| Grit removal | Prevent abrasion | "Velocity-controlled settling" |
| Flow equalisation | Buffer shock loads (esp. from dairy / slaughter) | "Equalisation tank" |
| Grease trap / API | Remove free FOG | "Gravity separation" |
| DAF | Remove emulsified FOG + TSS | "Air-flotation clarifier" |
| Biological (ASP / SBR) | Oxidise dissolved organics | "BOD/COD reduction" |
| Sludge handling | Dewater solid residues | "Cake disposal" |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory reference table for Module 3. Memorise the units — board examiners routinely deduct marks for missing units.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Characterise raw residue |
| 2 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Drier design |
| 3 | Total Solids | $TS = TDS + TSS$ | mg/L | Effluent characterisation |
| 4 | Total Suspended Solids | $TSS = \dfrac{(W_f - W_e) \times 1000}{V}$ | mg/L | Settling design |
| 5 | Volatile Solids | $VS = \dfrac{W_d - W_a}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Biogas yield potential |
| 6 | Fixed Solids | $FS = 100 - VS$ | % | Inorganic fraction |
| 7 | FOG by Hexane Extraction | $FOG = \dfrac{W_{re} - W_{fe}}{V}$ | mg/L | Standard Methods 5520 B |
| 8 | FOG Removal Efficiency | $\eta_{FOG} = \dfrac{FOG_{in} - FOG_{out}}{FOG_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Equipment rating |
| 9 | Stokes Rising Velocity | $v_r = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | API separator design |
| 10 | Specific Gravity of FOG | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$ | — | Determines floatability |
| 11 | Biogas Yield from VS | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot VS \cdot 0.5$ | m³ CH₄/kg VS | Anaerobic digester sizing |
| 12 | Oil-Water Surface Loading Rate | $SLR = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m³/m²·h | API design check |
| 13 | BOD/COD Ratio | $R_{bc} = \dfrac{BOD_5}{COD}$ | — | Biodegradability indicator |

> *Where: W_w = wet weight, W_d = dry weight, W_f = filter + residue, W_e = empty filter, W_a = ash weight, W_{re} = residue + flask, W_{fe} = empty flask, V = sample volume, g = 9.81 m/s², d = droplet diameter, μ = dynamic viscosity.*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solid residues** feed into **biogas digesters**, **composting yards**, **animal-feed pelletisers**, and **rendering plants** — converting a disposal liability into a revenue line.
- **FOG**, if allowed into municipal sewers, causes **"fat-bergs"** that block pumping mains — a public-health and engineering crisis documented globally.
- Captured FOG is a feedstock for **biodiesel (trans-esterification)** and **oleochemicals** — direct industry relevance to **circular-economy** objectives of the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Moisture-Content Relationship (Wet vs Dry Basis)

### Step 1 — Define the Weights
Let $W_w$ be the wet weight of the food residue sample as received. After oven drying at **103 – 105 °C** for **1 hour** until constant weight, the dry weight is $W_d$.

The water mass held in the sample is:
$$W_{water} = W_w - W_d$$

### Step 2 — Define Moisture on a Wet Basis (Engineering Standard)
By definition, wet-basis moisture is the ratio of water mass to the original wet mass:
$$M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_{water}}{W_w} \times 100$$

Substituting $W_{water}$:
$$M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

### Step 3 — Define Moisture on a Dry Basis (Drier Design Standard)
For drying-equipment design, the denominator is the dry mass because the engineer is interested in how much water must be removed per kg of bone-dry solid:
$$M_{db} = \dfrac{W_{water}}{W_d} \times 100$$

### Step 4 — Convert Between the Two Bases
Start with the relationship:
$$M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{1 + \dfrac{M_{db}}{100}}$$

**Algebraic Proof:**
$$M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $W_w$:
$$M_{db} = \dfrac{\dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}}{\dfrac{W_d}{W_w}} = \dfrac{1 - \dfrac{W_d}{W_w}}{\dfrac{W_d}{W_w}}$$

But $\dfrac{W_d}{W_w} = 1 - \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}$. Substituting and simplifying:
$$M_{db} = \dfrac{\dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}}{1 - \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}}$$

Inverting:
$$\boxed{M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{1 + \dfrac{M_{db}}{100}} \times 100}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Solids Characterisation (Board Pattern)

> **KTU University Exam Style Problem (10-Mark Standard):**
> *A dairy effluent sample of 50 mL is filtered through a pre-weighed Gooch crucible (mass = 24.6320 g). After drying at 105 °C, the crucible + residue weighs 24.6895 g. After ignition at 550 °C, the final mass is 24.6510 g. Calculate TSS, VS, FS, and comment on biodegradability.*

### Solution Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Calculate TSS**
$$W_f - W_e = 24.6895 - 24.6320 = 0.0575 \text{ g}$$
$$TSS = \dfrac{0.0575 \times 1000}{50} = \boxed{1150 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate VS (loss on ignition)**
$$VS = \dfrac{W_d - W_a}{W_d} = \dfrac{0.0575 - (24.6510 - 24.6320)}{0.0575} = \dfrac{0.0575 - 0.0190}{0.0575}$$
$$VS = \dfrac{0.0385}{0.0575} \times 100 = \boxed{66.96 \%}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate FS**
$$FS = 100 - 66.96 = \boxed{33.04 \%}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Comment**
Since $VS/TS > 0.65$, the solids are **highly putrescible** and suitable for **anaerobic digestion** with a high methane yield.

## 3.3 Derivation of FOG Quantification (Partition-Gravimetric Method)

### Step 1 — Sample Acidification
The sample is acidified to pH < 2 with HCl to convert soap-FOG to free FOG:
$$\text{R-COONa} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{R-COOH} + \text{NaCl}$$

### Step 2 — Liquid-Liquid Extraction
FOG is extracted into **n-hexane** in a separatory funnel. The density of hexane (0.659 g/cm³) ensures clean separation from water.

### Step 3 — Evaporation and Gravimetry
Hexane is evaporated; the residue mass is measured.

$$FOG \text{ (mg/L)} = \dfrac{(W_{re} - W_{fe}) \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$$

## 3.4 Stokes' Law Derivation for FOG Rise Velocity (API Separator Design)

Starting from the balance of forces on a spherical oil droplet rising in quiescent water:

$$F_b - F_g - F_d = 0$$

$$\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_w g - \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_o g - 3 \pi \mu d v_r = 0$$

Solving for $v_r$:
$$v_r = \dfrac{g (\rho_w - \rho_o) d^2}{18 \mu}$$

### Worked Example
- $d = 150 \times 10^{-6}$ m (150 μm, boundary free/emulsified)
- $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_o = 920$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (water at 20 °C)

$$v_r = \dfrac{9.81 \times (1000 - 920) \times (150 \times 10^{-6})^2}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_r = \dfrac{9.81 \times 80 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8}}{1.8036 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v_r = \dfrac{1.766 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8036 \times 10^{-2}} = 9.79 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_r \approx 3.52 \text{ m/h}$$

This low velocity is why **API separators need long retention times (≥ 20 min)** and large surface areas — a frequent KTU question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quantitative Decision Table for Food-Industry Residues

| Industry | Dominant Solid Residue | Dominant FOG Source | VS/TS | Recommended Valorisation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dairy | Whey, sludge | Cream, butter serum | 0.85 | Anaerobic digester |
| Slaughterhouse | Blood, bones, paunch | Tallow from rendering | 0.80 | Rendering / biogas |
| Edible oil refinery | Gum, spent earth | Spent process oil | 0.90 | Biodiesel, soap |
| Bakery | Stale bread, dough | Spent frying oil | 0.95 | Animal feed, compost |
| Fruit processing | Peel, pomace, seeds | Wax coating wash | 0.92 | Pectin extraction |
| Brewery | Spent grain, yeast | — | 0.88 | Cattle feed, biogas |
| Seafood | Shells, scales | Fish oil | 0.75 | Chitin extraction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Solid and FOG Residue Treatment (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    F1 --> F2[API Separator]
    F2 --> F3[DAF Unit]
    F3 --> F4[Biodiesel Trans Esterification]
    D3 --> L1[Equalisation Tank]
    L1 --> L2[Primary Clarifier]
    L2 --> L3[Biological Reactor]
    L3 --> L4[Secondary Clarifier]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — FOG Phase-Separation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Inlet Wastewater] --> P2{pH Check}
    P2 -->|pH greater than 2| P3[Acid Dosing Stage]
    P2 -->|pH less or equal 2| P4[Coarse Screen]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Free Oil Zone]
    P5 --> P6[Emulsified Oil Zone]
    P6 --> P7[Dissolved Micelle Zone]
    P5 -->|Skimmed| P8[FOG Holding Tank]
    P6 -->|DAF Float| P8
    P7 -->|Biological Breakup| P9[Effluent Polishing]
    P8 --> P10[Biodiesel Reactor]

```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Solid-Residue Mass-Flow Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Food Industry Solid Residue] --> M2{Moisture Content}

    M2 -->|50 to 85 percent| M5[Centrifuge or Belt Press]

    M4 --> M7
    M5 --> M7
    M6 --> M7
    M7 -->|High VS and Putrescible| M8[Anaerobic Digester]
    M7 -->|Low VS and Stable| M9[Composting Yard]
    M7 -->|High Protein and Safe| M10[Animal Feed Pelletiser]
    M7 -->|Tainted or Hazardous| M11[Secure Landfill or Incinerator]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Residues" in the context of food industry waste management. Classify them into three broad categories with one example each.

**Model Answer (KTU Board Key):**
*Residues are the unwanted by-products or spent matter generated during food processing operations, classified as:*
1. *Solid residues (e.g., fruit peel from juice processing).*
2. *Fats, Oils and Grease (e.g., spent cooking oil from fryers).*
3. *Dissolved and suspended solids in liquid effluent (e.g., milk solids in dairy wash water).*

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for classification with examples.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Free Oil, Emulsified Oil and Dissolved Oil in food industry wastewater. Why is the distinction important for treatment selection?

**Model Answer:**
- *Free oil — droplet size > 150 μm; rises naturally in gravity separators.*
- *Emulsified oil — 20–150 μm; stabilised by surfactants/proteins; needs DAF.*
- *Dissolved/micellar oil — < 20 μm; needs biological or chemical treatment.*

> *Distinction is critical because each fraction requires a different unit operation for effective removal and regulatory compliance.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **classification of solid residues** in the food industry based on **biodegradability** and **moisture content**. With a labelled diagram, describe the **process flow of solid-residue handling** in a typical food processing plant.

**Model Answer:**

**Classification:**
- *By biodegradability — Putrescible (organic, high VS) vs Non-putrescible (inorganic).*
- *By moisture — Dry (< 15 %), Moist (15–50 %), Wet (50–85 %), Sludge (> 85 %).*

**Process Flow Description (refer to diagram in Section 4.3):**
1. *Source segregation at the process floor.*
2. *Size reduction using grinders/shredders.*
3. *Conveying to dewatering unit (screw press / centrifuge).*
4. *Moisture-adjusted transfer to valorisation (compost, biogas, feed).*
5. *Reject stream routed to secured disposal.*

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A meat-processing unit generates **2.5 tonnes/day** of solid residue with **moisture content of 78 % (wet basis)**. The residue is to be dewatered to **60 % moisture (wb)** using a screw press. Calculate:
- (i) The mass of water to be removed per day.
- (ii) The dry mass of the residue.
- (iii) The volume of press-cake produced per day, assuming cake density **1200 kg/m³**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**(i) Water to be removed:**
Initial water mass:
$$W_{w,initial} = 0.78 \times 2500 = 1950 \text{ kg/day}$$

Dry mass (constant throughout):
$$W_d = 2500 - 1950 = 550 \text{ kg/day}$$

Final moisture = 60 % wb means final wet mass = 40 % dry mass = 550 / 0.40 = 1375 kg/day. Final water mass:
$$W_{w,final} = 1375 - 550 = 825 \text{ kg/day}$$

Water removed:
$$\Delta W = 1950 - 825 = \boxed{1125 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**(ii) Dry mass:** already calculated = **550 kg/day**

**(iii) Volume of press-cake:**
$$V = \dfrac{1375}{1200} = \boxed{1.146 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Discuss the **sources and characteristics of Fats, Oils and Grease (FOG)** in food industry wastewater. Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of an **API oil-water separator** with the design equation.

**Model Answer Outline:**
- *Sources — dairy, slaughterhouse, edible oil refining, bakery, snack-food frying lines.*
- *Characteristics — density 0.91–0.93 g/cm³; immiscible; high COD contribution (1 g oil ≈ 2.9 g COD theoretical).*
- *API separator — rectangular tank with inclined corrugated plates; FOG rises to surface and is skimmed; clarified water exits at bottom.*
- *Design Equation — Stokes' rising velocity $v_r = \dfrac{g (\rho_w - \rho_o) d^2}{18 \mu}$; Surface loading rate $SLR = Q/A$.*

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* The wastewater from a confectionery unit has a flow of **200 m³/day** and an inlet FOG concentration of **1200 mg/L**. The outlet FOG is required to be **≤ 50 mg/L**. Calculate:
- (i) The FOG removal efficiency.
- (ii) The mass of FOG captured per day.
- (iii) The theoretical volume of biodiesel (density 880 kg/m³) recoverable assuming 95 % trans-esterification efficiency and 1 kg FOG → 1 L biodiesel stoichiometric.

**Solution:**

**(i) Removal efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{1200 - 50}{1200} \times 100 = \boxed{95.83 \%}$$

**(ii) Mass of FOG captured:**
$$\dot{m}_{FOG} = (1200 - 50) \times 200 = 1150 \times 200 = 230{,}000 \text{ g/day}$$
$$\dot{m}_{FOG} = \boxed{230 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**(iii) Biodiesel volume:**
Mass of biodiesel theoretical = $230 \times 0.95 = 218.5$ kg/day.
Volume:
$$V_{bio} = \dfrac{218.5}{880} = \boxed{0.2483 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 248 \text{ L/day}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. *Omitting units* in the final answer → **−1 to −2 marks** per sub-part.
> 2. *Confusing wet-basis vs dry-basis moisture* in dewatering problems → **−2 marks**.
> 3. *Using 1000 mg/g incorrectly* in FOG mass-balance → **−1 mark**.
> 4. *Forgetting the stoichiometric 1 kg FOG ≈ 1 L biodiesel assumption stated in the question* → **−1 mark**.
> 5. *Drawing the API separator without the inclined plate pack* → **−2 marks** in 7-mark descriptive parts.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Residues** = solids + FOG + dissolved solids in effluent.
- **Moisture content wb** vs **db** — always specify the basis.
- **TS = TDS + TSS**; **VS** by loss on ignition at **550 °C**.
- **Free FOG** removed by **gravity**; **emulsified FOG** needs **DAF**; **dissolved FOG** needs **biological** treatment.
- **FOG density** is **less than water** → it floats, enabling API separator use.
- **Stokes' Law** governs droplet rise velocity; design $v_r$ and **SLR** define separator area.
- **VS/TS > 0.65** → high biodegradability → anaerobic digestion favoured.
- **Valorisation routes**: biogas, compost, animal feed, biodiesel, oleochemicals.
- **Always quote units**; **always state the basis** (wb or db); **always reference APHA Standard Methods** for analytical steps.
- **KTU buzz-phrases to include in answers**: "headworks protection," "three-phase separation," "circular economy," "valorisation," "resource recovery."
- **Numerical must-know**: $1 \text{ g oil} \approx 2.9 \text{ g COD}$; $1 \text{ kg FOG} \approx 1 \text{ L biodiesel}$ (stoichiometric).
- **Equipment sequence for FOG-laden wastewater**: Screen → Grit → Equalisation → Grease trap → API → DAF → Biological → Polishing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forms of Nitrogen, Sulphur and Phosphorus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forms of Nitrogen, Sulphur and Phosphorus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Nutrient Forms in Food Industry Wastewater

In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management (OEFTT834)**, the characterization of wastewater is incomplete without a detailed analysis of the macro-nutrients—**Nitrogen (N), Sulphur (S), and Phosphorus (P)**. These elements enter the waste stream primarily through raw food materials (proteins, lipids, carbohydrates, and salts) and processing chemicals. Their "forms" refer to the different oxidation states and chemical speciation in which they exist, each exhibiting distinct reactivity, toxicity, and treatment behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Understanding the various forms of N, S, and P is the foundation for designing **biological treatment units (activated sludge, anaerobic digesters)**, **nutrient recovery systems**, and **discharge compliance assessments** under the Pollution Control Board norms.

---

## 1.2 Forms of Nitrogen — Technical Definition

Nitrogen in food industry wastewater exists in **six principal forms**, often quantified together as **Total Nitrogen (TN)**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Nitrogen (TN) Equation:**
> $$\text{TN} = \text{Organic N} + \text{NH}_3\text{/NH}_4^+ + \text{NO}_2^- + \text{NO}_3^-$$

| Form | Chemical Symbol | Oxidation State | Typical Source in Food Waste |
|------|----------------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Organic Nitrogen | R-NH₂ | -3 | Proteins, amino acids, urea |
| Ammonia (un-ionized) | NH₃ | -3 | Breakdown of proteins, urea hydrolysis |
| Ammonium (ionized) | NH₄⁺ | -3 | Equilibrium with NH₃ |
| Nitrite | NO₂⁻ | +3 | Partial nitrification |
| Nitrate | NO₃⁻ | +5 | Complete nitrification |
| Nitrogen Gas | N₂ | 0 | Denitrification end-product |

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of nitrogen forms as a "ladder of oxidation." Food waste sits at the bottom of the ladder (organic N, like a tightly wound ball of yarn — proteins). Microbes act as the "climber" that gradually unrolls the yarn, lifting nitrogen rung by rung: organic N → ammonia → nitrite → nitrate → nitrogen gas (which escapes to the atmosphere). The position on this ladder dictates the treatment strategy you must adopt.

---

## 1.3 Forms of Sulphur — Technical Definition

Sulphur in food industry effluents originates from **sulphur-containing amino acids (cysteine, methionine)**, **sulphiting agents (SO₂)** used in preservation, and **process water salts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Sulphur (TS) = Organic S + H₂S/S²⁻ + SO₃²⁻ + SO₄²⁻ + S⁰ (elemental)**

| Form | Chemical Symbol | Oxidation State | Significance |
|------|----------------|-----------------|--------------|
| Organic Sulphur | R-SH, R-S-R | -2 | Cysteine, methionine in proteins |
| Hydrogen Sulphide | H₂S | -2 | Anaerobic decomposition product (toxic, odorous) |
| Sulphide ion | S²⁻ | -2 | Anaerobic digesters, corrodes pipes |
| Sulphite | SO₃²⁻ | +4 | Used as preservative in fruits/wines |
| Sulphate | SO₄²⁻ | +6 | Stable oxidized form; common in dairy effluent |
| Elemental Sulphur | S⁰ | 0 | Thiobacillus oxidation product |

**Conceptual Analogy:** Sulphur's journey is like a "smelly-to-stable" transformation. Imagine a rotten egg (H₂S — extremely foul-smelling) sitting in a wastewater tank. When oxygen is supplied (aerobic conditions), the egg-smell disappears because microbes oxidize S²⁻ progressively to SO₄²⁻, which is odourless, non-toxic, and stable. The "egg" is the same sulphur, but its *form* has changed.

---

## 1.4 Forms of Phosphorus — Technical Definition

Phosphorus does **not** undergo redox changes like nitrogen or sulphur; instead, it transitions between **dissolved, particulate, organic, and poly-condensed (polyphosphate) forms**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Phosphorus (TP) Equation:**
> $$\text{TP} = \text{Orthophosphate} + \text{Polyphosphate} + \text{Organic P}$$

| Form | Chemical Symbol | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| Orthophosphate | PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, H₂PO₄⁻ | Directly bioavailable form |
| Polyphosphate | P₂O₇⁴⁻, P₃O₁₀⁵⁻ | Condensed forms in cleaning agents |
| Organic Phosphorus | DNA, ATP, phospholipids | Bound in cellular structures |
| Particulate P | — | Adsorbed onto suspended solids |

**Conceptual Analogy:** Phosphorus is like "money in different wallets." A student might keep cash in their pocket (orthophosphate — immediately usable by microbes), savings in a bank (polyphosphate — needs hydrolysis to release), and investments in stocks (organic P — locked in biological molecules, must be mineralized first). The wastewater treatment plant must "empty all wallets" (convert all to orthophosphate) before chemical precipitation can remove phosphorus effectively.

---

## 1.5 Engineering Significance of Knowing the Forms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does form matter in food industry waste management?**
> 1. **Toxicity Assessment:** NH₃ (un-ionized) is highly toxic to aquatic life at concentrations as low as **0.025 mg/L**, while NH₄⁺ is far less toxic.
> 2. **Treatment Selection:** Nitrification requires NH₃/NH₄⁺; denitrification requires NO₃⁻. You cannot denitrify what is not yet nitrified.
> 3. **Oxygen Demand:** Oxidizing NH₃ to NO₃⁻ consumes **4.57 mg O₂ per mg NH₃-N**.
> 4. **Corrosion Control:** H₂S concentrations >**0.5 mg/L** cause severe pipe corrosion in sewer networks.
> 5. **Eutrophication Prevention:** Even **0.01–0.02 mg/L** of P can trigger algal blooms in receiving water bodies.
> 6. **Nutrient Recovery:** Knowing speciation enables struvite (MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O) precipitation for fertilizer recovery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Speciation of Nitrogen

### 2.1.1 Organic Nitrogen (Org-N)
This is the **default form** in which nitrogen enters food industry waste — as proteins, peptides, amino acids, urea, and nucleic acids. It is **not directly bioavailable**; it must first undergo **ammonification**:

$$\text{Organic N} \xrightarrow{\text{Hydrolysis (aerobic/anaerobic)}} \text{NH}_3/\text{NH}_4^+$$

**Key Insight:** In dairy and meat processing wastewaters, organic N can constitute **60–80% of the Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN)**, making it the dominant fraction.

### 2.1.2 The Ammonia–Ammonium Equilibrium
This is the most critical equilibrium in food wastewater treatment:

$$\text{NH}_4^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+$$

The distribution is governed by **temperature (T)** and **pH**:

$$\text{Fraction as NH}_3 = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}}$$

> Where **pKa ≈ 0.09018 + 2729.92/T (K)** — an empirical constant that decreases with increasing temperature.

**Why this matters:** At pH **7.0 and 25 °C**, only ~**0.6%** of total ammoniacal nitrogen is in the toxic NH₃ form. But at pH **9.5 and 35 °C** (typical of anaerobic digesters), the NH₃ fraction rises to **~28%** — a critical operational concern.

### 2.1.3 Nitrification (Two-Step Oxidation)
Nitrification is performed by two distinct genera of chemoautotrophic bacteria:

$$\text{Step 1: } 2\text{NH}_4^+ + 3\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} 2\text{NO}_2^- + 4\text{H}^+ + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Step 2: } 2\text{NO}_2^- + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrobacter}} 2\text{NO}_3^-$$

**Net Reaction:**

$$\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**KTU High-Yield Note:** The stoichiometric oxygen demand is **4.57 g O₂ per g NH₃-N oxidized**, and **7.14 g alkalinity (as CaCO₃) is consumed per g NH₃-N**.

### 2.1.4 Denitrification
Under anoxic conditions, denitrifying bacteria use NO₃⁻ as a terminal electron acceptor:

$$\text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow$$

**Practical Carbon Requirement:** **2.86 g COD consumed per g NO₃⁻-N reduced** to N₂ gas.

### 2.1.5 Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN)
This is the **standard analytical parameter** in KTU laboratory and board questions:

$$\text{TKN} = \text{Organic N} + \text{NH}_3\text{-N} + \text{NH}_4^+\text{-N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** TKN does **NOT** include NO₂⁻ and NO₃⁻ forms, because the Kjeldahl digestion step converts only organically bound N and ammoniacal N to ammonium sulphate. Therefore, **TN = TKN + (NO₂⁻-N + NO₃⁻-N)**.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Speciation of Sulphur

### 2.2.1 The Sulphur Cycle in Food Waste
Sulphur transformations follow a redox sequence:

$$\text{Organic S (R-SH)} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S}/\text{S}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{S}^0 \rightarrow \text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{SO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{SO}_4^{2-}$$

### 2.2.2 Hydrogen Sulphide Production
Under anaerobic conditions, **sulphate-reducing bacteria (SRB)** like *Desulfovibrio* reduce SO₄²⁻:

$$\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2\text{CH}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{SRB}} \text{H}_2\text{S} + 2\text{HCO}_3^-$$

**Engineering Concern:** H₂S at concentrations of **>100 ppm** is lethal to humans; at **>0.5 mg/L** in liquid phase, it causes microbial inhibition in anaerobic digesters.

### 2.2.3 Sulphide Speciation (Similar to Ammonia)
H₂S exists in equilibrium with HS⁻ and S²⁻:

$$\text{H}_2\text{S} \rightleftharpoons \text{HS}^- + \text{H}^+ \quad (pK_{a1} = 7.04)$$

$$\text{HS}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{S}^{2-} + \text{H}^+ \quad (pK_{a2} = 11.96)$$

At neutral pH (7.0), the dominant form is **HS⁻ (~50%)** with equal H₂S and S²⁻ traces.

### 2.2.4 Sulphate in Food Industry Effluents
Dairy, brewery, and seafood processing wastewaters often contain **200–1000 mg/L SO₄²⁻** due to:
- Cleaning-in-place (CIP) chemicals (H₂SO₄-based)
- Preservation brines
- Naturally occurring sulphur amino acids

> [!WARNING]
> **High SO₄²⁻ in Anaerobic Digesters:** SRB outcompete methanogens for acetate and H₂ when SO₄²⁻/COD ratio >**0.67**, leading to process failure.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Speciation of Phosphorus

### 2.3.1 Orthophosphate Equilibrium
Phosphoric acid dissociates in three steps:

$$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- \rightleftharpoons \text{HPO}_4^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \text{PO}_4^{3-}$$

$$pK_{a1} = 2.15, \quad pK_{a2} = 7.20, \quad pK_{a3} = 12.35$$

**At typical wastewater pH (6.5–7.5):** The dominant species are **H₂PO₄⁻ and HPO₄²⁻** (a roughly 1:1 mixture at pH 7.2).

### 2.3.2 Polyphosphates
These are **linear chains** of PO₄ tetrahedra, formed by condensation:

$$n\text{HPO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow (\text{PO}_3)_n^{n-} + (n-1)\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Example:** Sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP, Na₅P₃O₁₀) — common in **detergents and processed food** as a water-softening agent.

Polyphosphates undergo **hydrolysis** to orthophosphate:

$$\text{P}_3\text{O}_{10}^{5-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HPO}_4^{2-} + \text{P}_2\text{O}_7^{4-} \rightarrow 3\text{PO}_4^{3-}$$

### 2.3.3 Organic Phosphorus
Found in:
- **Phospholipids** (cell membranes of food materials)
- **Nucleic acids** (DNA, RNA)
- **ATP** (energy molecules in biological systems)
- **Phytic acid** (in cereals and legumes — antinutrient)

Mineralization releases orthophosphate through enzymatic hydrolysis by **phosphatase enzymes**.

### 2.3.4 Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)
Polyphosphate-accumulating organisms (PAOs) take up orthophosphate in excess under alternating anaerobic-aerobic conditions:

$$\text{Poly-P}_{\text{intracellular}} + \text{Acetate} \rightarrow \text{PHA}_{\text{stored}} + \text{PO}_4^{3-}\text{(released)}$$

$$\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{Poly-P}_{\text{intracellular}} \text{ (luxury uptake)}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Total Nitrogen | TN = TKN + NO₂⁻-N + NO₃⁻-N | mg N/L | Complete N balance |
| 2 | Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen | TKN = Org-N + NH₃-N + NH₄⁺-N | mg N/L | Digestion-based analysis |
| 3 | NH₃ fraction | $\frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}}$ | dimensionless | Toxicity assessment |
| 4 | Oxygen demand (nitrification) | $4.57 \cdot [\text{NH}_3\text{-N}]$ | mg O₂/L | Aeration tank design |
| 5 | Alkalinity consumed | $7.14 \cdot [\text{NH}_3\text{-N}]$ | mg CaCO₃/L | Buffering capacity |
| 6 | COD for denitrification | $2.86 \cdot [\text{NO}_3^-\text{-N}]$ | mg COD/L | External C-source dosing |
| 7 | Struvite precipitation | Mg²⁺ + NH₄⁺ + PO₄³⁻ + 6H₂O → MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O | — | Nutrient recovery |
| 8 | P removal by alum | $\text{Al}^{3+} : \text{PO}_4^{3-} = 1:1$ (molar) | mol | Chemical coagulation |
| 9 | H₂S equilibrium (pKa₁) | 7.04 at 25 °C | — | Odor control design |
| 10 | SO₄²⁻ inhibition ratio | SO₄²⁻/COD < 0.67 | dimensionless | Anaerobic digester safety |
| 11 | SRB stoichiometry | 1 g SO₄²⁻ → 0.33 g H₂S | g | Biogas H₂S estimation |
| 12 | P precipitation (FeCl₃) | Fe³⁺ + PO₄³⁻ → FePO₄↓ | — | Tertiary treatment |
| 13 | C:N:P for biological growth | 100 : 5 : 1 (typical) | mass ratio | Nutrient balance |
| 14 | Biomass yield on N | $Y_N = 0.12$ g biomass/g NH₃-N | g/g | Sludge production |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula sheet is sufficient to solve **90% of numerical problems** asked in KTU board examinations on food wastewater characterization. Memorize entries 1, 3, 4, 6, and 7 as the "Big Five."

---

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Applications

| Industry | Dominant Form | Specific Concern |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Dairy** | High Org-N, high PO₄³⁻ (from milk proteins, cleaning agents) | Struvite scaling in pipes |
| **Meat & Poultry** | High TKN, blood-rich (proteins) | High NH₃ toxicity post-hydrolysis |
| **Brewery** | High SO₄²⁻ (from yeast nutrients, CIP chemicals) | H₂S in anaerobic digesters |
| **Seafood** | High organic S (fish proteins), high salinity | Odour and corrosion |
| **Fruit & Vegetable** | High NO₃⁻ (from soil residues, fertilizer carryover) | Eutrophication of receiving water |
| **Sugar Industry** | Moderate Org-N, high SO₃²⁻ (from sulphitation in sugar refining) | SO₂ emission, colour removal |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ammonia–Ammonium Equilibrium Constant as a Function of Temperature

**Starting Point:** The equilibrium between NH₄⁺ and NH₃ in aqueous solution is governed by an acid-base reaction:

$$\text{NH}_4^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+$$

The **thermodynamic equilibrium constant** at standard conditions is $K_a$:

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{NH}_3][\text{H}^+]}{[\text{NH}_4^+]}$$

By definition, $pK_a = -\log_{10}(K_a)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the van't Hoff Equation:**
The van't Hoff equation relates the equilibrium constant to temperature:

$$\frac{d(\ln K_a)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{RT^2}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Assuming Constant Enthalpy:**
Integrating between reference temperature $T_0 = 298.15$ K (25 °C) and any temperature $T$:

$$\ln K_a(T) - \ln K_a(T_0) = \frac{\Delta H^0}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Known Values:**
At $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $pK_a(T_0) = 9.25$ and $\Delta H^0 = 52.21$ kJ/mol for the NH₄⁺ ionization.

$$pK_a(T) = pK_a(T_0) - \frac{\Delta H^0}{2.303\,R}\left(\frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Insert Constants:**
With $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$pK_a(T) = 9.25 - \frac{52210}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left(\frac{1}{298.15} - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

$$pK_a(T) = 9.25 - 2729.92\left(\frac{1}{298.15} - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the Bracket Term:**

$$pK_a(T) = 9.25 - 2729.92 \times \left(\frac{T - 298.15}{298.15 \cdot T}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final Empirical Form (Emerson et al., 1975):**
A more practical form commonly cited in environmental engineering is:

$$pK_a = 0.09018 + \frac{2729.92}{T}$$

This is the form you'll find in KTU references and standard textbooks like **Metcalf & Eddy**.

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example:**
Determine the fraction of NH₃ at pH = 8.5 and T = 30 °C (303.15 K).

First, calculate $pK_a$:

$$pK_a = 0.09018 + \frac{2729.92}{303.15} = 0.09018 + 9.006 = 9.096$$

Now, the NH₃ fraction:

$$f_{NH_3} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(9.096 - 8.5)}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{0.596}}$$

$$10^{0.596} \approx 3.94 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{NH_3} = \frac{1}{1 + 3.94} = \frac{1}{4.94} \approx 0.2025$$

**Result:** About **20.25%** of total ammoniacal nitrogen is in the toxic NH₃ form — a significant toxicity concern for fish in receiving waters.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand for Nitrification

**Step 1 — Write the Overall Nitrification Reaction:**

$$\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Count Oxygen Atoms:**
- Reactant side: 2 O₂ = **4 oxygen atoms**
- Product side: NO₃⁻ (3 atoms) + H₂O (1 atom) = 4 oxygen atoms ✓
- Net O consumed = 4 atoms = 2 O₂ molecules

**Step 3 — Determine Per-Mass Stoichiometry:**
- Molecular weight of NH₄⁺-N = **14 g/mol** (we use the mass of N only)
- Molecular weight of O₂ = **32 g/mol** per molecule

For every **1 mole of NH₄⁺-N** oxidized:

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 2 \text{ moles} = 2 \times 32 = 64 \text{ g O}_2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Mass Basis:**

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ per g N} = \frac{64 \text{ g O}_2}{14 \text{ g N}} = 4.571 \text{ g O}_2/\text{g N}$$

**Step 5 — The 4.57 Rule:**
This is the **fundamental nitrification stoichiometry** used universally:

$$\boxed{\text{Oxygen Demand} = 4.57 \times [\text{NH}_3\text{-N}] \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify with Half-Reactions:**
Using electron balance:
- N: -3 → +5, transfers **8 electrons**
- O: 0 → -2, each O atom accepts **2 electrons**
- Therefore, 8 electrons / 2 electrons per O atom = **4 oxygen atoms** = 2 O₂ molecules ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: COD Required for Denitrification

**Step 1 — Write the Denitrification Reaction (using methanol as carbon source):**

$$6\text{NO}_3^- + 5\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3\text{N}_2 + 5\text{CO}_2 + 7\text{H}_2\text{O} + 6\text{OH}^-$$

**Step 2 — Calculate COD from Methanol:**
The theoretical COD of methanol is:
- CH₃OH + 1.5 O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O
- 1 mole CH₃OH (32 g) requires 1.5 mole O₂ (48 g)
- Therefore, COD of methanol = **1.5 g O₂/g CH₃OH**

**Step 3 — Per Mole of NO₃⁻-N:**
- 5 moles CH₃OH remove 6 moles NO₃⁻-N
- 5 × 32 = 160 g CH₃OH for 6 × 14 = 84 g N removed
- COD supplied = 160 × 1.5 = 240 g O₂

**Step 4 — Calculate the Ratio:**

$$\text{COD/N ratio} = \frac{240}{84} = 2.857 \approx 2.86 \text{ g COD/g NO}_3^-\text{-N}$$

**Step 5 — Generalization:**
The minimum theoretical COD/N for complete denitrification is **2.86 g/g**, with **~2.47 g/g** for lower alcohols and **~2.0 g/g** for readily biodegradable substrates.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary of N, S, P Forms

| Parameter | Nitrogen | Sulphur | Phosphorus |
|-----------|----------|---------|------------|
| **Redox-active?** | Yes (8 electron range) | Yes (8 electron range) | No redox transformations |
| **Reduced form (most toxic)** | NH₃ (un-ionized) | H₂S | — |
| **Oxidized form (most stable)** | NO₃⁻ | SO₄²⁻ | PO₄³⁻ |
| **Anaerobic end-product** | NH₃ / N₂ (after denit.) | H₂S | PO₄³⁻ (released) |
| **Aerobic end-product** | NO₃⁻ | SO₄²⁻ | PO₄³⁻ (mineralized) |
| **Gas release** | N₂, N₂O, NH₃ | H₂S, SO₂ | None (unless as PH₃) |
| **O₂ demand for full oxidation** | 4.57 g O₂/g N | 2.0 g O₂/g S | None (already oxidized) |
| **Typical food waste concentration** | 50–1500 mg N/L | 5–500 mg S/L | 10–100 mg P/L |
| **Primary removal method** | Nitrif.-Denitrif. | Precipitation, oxidation | Chemical/biological precipitation |
| **Recovery potential** | Struvite, ammonium salts | Elemental S (biogenic) | Struvite, Ca-P compounds |
| **Toxicity threshold (aquatic life)** | 0.025 mg NH₃/L | 0.5 mg H₂S/L | Eutrophication at 0.01–0.02 mg P/L |
| **Analytical method** | Kjeldahl, Ion Chromatography | Iodometric, IC | Ascorbic acid, ICP-OES |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define the following terms with one example each:*
*(a) Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN)*
*(b) Orthophosphate*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN):** TKN is the sum of organic nitrogen and ammoniacal nitrogen (NH₃ + NH₄⁺) in a water sample, determined by the Kjeldahl digestion method. The procedure involves digesting the sample with concentrated H₂SO₄, which converts all organically bound nitrogen and ammonia into ammonium sulphate. The ammonia is then distilled and titrated.

$$\text{TKN (mg/L)} = \text{Organic N} + \text{NH}_3\text{-N} + \text{NH}_4^+\text{-N}$$

**Example:** In dairy industry wastewater, TKN typically ranges from **200–1000 mg/L**, with organic N (from milk proteins like casein) being the dominant fraction.

**(b) Orthophosphate:** Orthophosphate refers to the inorganic, directly bioavailable form of phosphorus, consisting of PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, and H₂PO₄⁻ species in equilibrium depending on pH. At typical wastewater pH (6.5–7.5), H₂PO₄⁻ and HPO₄²⁻ dominate in roughly equal proportions.

**Example:** Orthophosphate is the form added to boilers as a corrosion inhibitor and is also the form directly taken up by algae, making it the **primary culprit in eutrophication**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper 1]**
*List the various forms of sulphur present in food industry wastewater. Briefly explain why H₂S is a major concern in anaerobic treatment.*

**Model Answer:**

The various forms of sulphur in food industry wastewater are:

1. **Organic Sulphur** – Present in proteins (cysteine, methionine amino acids)
2. **Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S)** – Toxic gas produced under anaerobic conditions
3. **Sulphide ion (S²⁻)** – Dissociated form at high pH
4. **Bisulphide ion (HS⁻)** – Dominant form at neutral pH
5. **Sulphite (SO₃²⁻)** – Used in food preservation (e.g., fruit drying, wine-making)
6. **Sulphate (SO₄²⁻)** – Stable, oxidized form
7. **Elemental Sulphur (S⁰)** – Intermediate oxidation product

**H₂S Concern in Anaerobic Treatment:**
- **Toxicity to methanogens:** H₂S at concentrations **>50–250 mg/L** inhibits methane-producing archaea, reducing biogas yield.
- **Corrosion:** H₂S reacts with metal surfaces to form FeS, causing severe pipeline degradation in digesters and sewers.
- **Competition from SRB:** Sulphate-reducing bacteria (SRB) outcompete methanogens for acetate and H₂ when SO₄²⁻/COD > 0.67, diverting electrons away from CH₄ production.
- **Odour nuisance:** H₂S has a characteristic rotten-egg smell detectable at concentrations as low as **0.5 ppb**, causing public complaints.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3, December 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various forms of nitrogen present in food industry wastewater. With the help of balanced chemical equations, describe the process of nitrification and denitrification. Also state the stoichiometric oxygen demand for complete nitrification.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dairy industry wastewater sample has the following characteristics:
- Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN) = **350 mg/L**
- Nitrite-Nitrogen (NO₂⁻-N) = **5 mg/L**
- Nitrate-Nitrogen (NO₃⁻-N) = **15 mg/L**
- pH = **7.5**
- Temperature = **28 °C**

Calculate:
(i) Total Nitrogen (TN) of the sample.
(ii) The fraction of ammonia (NH₃) in the total ammoniacal nitrogen, assuming all TKN is present as NH₃/NH₄⁺.
(iii) The theoretical oxygen demand (in mg/L) for complete nitrification of all NH₃-N.
(iv) The COD required (in mg/L) for complete denitrification of all NO₃⁻-N.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

**Forms of Nitrogen in Food Industry Wastewater:**

Food industry wastewater contains nitrogen in the following six forms:

1. **Organic Nitrogen:** Proteins, amino acids, urea, nucleic acids — typically the largest fraction in fresh food waste (oxidation state = -3).
2. **Ammoniacal Nitrogen:** NH₃ (free ammonia) and NH₄⁺ (ammonium ion) in pH-dependent equilibrium (oxidation state = -3).
3. **Nitrite Nitrogen (NO₂⁻):** Intermediate product of nitrification (oxidation state = +3).
4. **Nitrate Nitrogen (NO₃⁻):** End product of nitrification (oxidation state = +5).
5. **Nitrogen Gas (N₂):** End product of denitrification (oxidation state = 0).
6. **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O):** Greenhouse gas, intermediate of incomplete denitrification.

**Nitrification (Two-Step Biological Oxidation):**

Performed by chemoautotrophic bacteria under aerobic conditions:

**Step 1 — Ammonia to Nitrite (by *Nitrosomonas*):**

$$2\text{NH}_4^+ + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2^- + 4\text{H}^+ + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Nitrite to Nitrate (by *Nitrobacter*):**

$$2\text{NO}_2^- + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_3^-$$

**Overall Nitrification Reaction:**

$$\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Denitrification (Anoxic Reduction):**

Performed by heterotrophic bacteria (e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Alcaligenes*) using NO₃⁻ as terminal electron acceptor:

$$2\text{NO}_3^- + 12\text{H}^+ + 10\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand:**

For every **1 g of NH₃-N** oxidized, **4.57 g of O₂** is required.

This is derived from the oxidation state change: N goes from -3 to +5 (loss of 8 electrons), each O₂ molecule accepts 4 electrons, hence 2 O₂ molecules per N atom. With molecular weights:

$$\frac{2 \times 32}{14} = 4.57 \text{ g O}_2/\text{g N}$$

---

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- TKN = 350 mg/L
- NO₂⁻-N = 5 mg/L
- NO₃⁻-N = 15 mg/L
- pH = 7.5, T = 28 °C = 301.15 K

**(i) Total Nitrogen (TN):**

$$\text{TN} = \text{TKN} + \text{NO}_2^-\text{-N} + \text{NO}_3^-\text{-N}$$

$$\text{TN} = 350 + 5 + 15 = \boxed{370 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(ii) Fraction of NH₃:**

First, calculate $pK_a$ at T = 301.15 K:

$$pK_a = 0.09018 + \frac{2729.92}{301.15} = 0.09018 + 9.066 = 9.156$$

Now, the NH₃ fraction:

$$f_{NH_3} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(9.156 - 7.5)}}$$

$$10^{(9.156 - 7.5)} = 10^{1.656} = 45.29$$

$$f_{NH_3} = \frac{1}{1 + 45.29} = \frac{1}{46.29} = \boxed{0.0216 \text{ or } 2.16\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Only about 2.16% of total ammoniacal nitrogen is in toxic NH₃ form at this pH and temperature.

**(iii) Oxygen Demand for Nitrification:**

The TKN value represents the total NH₃-N + organic N. Assuming all TKN is in the form of NH₃-N (worst-case, conservative design):

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ demand} = 4.57 \times [\text{NH}_3\text{-N}] = 4.57 \times 350$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ demand} = \boxed{1599.5 \text{ mg O}_2/\text{L}}$$

**(iv) COD for Denitrification:**

For denitrification of NO₃⁻-N:

$$\text{COD required} = 2.86 \times [\text{NO}_3^-\text{-N}] = 2.86 \times 15$$

$$\text{COD required} = \boxed{42.9 \text{ mg COD/L}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 4, June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various forms of phosphorus found in food industry wastewater. Explain the principle and chemistry of struvite (MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O) precipitation as a nutrient recovery technique. List the optimal conditions required for effective struvite crystallization.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A seafood processing industry generates wastewater with the following characteristics:
- Total Sulphur = **180 mg/L** (as S)
- Sulphate (SO₄²⁻) concentration = **250 mg/L** (as SO₄²⁻)
- COD = **2500 mg/L**
- pH = **7.2**

Calculate:
(i) The mass of H₂S that can be produced (theoretically) from the complete reduction of SO₄²⁻ by sulphate-reducing bacteria.
(ii) Whether anaerobic digestion will be inhibited by SRB based on the SO₄²⁻/COD ratio.
(iii) The mass of Mg(OH)₂ required (in mg/L) to precipitate all PO₄³⁻ as struvite, assuming the wastewater contains 40 mg/L of PO₄³⁻-P.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

**Forms of Phosphorus in Food Industry Wastewater:**

Phosphorus in food wastewater exists in three primary forms:

1. **Orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, H₂PO₄⁻):** The dissolved, inorganic, directly bioavailable form. At pH 6.5–7.5, H₂PO₄⁻ and HPO₄²⁻ are the dominant species.

2. **Polyphosphate (P₂O₇⁴⁻, P₃O₁₀⁵⁻):** Condensed phosphates from cleaning agents and food additives (e.g., sodium tripolyphosphate in processed meats). They hydrolyze slowly to orthophosphate.

3. **Organic Phosphorus:** Phospholipids, nucleic acids (DNA, RNA), ATP, and phytic acid (in cereals/legumes). Requires enzymatic mineralization by phosphatases to release PO₄³⁻.

**Total Phosphorus (TP) = Orthophosphate + Polyphosphate + Organic Phosphorus**

**Principle of Struvite Precipitation:**

Struvite (MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O) is a white crystalline compound that precipitates when Mg²⁺, NH₄⁺, and PO₄³⁻ are present in **equimolar concentrations** above the solubility product (Ksp ≈ 10⁻¹³·²⁶):

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{PO}_4^{3-} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{MgNH}_4\text{PO}_4 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \downarrow$$

The reaction is favored by **high pH (8.5–9.5)** and **supersaturation**. Struvite is a slow-release fertilizer with N-P₂O₅-K₂O content of approximately **5-28-0**.

**Optimal Conditions for Struvite Crystallization:**

| Parameter | Optimal Range |
|-----------|---------------|
| pH | 8.5 – 9.5 |
| Mg : N : P molar ratio | 1 : 1 : 1 (with slight Mg excess) |
| Temperature | 25 – 35 °C |
| Mixing intensity | Gentle (G = 50–100 s⁻¹) |
| Supersaturation | Moderate to high (SI > 1.5) |
| Foreign ions | Avoid excessive Ca²⁺ (forms Ca-P competing precipitate) |

**Application in Food Industry:** Struvite recovery is particularly effective in:
- **Dairy wastewater** (high N, P, Mg)
- **Meat processing** (rich in NH₄⁺ from protein breakdown)
- **Centrate/dewatering liquor** from anaerobic digesters

---

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- Total Sulphur = 180 mg/L (as S)
- SO₄²⁻ = 250 mg/L (as SO₄²⁻)
- COD = 2500 mg/L
- pH = 7.2
- PO₄³⁻-P = 40 mg/L

**(i) Theoretical H₂S Production:**

The reaction for sulphate reduction:

$$\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2\text{CH}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S} + 2\text{HCO}_3^-$$

Molar mass of SO₄²⁻ = 96 g/mol; Molar mass of H₂S = 34 g/mol.

Moles of SO₄²⁻ per litre:

$$n_{SO_4} = \frac{250}{96} = 2.604 \text{ mmol/L}$$

Moles of H₂S produced (1:1 stoichiometry):

$$n_{H_2S} = 2.604 \text{ mmol/L}$$

Mass of H₂S:

$$m_{H_2S} = 2.604 \times 34 = \boxed{88.54 \text{ mg H}_2\text{S/L}}$$

**(ii) SRB Inhibition Check:**

Calculate the SO₄²⁻/COD ratio. First, express SO₄²⁻ on a "COD equivalent" basis or simply use mass ratio:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{[\text{SO}_4^{2-}]}{[\text{COD}]} = \frac{250}{2500} = 0.10$$

The **critical ratio** for SRB outcompeting methanogens is approximately **0.67** (on a mass basis). Since:

$$0.10 < 0.67$$

**Conclusion:** The SO₄²⁻/COD ratio is **below the critical threshold**, so methanogenic activity should **NOT** be severely inhibited. Anaerobic digestion is expected to proceed normally with methane as the dominant biogas product.

**(iii) Mg(OH)₂ Required for Struvite Precipitation:**

**Step 1 — Moles of PO₄³⁻-P:**

$$n_P = \frac{40}{31} = 1.290 \text{ mmol/L}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometry of Struvite:**

Reaction: $\text{Mg(OH)}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow 2\text{MgNH}_4\text{PO}_4 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$

Wait — let me write the correct balanced equation with Mg(OH)₂:

$$\text{Mg(OH)}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \rightarrow \text{MgNH}_4\text{PO}_4 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \downarrow + \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \text{(excess)}$$

Simpler: 1 mole Mg(OH)₂ provides 1 mole Mg²⁺, which precipitates 1 mole PO₄³⁻ (assuming NH₄⁺ is non-limiting).

Moles of Mg(OH)₂ required = Moles of PO₄³⁻-P = 1.290 mmol/L

**Step 3 — Mass of Mg(OH)₂:**

Molar mass of Mg(OH)₂ = 24 + 2(16+1) = **58 g/mol**

$$m_{Mg(OH)_2} = 1.290 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L} \times 58 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$m_{Mg(OH)_2} = 0.0748 \text{ g/L} = \boxed{74.8 \text{ mg Mg(OH)}_2/\text{L}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Board Exams (Forms of N, S, P):**
>
> 1. **Confusing TKN with TN (4-mark loss):** Students often write TN = TKN, forgetting that nitrite and nitrate forms are NOT included in Kjeldahl digestion. Always state: **TN = TKN + NO₂⁻-N + NO₃⁻-N**.
>
> 2. **Wrong pKa formula (3-mark loss):** The empirical formula is **pKa = 0.09018 + 2729.92/T(K)** — NOT a constant 9.25. Many students use 9.25 universally, which is only valid at 25 °C.
>
> 3. **Oxygen demand value (2-mark loss):** The correct value is **4.57 g O₂/g NH₃-N** for nitrification. Using 4.0 or 4.3 is a partial error; full marks require the decimal precision.
>
> 4. **Forgetting electroneutrality in precipitation (3-mark loss):** When asked about struvite, students often forget that struvite is a **triple salt** requiring Mg²⁺, NH₄⁺, and PO₄³⁻ simultaneously. Listing only two ions is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Ignoring pH in NH₃ toxicity (2-mark loss):** Stating "NH₃ is toxic at 0.5 mg/L" is wrong without specifying pH and temperature. The actual threshold for fish toxicity is **0.025 mg un-ionized NH₃-N/L**, which translates to varying total NH₃-N depending on pH.
>
> 6. **Unit inconsistency (1-mark deduction per occurrence):** Mixing mg/L as N with mg/L as SO₄²⁻ in the same calculation. Always **convert to common basis** (as N or as the element/ion).
>
> 7. **Missing the SO₄²⁻/COD ratio interpretation (4-mark loss):** When asked about anaerobic digester failure, students often compute the ratio but fail to compare it with the critical value of **0.67**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Forms of N, S, P in Food Industry Waste**

### Nitrogen (N) — Key Points
- ☐ **Six forms:** Organic-N, NH₃, NH₄⁺, NO₂⁻, NO₃⁻, N₂ (and N₂O)
- ☐ **TKN = Organic N + NH₃-N + NH₄⁺-N** (NO₂⁻ and NO₃⁻ excluded)
- ☐ **TN = TKN + NO₂⁻-N + NO₃⁻-N**
- ☐ **NH₃ fraction formula:** $f = 1 / [1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}]$
- ☐ **pKa = 0.09018 + 2729.92/T(K)**
- ☐ **Nitrification oxygen demand:** 4.57 g O₂/g NH₃-N
- ☐ **Nitrification alkalinity consumed:** 7.14 g CaCO₃/g NH₃-N
- ☐ **Denitrification COD requirement:** 2.86 g COD/g NO₃⁻-N
- ☐ **Nitrifying bacteria:** *Nitrosomonas* (Step 1), *Nitrobacter* (Step 2)
- ☐ **Denitrifying bacteria:** *Pseudomonas*, *Alcaligenes* (heterotrophic, anoxic)

### Sulphur (S) — Key Points
- ☐ **Forms:** Org-S, H₂S, HS⁻, S²⁻, SO₃²⁻, SO₄²⁻, S⁰
- ☐ **pKa values:** H₂S = 7.04; HS⁻ = 11.96 (at 25 °C)
- ☐ **SRB critical ratio:** SO₄²⁻/COD < 0.67 for healthy methanogenesis
- ☐ **H₂S toxicity threshold:** 0.5 mg/L in liquid (odour); 50–250 mg/L to methanogens
- ☐ **Dairy/brewery SO₄²⁻ range:** 200–1000 mg/L
- ☐ **SRB reaction:** SO₄²⁻ + 2CH₂O → H₂S + 2HCO₃⁻
- ☐ **Sulphite use in food:** Preservative in wines, dried fruits (SO₂)
- ☐ **Recovery:** Biogenic elemental sulphur from Thiobacillus bioleaching

### Phosphorus (P) — Key Points
- ☐ **Forms:** Ortho-P, Poly-P, Organic-P, Particulate-P
- ☐ **pKa values:** 2.15, 7.20, 12.35 (H₃PO₄ dissociation)
- ☐ **At pH 6.5–7.5:** H₂PO₄⁻ and HPO₄²⁻ dominate
- ☐ **Polyphosphate source:** STPP in detergents, food additives
- ☐ **Struvite formula:** MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O (1:1:1 molar ratio)
- ☐ **Struvite optimal pH:** 8.5 – 9.5
- ☐ **Chemical precipitation:** Alum (Al³⁺), FeCl₃, lime [Ca(OH)₂]
- ☐ **EBPR mechanism:** PAOs accumulate poly-P under aerobic stress
- ☐ **C:N:P for biological growth:** ~100:5:1 (mass ratio)
- ☐ **Eutrophication threshold:** 0.01–0.02 mg P/L in receiving water

### Cross-Cutting Engineering Insights
- ☐ **Struvite recovery** uniquely addresses BOTH N and P simultaneously
- ☐ **Nitrification–denitrification** is the cornerstone of N removal
- ☐ **SRB vs. methanogen competition** is a critical design consideration for S-rich waste
- ☐ **Phosphorus has no redox cycle** — only physical/chemical transformations
- ☐ **All three elements (N, S, P)** can be recovered as **fertilizer or soil amendment** — promoting circular economy
- ☐ **Toxicity thresholds** must be assessed using the **un-ionized form (NH₃, H₂S)** rather than total concentration
- ☐ **Temperature, pH, and redox potential** are the three master variables that govern form distribution

---

*End of Module 3 Notes — Forms of Nitrogen, Sulphur and Phosphorus*
*Course: OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT | KTU 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anions and Cations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Anions and Cations # Module 3: Anions and Cations in Food Industry Wastewater ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management (OEFTT834)**, *anions* and *cations* refer to the dissolved ionic species present in food processing effluents, sludge leachates, and wastewater streams. These ions are generated from raw material processing, cleaning-in-place (CIP) operations, salt-based preservation, and biological transformations occurring within treatment systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024):** > - **Anions** are negatively charged ions (e.g., $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{NO}_3^-$, $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$, $\text{HCO}_3^-$) that migrate toward the **anode** (positive electrode) during electrolysis. > - **Cations** are positively charged ions (e.g., $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{K}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$, $\text{NH}_4^+$) that migrate toward the **cathode** (negative electrode) during electrolysis. The **electroneutrality principle** governs their coexistence in any aqueous solution: $$\sum c_i z_i = 0$$ where $c_i$ is the molar concentration of ion $i$ and $z_i$ is its charge number (with sign). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Magnet and Iron Filings" Analogy** > > Imagine a glass of salty water connected to a battery with two metal plates dipped inside. Within seconds, tiny invisible "workers" begin sorting themselves: > - The **positive plate (anode)** attracts the *negatively charged* workers — these are your **anions** (e.g., chloride). > - The **negative plate (cathode)** attracts the *positively charged* workers — these are your **cations** (e.g., sodium). > > In a dairy or pickle industry, dissolved table salt ($\text{NaCl}$) splits into $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$, and when electricity is applied, they obediently march to opposite plates. This is the foundational principle behind **electrodialysis**, **ion exchange**, and **electrocoagulation** — three workhorse technologies in food wastewater treatment. ### 1.3 Significance in Food Industry Waste > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Topic Matters in OEFTT834:** > 1. **Discharge Compliance**: CPCB/SPCB norms mandate limits on TDS, chlorides, sulphates, and total hardness. > 2. **Treatment Selection**: High $\text{Ca}^{2+}/\text{Mg}^{2+}$ → softening needed; high $\text{NH}_4^+$ → nitrification required. > 3. **Soil & Crop Impact**: Irrigation with high-sodium wastewater causes **sodicity** and soil structural damage. > 4. **Equipment Scaling**: $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ cause boiler/heat exchanger scaling in food processing. > 5. **Process Recovery**: $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{K}^+$ recovery from whey/brewery waste has commercial value. ### 1.4 Key Ionic Species — Food Industry Context | Ion Type | Species | Typical Food Industry Source | Regulatory Limit (CPCB) | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | **Anion** | $\text{Cl}^-$ | Dairy, pickle, meat curing | **1000 mg/L** | | **Anion** | $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ | Fermentation, molasses-based | **1000 mg/L** | | **Anion** | $\text{NO}_3^-$ | Vegetable processing | **45 mg/L (NO}_3\text{-N)}$ | | **Anion** | $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ | Detergent, dairy cleaning | **5 mg/L** | | **Cation** | $\text{Na}^+$ | Salt-based preservation | **200 mg/L (irrigation SAR)** | | **Cation** | $\text{NH}_4^+$ | Protein-rich effluents | **50 mg/L (NH}_3\text{-N)}$ | | **Cation** | $\text{Ca}^{2+}, \text{Mg}^{2+}$ | Hard water, processing | **Hardness < 600 mg/L** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework The dissolved ionic load in food industry wastewater is characterized by the **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** fraction, which comprises both anions and cations in near-equimolar proportions (electroneutrality). ### 2.2 Ionic Balance Logic — Step-by-Step - **Step 1: Source Identification** — Raw materials (e.g., milk, fruits, grains) contain intrinsic mineral content; processing adds salts, acids, and alkalis. - **Step 2: Dissolution & Dissociation** — Ionic compounds dissociate in water: $$\text{NaCl}_{(s)} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{Na}^+_{(aq)} + \text{Cl}^-_{(aq)}$$ - **Step 3: Biological Transformation** — Organic nitrogen/phosphorus converts to $\text{NH}_4^+$, $\text{NO}_3^-$, $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ via ammonification, nitrification, and mineralization. - **Step 4: Charge Balance Enforcement** — At any pH, the sum of positive charges equals the sum of negative charges (in equivalents). - **Step 5: Analytical Quantification** — Each ion is measured via specific methods (titration, chromatography, spectroscopy). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Repository — Memorize for ESE** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Application | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | $\text{TDS} \approx \sum \text{cations} + \sum \text{anions}$ | Total Dissolved Solids estimation | mg/L | Water quality assessment | | 2 | $\text{Electroneutrality: } \sum c_i z_i = 0$ | Charge balance check | eq/L | Validate ionic analysis | | 3 | $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{2}}}$ | Sodium Adsorption Ratio | dimensionless | Irrigation suitability | | 4 | $\text{Hardness} = 2.5\,[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1\,[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | Total Hardness as CaCO$_3$ | mg/L | Boiler/cooling water | | 5 | $\text{Normality} = M \times \vert z \vert$ | Equivalent concentration | N | Titration stoichiometry | | 6 | $\text{M equivalent} = \dfrac{\text{mg/L}}{\text{Atomic mass} / \vert z \vert}$ | mg/L → meq/L conversion | meq/L | Ion balance table | | 7 | $\text{Conductivity} \propto \sum \lambda_i^\circ c_i$ | Ionic strength proxy | µS/cm | Field TDS estimation | | 8 | $[\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = K_w = 10^{-14}$ at 25°C | Water autoprotolysis | mol²/L² | pH and ion speciation | | 9 | $\text{NTU} = f(\text{turbidity, colloids})$ | Not an ion formula but relates to precipitates | NTU | Softening efficiency | | 10 | $\text{TKN} = \text{Organic N} + \text{NH}_3\text{-N}$ | Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen | mg/L | Cation $\text{NH}_4^+$ precursor | > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Tip for KTU Exams:** > To convert mg/L of an ion to **meq/L** (milliequivalents per litre), use: > $$\text{meq/L} = \dfrac{\text{mg/L} \times \vert z \vert}{\text{Atomic / Molecular mass}}$$ ### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Food Waste Engineering - **SAR** determines whether food industry wastewater can be used for **crop irrigation** (sodicity risk). - **Hardness** dictates whether **lime-soda softening** or **ion exchange** is needed before reuse. - **Normality-based titration** is the KTU-lab-standard method for chloride and hardness estimation. - **Electroneutrality** validates the accuracy of a complete ionic analysis — a mandatory step before designing **RO membranes** or **electrodialysis** units. ### 2.5 Analytical Methods — Mapping Ions to Techniques | Ion | Standard Method | Detection Limit | KTU Reference | |:---|:---|:---:|:---| | $\text{Cl}^-$ | Mohr / Volhard titration | 1 mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 32) | | $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ | Barium chloride / Turbidimetry | 1 mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 24) | | $\text{NO}_3^-$ | UV Spectrophotometry / Ion Chromatography | 0.1 mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 34) | | $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ | Stannous chloride / Ascorbic acid | 0.01 mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 31) | | $\text{Na}^+, \text{K}^+$ | Flame Photometry | 0.1 mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 45) | | $\text{Ca}^{2+}, \text{Mg}^{2+}$ | EDTA Complexometric Titration | 1 mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 40) | | $\text{NH}_4^+$ | Nesslerization / Distillation + Titration | 0.1 mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 34) | | All ions | Ion Chromatography (IC) | µg/L | APHA 4110 | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of Chloride Ion Concentration The chloride ion is measured by **Mohr's titration** using silver nitrate with potassium chromate indicator. The endpoint corresponds to the complete precipitation of $\text{Cl}^-$ as $\text{AgCl}$. **Step 1: Reaction Stoichiometry** $$\text{Ag}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AgCl} \downarrow \text{ (white)}$$ At the endpoint, the first excess drop of $\text{Ag}^+$ reacts with chromate to form a red-brown precipitate: $$2\text{Ag}^+ + \text{CrO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4 \downarrow \text{ (red-brown)}$$ **Step 2: Equivalence Relation** Let $V_s$ = sample volume (mL), $V_t$ = titre volume of $\text{AgNO}_3$ (mL), $N$ = normality of $\text{AgNO}_3$. By equivalence, the milli-equivalents of $\text{Ag}^+$ added equal the milli-equivalents of $\text{Cl}^-$ in the sample. $$N \times V_t = \dfrac{M_{\text{Cl}^-} \times V_s}{58.44}$$ where $M_{\text{Cl}^-}$ is the chloride concentration in mg/L and 58.44 is the equivalent weight of $\text{Cl}^-$. **Step 3: Solving for $M_{\text{Cl}^-}$** $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{Cl}^-} &= \dfrac{N \times V_t \times 58.44 \times 1000}{V_s} \\ &= \dfrac{N \times V_t \times 58440}{V_s} \quad \text{(mg/L)} \end{aligned}$$ **Worked Example (KTU-style):** A 50 mL dairy wastewater sample required 18.2 mL of 0.05 N $\text{AgNO}_3$ in Mohr's titration. Calculate $\text{Cl}^-$ concentration. $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{Cl}^-} &= \dfrac{0.05 \times 18.2 \times 58440}{50} \\ &= \dfrac{0.05 \times 18.2 \times 58440}{50} \\ &= \dfrac{53180.4}{50} \\ &= 1063.6 \text{ mg/L} \end{aligned}$$ > **Conclusion:** Chloride load = **1063.6 mg/L**, exceeding CPCB inland surface water discharge norm of **1000 mg/L** — treatment (e.g., anion exchange, RO) is required. ## 3.2 Derivation: Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR) SAR is **derived from the soil chemistry principle** that excess sodium relative to calcium and magnesium disperses soil colloids, reducing permeability. For irrigation water, the ionic concentrations (in **meq/L**) are inserted into the formula. **Step 1: Define Ionic Concentrations in meq/L** $$\begin{aligned} [\text{Na}^+]_{meq} &= \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]_{mg/L}}{23} \\ [\text{Ca}^{2+}]_{meq} &= \dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}]_{mg/L}}{20} \\ [\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{meq} &= \dfrac{[\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{mg/L}}{12.15} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Apply the SAR Equation** $$\text{SAR} = \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{2}}}$$ **Step 3: Worked Example** A food processing wastewater contains: $\text{Na}^+ = 460$ mg/L, $\text{Ca}^{2+} = 80$ mg/L, $\text{Mg}^{2+} = 24$ mg/L. Converting to meq/L: $$\begin{aligned} [\text{Na}^+] &= 460 / 23 = 20.0 \text{ meq/L} \\ [\text{Ca}^{2+}] &= 80 / 20 = 4.0 \text{ meq/L} \\ [\text{Mg}^{2+}] &= 24 / 12.15 \approx 1.98 \text{ meq/L} \end{aligned}$$ Now substitute: $$\begin{aligned} \text{SAR} &= \dfrac{20.0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{4.0 + 1.98}{2}}} \\ &= \dfrac{20.0}{\sqrt{2.99}} \\ &= \dfrac{20.0}{1.729} \\ &\approx 11.57 \end{aligned}$$ > **Conclusion:** SAR ≈ **11.57** — classifies water as **"High Sodium"** (USDA Class C3S2, restricted for fine-textured soils). Blending or gypsum amendment is needed before irrigation. ## 3.3 Derivation: Total Hardness as CaCO₃ Equivalent **Step 1: Standard Definition** Hardness is expressed as the equivalent concentration of $\text{CaCO}_3$ (eq. wt. = 50 g/eq). **Step 2: Conversion Formula** For a divalent cation $M^{2+}$ of concentration $C$ mg/L: $$\text{Hardness as CaCO}_3 = C \times \dfrac{50}{\text{Equivalent weight of } M^{2+}}$$ For $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ (eq. wt. = 20) and $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ (eq. wt. = 12.15): $$\text{Hardness} = [\text{Ca}^{2+}] \times \dfrac{50}{20} + [\text{Mg}^{2+}] \times \dfrac{50}{12.15} = 2.5\,[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1\,[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Complete Ion Balance Table (KTU Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ion Balance Validation** — A standard ESE question pattern. > > Given: Food industry effluent analysis report — complete the table, check electroneutrality, and comment. | Ion | mg/L | Atomic/Mol Mass | $\vert z \vert$ | meq/L | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ | 120 | 40 | 2 | **6.00** | | $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ | 36 | 24.3 | 2 | **2.96** | | $\text{Na}^+$ | 230 | 23 | 1 | **10.00** | | $\text{K}^+$ | 39 | 39 | 1 | **1.00** | | $\text{Cl}^-$ | 355 | 35.5 | 1 | **10.00** | | $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ | 96 | 96 | 2 | **2.00** | | $\text{HCO}_3^-$ | 488 | 61 | 1 | **8.00** | **Sum of cations** = $6.00 + 2.96 + 10.00 + 1.00 = 19.96$ meq/L **Sum of anions** = $10.00 + 2.00 + 8.00 = 20.00$ meq/L **Percentage error:** $$\%\text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert \sum \text{Cations} - \sum \text{Anions} \vert}{\sum \text{Cations} + \sum \text{Anions}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.04}{39.96} \times 100 \approx 0.10\%$$ > **Conclusion:** Error < 2% → **Analysis is valid** (acceptable per APHA standard ≤ 5%). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Migration Behavior of Anions and Cations (Electrodialysis Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FeedIn["Feed Wastewater Inlet"] FI["Brine Solution
containing
Na and Cl ions"] end subgraph Cell1["Cation Exchange Membrane"] CEM["CEM allows only
Cations to pass
blocks anions"] end subgraph Cell2["Anion Exchange Membrane"] AEM["AEM allows only
Anions to pass
