> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** "Match the unit to the pollutant's physical state." — *Suspended → Settle, Dissolved → Biodegrade, Refractory → Oxidize/adsorb, Pathogenic → Disinfect.*

| Pollutant Class | Physical State | Preferred Unit Operation |
|---|---|---|
| Large floating debris (sticks, packaging) | Gross solid | **Bar screen** (6–25 mm clear opening) |
| Grit, sand | Heavy inorganic | **Grit chamber** (controlled velocity **0.3 m/s**) |
| Oil & grease | Immiscible liquid | **DAF / API separator / grease trap** |
| Settleable solids | Suspended | **Primary clarifier** (overflow rate **25–40 m³/m²/day**) |
| Dissolved organics | Molecular (in solution) | **Activated sludge / UASB / MBBR** |
| Ammonia (NH₃) | Dissolved ion | **Nitrification** (nitrifying bacteria, aerobic) |
| Nitrate (NO₃⁻) | Dissolved ion | **Denitrification** (anoxic zone) |
| Phosphates (PO₄³⁻) | Dissolved ion | **Chemical precipitation with FeCl₃ / Al₂(SO₄)₃** |
| Pathogens | Microbial | **Chlorination (1–5 mg/L) / UV (30–40 mJ/cm²)** |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations constitute the **core numerical toolkit** for solving any food industry effluent treatment design problem in KTU examinations. Memorize the symbol definitions and the typical units.

| Formula | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| BOD₅ (Standard) | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)}{P}$ | mg/L | Dilution method BOD calculation |
| F/M Ratio | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ | Activated sludge process design (**0.2–0.5 d⁻¹** conventional) |
| Hydraulic Retention Time | $HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours or days | All reactor sizing |
| Sludge Volume Index | $SVI = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{X}$ | mL/g | Sludge settleability (**< 150 mL/g** is good) |
| COD/BOD Ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{COD}{BOD_5}$ | dimensionless | Biodegradability indicator (**> 0.5 = biodegradable**) |
| Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR) | $VLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·day | Anaerobic digester loading |
| Organic Loading Rate (OLR) | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{A}$ | kg BOD/m²·day | Trickling filter design |
| BOD Removal Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | % | Performance evaluation |
| Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | days | Activated sludge control |
| Recycle Ratio (R) | $R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q}$ | dimensionless | RAS / WAS calculations |

### Symbol Glossary

- $D_1, D_2$ — Initial and final dissolved oxygen of diluted sample (mg/L)
- $B_1, B_2$ — Initial and final DO of dilution water blank (mg/L)
- $P$ — Decimal fraction of sample volume
- $Q$ — Influent flow rate (m³/day)
- $V$ — Reactor volume (m³)
- $S_0$ — Influent substrate concentration (mg/L)
- $X$ — Mixed liquor suspended solids (mg/L)
- $V_{30}$ — Volume of settled sludge after 30 min (mL)
- $Q_r$ — Recycle flow rate
- $Q_w, Q_e$ — Waste sludge and effluent flows

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concepts in this unit directly govern:
- **Design of dairy, brewery, distillery, sugar, and meat-processing ETPs.**
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) reports** mandated by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC).**
- **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** systems in water-scarce zones.
- **Compliance audits** under ISO 14001 Environmental Management Systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Balance Around a Completely-Mixed Activated Sludge Reactor

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU examinations. We will perform an exhaustive step-by-step derivation to find the **effluent substrate concentration (Sₑ)** in terms of operating parameters.

### Statement

> For a steady-state, completely-mixed activated sludge reactor receiving a soluble substrate of concentration $S_0$ at flow rate $Q$, with recycle ratio $R$ and sludge wastage rate $Q_w$, derive the relationship between $S_e$ and the kinetic parameters.

### Step 1: Write the System Description

We have a single CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) of volume $V$ receiving:
- Influent flow $Q$ with substrate $S_0$
- Recycle flow $Q_r = R \cdot Q$ with substrate $S_e$ (since the recycle comes from the clarifier underflow, it is essentially thickened mixed liquor)
- Effluent flow $Q_e = Q$ with substrate $S_e$ (assumed soluble, leaves in the effluent)

### Step 2: Substrate Mass Balance (Steady-State)

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental principle: **Accumulation = Inflow − Outflow − Consumption (by reaction)**

$$0 = Q \cdot S_0 + Q_r \cdot S_e - (Q + Q_r) \cdot S_e - r_{su} \cdot V$$

where $r_{su}$ is the substrate utilization rate per unit volume.

### Step 3: Substitute Monod Kinetics

For activated sludge, the specific substrate utilization rate follows the **Monod equation**:

$$r_{su} = \dfrac{k \cdot X \cdot S_e}{K_s + S_e}$$

Substituting:

$$0 = Q \cdot S_0 + R \cdot Q \cdot S_e - (1 + R) \cdot Q \cdot S_e - \dfrac{k \cdot X \cdot S_e}{K_s + S_e} \cdot V$$

### Step 4: Simplify Inflow/Outflow Terms

The recycle terms partially cancel:

$$Q \cdot S_0 + R \cdot Q \cdot S_e - (1 + R) \cdot Q \cdot S_e = Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S_e$$

Therefore the mass balance becomes:

$$0 = Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) - \dfrac{k \cdot X \cdot S_e}{K_s + S_e} \cdot V$$

### Step 5: Rearrange for $S_e$

$$Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) = \dfrac{k \cdot X \cdot S_e}{K_s + S_e} \cdot V$$

Dividing both sides by $V$:

$$\dfrac{Q}{V} \cdot (S_0 - S_e) = \dfrac{k \cdot X \cdot S_e}{K_s + S_e}$$

Recognizing that $\dfrac{Q}{V} = \dfrac{1}{HRT}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{HRT} \cdot (S_0 - S_e) = \dfrac{k \cdot X \cdot S_e}{K_s + S_e}$$

### Step 6: Introduce the F/M Ratio

Recall $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$. After algebraic rearrangement and assuming $K_s \ll S_e$ (common simplification for high-strength food effluents):

$$S_e \approx \dfrac{K_s \cdot (1 + k \cdot \theta_c)}{\theta_c \cdot (k - 1/\theta_c)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental design equation for the activated sludge process** in food industry effluent treatment. It explicitly shows that **$S_e$ decreases as $\theta_c$ (SRT) increases**, justifying the use of high-SRT systems (like MBR) for low-effluent-quality targets.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Secondary Treatment Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam topic** — almost always tested in Part B (14 marks).

| Parameter | Activated Sludge (ASP) | UASB | MBBR | MBR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BOD Loading** | 0.3–0.6 kg BOD/m³·day | 5–15 kg COD/m³·day | 0.5–2.0 kg BOD/m³·day | 0.1–0.3 kg BOD/m³·day |
| **HRT** | 4–8 hours | 6–24 hours | 4–12 hours | 6–10 hours |
| **SRT** | 5–15 days | 30–100+ days | 15–45 days | 20–60 days |
| **Biomass form** | Suspended flocs | Granular sludge | Attached biofilm | Suspended flocs + UF membrane |
| **Effluent BOD₅** | 20–30 mg/L | 50–150 mg/L | 15–25 mg/L | < 5 mg/L |
| **Footprint** | Large | Very small | Compact | Compact |
| **Energy** | High (aeration) | Low | Moderate | High (membrane + aeration) |
| **Sludge yield** | High | Very low | Moderate | High |
| **Food industry suitability** | Medium (dairy, brewery) | High (distillery, sugar) | High (meat, vegetable) | Premium (dairy, beverage) |
| **Capital cost** | Low | Low | Medium | High |

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A dairy effluent has a flow of $Q = 500$ m³/day, BOD₅ $= 1500$ mg/L. It is to be treated in a completely-mixed activated sludge reactor with $X = 3000$ mg/L MLSS, achieving 90% BOD removal. Design the reactor volume assuming $F/M = 0.3$ day⁻¹.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply F/M definition:

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = 0.3$$

Step 2 — Solve for V:

$$V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}$$

$$V = \dfrac{500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 1500 \, \text{mg/L}}{3000 \, \text{mg/L} \times 0.3 \, \text{day}^{-1}}$$

$$V = \dfrac{750000}{900} \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V = 833.3 \, \text{m}^3$$

Step 3 — Calculate HRT:

$$HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{833.3}{500} = 1.67 \, \text{days} = 40 \, \text{hours}$$

Step 4 — Calculate Effluent BOD:

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot (1 - \eta) = 1500 \cdot (1 - 0.90) = 150 \, \text{mg/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 150 mg/L is *above* the CPCB inland discharge limit of 30 mg/L — therefore, a **tertiary polishing step** (e.g., sand filter + chlorination) is required downstream.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Complete Food Industry ETU Flow Train

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **complete functional flow** of a typical food industry Effluent Treatment Unit, from raw influent to final compliant discharge. Node IDs follow the alphanumeric prefix rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["RAW EFFLUENT INLET"] --> A2["BAR SCREEN 6 to 25 MM"]
    A2 --> A3["GRIT CHAMBER VELOCITY 0.3 M/S"]
    A3 --> A4["OIL AND GREASE TRAP OR DAF UNIT"]
    A4 --> A5["EQUALIZATION BASIN HRT 8 TO 24 HR"]
    A5 --> A6["PRIMARY CLARIFIER OFR 25 TO 40 M3/M2/DAY"]
    A6 --> A7["BIOLOGICAL REACTOR ASP OR UASB OR MBBR"]
    A7 --> A8["SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
    A8 --> A9["TERTIARY SAND FILTER"]
    A9 --> A10["ACTIVATED CARBON ADSORBER OPTIONAL"]
    A10 --> A11["DISINFECTION CHLORINATION OR UV"]
    A11 --> A12["TREATED EFFLUENT TO DISCHARGE OR REUSE"]
    A8 -.->|"SLUDGE RECYCLE"| A7
    A6 -.->|"PRIMARY SLUDGE"| B1["SLUDGE THICKENER"]
    A8 -.->|"WASTE ACTIVATED SLUDGE"| B1
    B1 --> B2["ANAEROBIC SLUDGE DIGESTER"]
    B2 --> B3["SLUDGE DEWATERING CENTRIFUGE OR BELT PRESS"]
    B3 --> B4["DRY CAKE TO LANDFILL OR COMPOSTING"]
    B2 -.->|"BIOGAS CAPTURE"| B5["GAS HOLDER FOR ENERGY RECOVERY"]
```

### Diagram Reading Guide (for the Mermaid block above)

- **Solid arrows** denote the **main liquid flow path** from raw influent to final discharge.
- **Dashed arrows** denote the **sludge flow path** to thickening, digestion, and dewatering.
- **A1 to A12** form the **liquid-phase treatment train**.
- **B1 to B5** form the **sludge handling subsystem**, with **B5** enabling **biogas-to-energy recovery** (a common sustainability feature in distilleries and breweries).

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Tertiary Treatment Subsystem (Detail View)

This expanded view focuses on the tertiary polishing stage, where residual organics, nutrients, and pathogens are removed to meet regulatory standards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["SECONDARY EFFLUENT INLET"] --> C2["SAND FILTER REMOVES TSS"]
    C2 --> C3["ACTIVATED CARBON REMOVES COLOR AND REFRACTORY COD"]
    C3 --> C4["NITRIFICATION TANK AEROBIC NH3 TO NO3"]
    C4 --> C5["DENITRIFICATION TANK ANOXIC NO3 TO N2 GAS"]
    C5 --> C6["CHEMICAL P PRECIPITATION FECL3 DOSAGE"]
    C6 --> C7["CHLORINE CONTACT TANK 30 MIN"]
    C7 --> C8["COMPLIANT TREATED WATER TO DISCHARGE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The sequence **C4 → C5** is **mandatory and irreversible** — nitrification **must precede** denitrification because denitrifying bacteria require nitrate (NO₃⁻) as the electron acceptor, which is only produced by nitrifying bacteria.

---

## 4.3 Decision Logic — When to Choose Aerobic vs. Anaerobic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the most frequently-asked KTU questions: *"Justify the choice of aerobic/anaerobic treatment for a given food industry effluent."* The decision is governed by **influent COD concentration** and **biogas recovery potential**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["START DECISION"] --> D2{"INFLUENT COD MG/L"}
    D2 -->|"COD < 1000"| D3["AEROBIC SYSTEM PREFERRED ASP OR MBBR"]
    D2 -->|"1000 < COD < 4000"| D4{"BIOGAS RECOVERY WANTED"}
    D2 -->|"COD > 4000"| D5["ANAEROBIC SYSTEM UASB HIGH RATE"]
    D4 -->|"YES"| D6["UASB FOLLOWED BY AEROBIC POLISHING"]
    D4 -->|"NO"| D3
    D3 --> D7["SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
    D5 --> D8["AEROBIC POLISHING OPTIONAL"]
    D6 --> D8
    D8 --> D9["TERTIARY AND DISCHARGE"]
    D7 --> D9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Effluent Treatment Unit" as applied to the food industry. List the four hierarchical levels of treatment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Effluent Treatment Unit (ETU)** is a coordinated assembly of physical, chemical, and biological processes designed to reduce the pollutional load of wastewater from a food processing facility to comply with statutory discharge standards (CPCB Schedule VI).

The four hierarchical levels are:
1. **Preliminary Treatment** — bar screens, grit chambers, oil & grease removal.
2. **Primary Treatment** — sedimentation / DAF for suspended solids.
3. **Secondary (Biological) Treatment** — activated sludge, UASB, MBBR for dissolved organics.
4. **Tertiary Treatment** — filtration, nutrient removal, disinfection for polishing.

*(Marking: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming levels, 1 mark for describing at least two levels.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the significance of the BOD/COD ratio in designing a food industry effluent treatment plant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **BOD/COD ratio** (denoted $\rho = COD / BOD_5$ ... conventionally we report BOD/COD as the inverse) is a **biodegradability index**. For food industry effluents, this ratio is typically **> 0.5**, indicating the organic fraction is **largely biodegradable** and therefore amenable to **biological treatment** (the lowest-cost option).

A ratio **< 0.3** indicates the presence of **refractory organics** that resist biodegradation, requiring **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** or **activated carbon adsorption**. This single metric drives the **choice of secondary treatment technology** and predicts **process performance**.

*(Marking: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the > 0.5 threshold, 1 mark for the engineering consequence.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss):** Students frequently *describe* treatment units but **fail to justify the selection** with numerical data (e.g., BOD load, F/M ratio, HRT). Always **justify** your technology choice with a **mass balance, kinetic parameter, or regulatory limit**. Marks are reserved for this justification step.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]

**A dairy processing plant discharges 800 m³/day of effluent with BOD₅ = 1800 mg/L and TSS = 600 mg/L. The discharge is to an inland surface water body.**

#### Part (a) [7 marks]
**Design a complete effluent treatment scheme (preliminary, primary, secondary, tertiary) for the plant. Justify each unit selection with at least one numerical parameter.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Equalization Basin (Preliminary):**
For a dairy plant with batch operations, an equalization basin with **HRT = 12 hours** is recommended.
$$V_{eq} = Q \times HRT = 800 \times 0.5 = 400 \, \text{m}^3$$
This buffers pH (typically 4–11 in dairy) and BOD fluctuations (1.2 times the daily average, peaking at **2160 mg/L**).

**Step 2 — Bar Screen & Grit Chamber:**
Bar screen clear opening **10 mm** (removes > 90% of gross solids), followed by a grit chamber designed at **horizontal velocity = 0.3 m/s** with detention time **60 s** to settle sand/silica without losing organic matter.

**Step 3 — DAF for Oil/Grease:**
Dairy effluent contains milk fats. **Dissolved Air Flotation** at **hydraulic loading 5–15 m³/m²·hr** removes 80–95% O&G, reducing load before biological stage.

**Step 4 — Primary Clarifier:**
**Overflow rate = 30 m³/m²·day** removes 50–60% TSS (300 mg/L) and 30% BOD (reducing BOD to ~1260 mg/L).
$$A_{pc} = \dfrac{Q}{OFR} = \dfrac{800}{30} = 26.7 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Activated Sludge (Secondary):**
With $S_0 = 1260$ mg/L, $F/M = 0.3$ day⁻¹, $X = 3500$ mg/L:
$$V_{ASP} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)} = \dfrac{800 \times 1260}{3500 \times 0.3} = 960 \, \text{m}^3$$
$$HRT = \dfrac{960}{800} = 1.2 \, \text{days}$$
Achieves 90–95% BOD removal, giving $S_e \approx 90$ mg/L.

**Step 6 — Tertiary Polishing:**
- **Sand filter** to reduce TSS below 30 mg/L.
- **Chlorination** at 2 mg/L dose, **30 min contact time** for coliform reduction to < 1000 MPN/100 mL.
- **Final effluent BOD₅ < 20 mg/L**, well within CPCB limit of 30 mg/L.

#### Part (b) [7 marks]
**If the plant wants to recover biogas for steam generation, propose a modified treatment scheme. Estimate the theoretical methane yield.**

**Model Answer:**

**Modification:** Insert a **UASB reactor** upstream of the activated sludge process.

**Design of UASB:**
- Influent to UASB: $S_0 = 1260$ mg/L COD-equivalent
- Volumetric organic loading: **VLR = 10 kg COD/m³·day**
- Required UASB volume: $V_{UASB} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{VLR} = \dfrac{800 \times 1.26}{10} = 100.8 \, \text{m}^3$

**Methane Yield Calculation (using the McCarty equation):**
$$V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times \left[ Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \right] - 1.42 \cdot P_x$$
Assume $S_e$ (UASB effluent) $= 250$ mg/L, and $P_x \approx 0$ (low sludge yield).

$$V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 800 \times (1.26 - 0.25) = 282.8 \, \text{m}^3 \, CH_4 / \text{day}$$
(at STP, 0 °C, 1 atm).

**Energy content:** Energy of methane ≈ **35.8 MJ/m³**; total daily energy ≈ **10,124 MJ/day**, sufficient for partial steam generation in the dairy.

**UASB effluent flows to ASP** for polishing to CPCB standards.

*(Marking: Part (a) — 1 mark per correct unit selection + numerical justification. Part (b) — 2 marks for UASB design, 3 marks for methane yield calculation, 2 marks for energy interpretation.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]

**A fruit juice processing industry generates 300 m³/day of effluent with pH = 3.5, BOD₅ = 2200 mg/L, COD = 4500 mg/L, and TSS = 800 mg/L.**

#### Part (a) [7 marks]
**Identify the challenges in treating this effluent. Propose a suitable preliminary and primary treatment scheme with justification.**

**Model Answer:**

**Challenges Identified:**
1. **Low pH (3.5):** Outside biological range (**6.5–8.5**) — must be neutralized before biological treatment.
2. **High BOD/COD ratio (2200/4500 = 0.49):** Borderline biodegradable — feasible for biological treatment.
3. **High TSS (800 mg/L):** Will cause clogging in biofilm reactors (MBBR, TF); needs effective sedimentation.
4. **Seasonal variability:** Fruit processing is **campaign-based** (only 4–6 months of operation) — requires robust equalization.
5. **High color** from fruit pigments (anthocyanins, carotenoids) — refractory in biological stage.

**Proposed Preliminary Treatment:**
- **pH correction tank** with **NaOH dosing (lime/Na₂CO₃)** to bring pH to 7.0 ± 0.5.
- **Neutralization HRT = 30 min** with inline pH controller.
- **Bar screen (15 mm)** and **grit chamber**.
- **Equalization basin HRT = 24 hours** to dampen seasonal peaks.
- **Cooling tower** if effluent temperature exceeds 40 °C (fruit processing can discharge hot streams).

**Proposed Primary Treatment:**
- **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** is preferred over sedimentation because:
  - High TSS with low-density organic particles (fruit pulp fibers) settle poorly.
  - DAF at **hydraulic loading 10–20 m³/m²·hr** with **10–30 mg/L polymer flocculant** removes 70–85% TSS and 30% BOD.
  - Produces **float sludge** which is easier to dewater than sedimented sludge.
- Expected effluent after primary: $TSS \approx 160$ mg/L, $BOD_5 \approx 1540$ mg/L.

#### Part (b) [7 marks]
**Design a UASB reactor for this effluent and compute the HRT.**

**Model Answer:**

**UASB Design Rationale:**
With influent COD = 4500 mg/L and BOD/COD = 0.49, the effluent is **biodegradable and high-strength**, making **UASB the most cost-effective choice** (low energy, biogas recovery, low sludge).

**Design Parameters:**
- Volumetric loading rate (VLR) for fruit juice: **8–12 kg COD/m³·day** (conservative)
- Choose **VLR = 10 kg COD/m³·day**

**Reactor Volume:**
$$V_{UASB} = \dfrac{Q \cdot COD_0}{VLR} = \dfrac{300 \times 4.5}{10} = 135 \, \text{m}^3$$

**HRT Calculation:**
$$HRT = \dfrac{V_{UASB}}{Q} = \dfrac{135}{300} = 0.45 \, \text{days} \approx 10.8 \, \text{hours}$$

**UASB Effluent Quality:**
Expected **60–75% COD removal**, giving $COD_{eff} \approx 1350$ mg/L and $BOD_5 \approx 660$ mg/L.

**Post-Treatment (to meet 30 mg/L BOD inland discharge):**
A **polishing aerobic stage** (e.g., MBBR) is essential to bring BOD to < 30 mg/L, followed by **tertiary filtration and disinfection**.

**Sludge & Biogas:**
- Granular sludge blanket height: **1–2 m** with upflow velocity **0.5–1.0 m/h**.
- Methane yield (using McCarty): ~**0.35 × Q × (COD_removed) ≈ 0.35 × 300 × 3.15 ≈ 331 m³ CH₄/day**.

*(Marking: Part (a) — 1 mark per challenge (≥3), 2 marks for neutralization, 2 marks for DAF justification. Part (b) — 3 marks for VLR selection, 2 marks for V calculation, 1 mark for HRT, 1 mark for post-treatment.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist during the last 30 minutes before your KTU exam for a focused review.**

- ☐ **Definition:** ETU = coordinated physical + chemical + biological treatment train; objective is **CPCB/SPCB compliance**.
- ☐ **Four tiers:** Preliminary → Primary → Secondary → Tertiary.
- ☐ **Preliminary units:** Bar screen, grit chamber ($v = 0.3$ m/s), oil & grease trap, equalization basin.
- ☐ **Primary units:** Clarifier (OFR 25–40 m³/m²·day), DAF (for high TSS or O&G).
- ☐ **Secondary units:** Aerobic — ASP, MBBR, TF, SBR, MBR; Anaerobic — UASB, ABR.
- ☐ **Tertiary units:** Sand filter, activated carbon, nitrification-denitrification, chemical P-precipitation, chlorination, UV.
- ☐ **Key design equations:** $F/M$, $HRT$, $SRT$, $SVI$, $VLR$, $OLR$, $\eta$.
- ☐ **Selection rule:** High-strength biodegradable → UASB; low-strength or strict limit → ASP/MBR; high color/refractory → AOPs.
- ☐ **Discharge limits to remember:** BOD₅ < 30 mg/L, COD < 250 mg/L, TSS < 100 mg/L, pH 6.5–8.5 (inland discharge).
- ☐ **BOD/COD > 0.5** → biodegradable, biological treatment preferred; **< 0.3** → refractory, physico-chemical needed.
- ☐ **Anaerobic advantage:** Low energy, biogas production, low sludge; **disadvantage:** slow start-up, requires post-treatment.
- ☐ **Aerobic advantage:** Compact, robust effluent; **disadvantage:** high energy (aeration), high sludge yield.
- ☐ **Methane yield equation:** $V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) - 1.42 \cdot P_x$ (McCarty, at STP).
- ☐ **Nitrification must precede denitrification** — always.
- ☐ **Sludge handling:** Thickener → Anaerobic Digester → Dewatering → Cake disposal; Biogas → energy recovery.
- ☐ **CPCB Schedule VI** is the master reference for all discharge standards in KTU problems.
- ☐ **ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge):** Increasingly required in water-scarce regions; uses RO + evaporation ponds + crystallization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Always start with effluent characterization → match unit operations to pollutant class → justify with mass balance or kinetic equation → check regulatory compliance → discuss sludge and energy."* Following this four-step structure consistently will earn full marks in KTU Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screening, Sedimentation and Floatation as Pre and Primary Treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Screening, Sedimentation and Floatation as Pre and Primary Treatments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pre-treatment & Primary Treatment — The "Gatekeepers" of Food Industry Effluent

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for *Food Industry Waste Management* (OEFTT834), Module 3 lays the foundation of **physical unit operations** that constitute the very first barrier between a polluted food-processing effluent stream and the downstream biological/tertiary treatment units. These operations are collectively classified as **Pre-treatment and Primary Treatment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard)**
> **Pre-treatment** refers to physical operations (screening, grit removal, comminution, floatation) applied to remove large suspended, floating, or coarse particulate matter that would otherwise damage pumps, clog pipes, or interfere with subsequent processes. **Primary Treatment** is the subsequent stage (chiefly sedimentation) designed to remove a major fraction of settleable suspended solids and associated organic matter using gravity alone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen-Sink Model

Imagine washing extremely dirty dishes after a Sunday lunch with sambar, dal, and vegetable peels.

- **Screening** = The **strainer in your sink drain**. You don't let big chunks (curry leaves, vegetable skins, meat gristle) enter the pipe because they will choke it.
- **Sedimentation** = **Leaving the dirty water in a bucket overnight**. Heavy particles (sand, rice grains, dal) settle to the bottom under gravity, leaving relatively clearer water on top.
- **Floatation** = **Skimming oil/grease that floats on top of a hot curry pot**. The lighter-than-water fats, oils, and grease (FOG) rise to the surface and can be physically scraped off.

These three physical operations are the **"big-volume, low-cost, mechanical workhorses"** of any food-industry effluent treatment plant (ETP), especially critical because food-industry wastewater (dairy, brewery, slaughterhouse, vegetable cannery) is typically **high in BOD/COD, suspended solids (SS), oils & grease (O&G), and biodegradable organics**.

### 1.3 Hierarchy in the Food-Industry ETP Treatment Train

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Treatment Train Order (Module 3 Focus)**
> 1. **Equalisation & Neutralisation** (Module 2 concept, but a prerequisite)
> 2. **Screening** → **Grit Removal** → **Comminution** (Pre-treatment)
> 3. **Floatation** (DAF — Pre/Primary)
> 4. **Sedimentation / Primary Clarifier** (Primary Treatment)
> 5. → Biological (Secondary) Treatment → Tertiary → Sludge Handling

### 1.4 Screening — Formal KTU Definition

**Screening** is the **physical separation of solids from wastewater by passing it through a perforated plate, mesh, or bars with uniform openings**. The retained solids are called **screenings**; the liquid passing through is called **screen effluent** or **screened wastewater**.

The openings may be **bars, perforations, or wire mesh**, classifying screens into the categories discussed in Section 2.

### 1.5 Sedimentation — Formal KTU Definition

**Sedimentation** is the **gravity-driven separation of suspended particles heavier than water, accomplished by reducing the velocity of the wastewater flow below the settling velocity of the particles**, allowing them to settle at the bottom of a quiescent tank.

The unit operation is realised in a **settling tank / sedimentation tank / clarifier**. In food-industry ETP design, sedimentation is rated a **primary treatment** because it can typically remove **50–70 % of total suspended solids (TSS)** and **25–40 % of BOD₅** in domestic-strength wastewater, with comparable or higher values for food streams.

### 1.6 Floatation — Formal KTU Definition

**Floatation** (specifically **Dissolved Air Flotation, DAF**, in food-industry practice) is a **physical separation process in which microscopic air bubbles (typically 10–100 µm diameter) are attached to suspended matter, causing the particle–bubble agglomerate to rise to the surface where it is skimmed off as floated sludge**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Floatation is Critical for Food Industry Wastewater**
> Food-industry streams contain high concentrations of **fats, oils, grease (FOG), proteins, emulsified matter, and colloidal particles** that are *lighter than water* or *of near-neutral density*. Conventional sedimentation alone is insufficient because these particles **do not settle**. Floatation exploits their surface hydrophobicity and attachment to air bubbles for removal.

### 1.7 Standard Metrics & Units (Bolded as Required)

- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT / Detention Time, t_d)** = **hours (h)** — typical primary clarifier: **1.5 to 2.5 h**
- **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR), v_o** = **m³/(m²·d)** or **m/h** — typical: **24 to 48 m³/(m²·d)**
- **Weir Loading Rate** = **m³/(m·d)** — typical: **125 to 250 m³/(m·d)**
- **Bar spacing (screen opening)** = **mm** — coarse: **≥ 50 mm**, fine: **0.25 – 6 mm**
- **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S, in DAF)** = dimensionless — typical food industry: **0.005 – 0.060**
- **Recycle ratio (DAF)** = dimensionless — typical: **10 – 30 %**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SCREENING — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Classification of Screens (High-Yield Table for KTU)

| Screen Type | Opening Size | Bar/mesh geometry | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bar Rack (Coarse)** | **≥ 50 mm** (typically 50–100 mm) | Parallel bars | Remove large debris: rags, sticks, bones, vegetable matter |
| **Medium Screen** | **6 – 40 mm** | Perforated plates / bars | Protect downstream pumps |
| **Fine Screen** | **0.25 – 6 mm** | Wedge-wire / perforated | Remove smaller fibrous solids |
| **Micro-screen** | **< 0.25 mm** | Fine woven mesh | Polishing step; rare in food ETPs |

### 2.1.2 Cleaning Mechanism — How Screening "Works"

1. **Approach velocity** of wastewater (typically **0.6 to 1.2 m/s** at peak flow) carries the water–solid mixture to the screen face.
2. **Inertial and gravitational forces** drive larger, heavier solids to be **retained on the screen face**.
3. Water **passes through the openings** by gravity or under slight head.
4. **Headloss builds** as screenings accumulate — when Δh exceeds design threshold (typically **150 – 600 mm**), cleaning is initiated (manual raking or mechanical cleaning by rakes/brooms driven by chains).

### 2.1.3 Design Equations (Key for KTU Numericals)

**Clear opening area between bars (efficiency):**

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{clear opening}}{\text{bar spacing + bar width}} \times 100\%
$$

> A well-designed bar rack has **η ≈ 60 – 80 %** to avoid excessive clogging.

**Headloss through a clean bar rack (Kirschmer's equation, KTU most-tested):**

$$
h_L = \beta \left(\frac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \frac{v^2}{2g} \sin \theta
$$

Where:
- $h_L$ = headloss (m)
- $\beta$ = **bar shape factor** (1.67 for sharp-edged, 1.44 for rectangular, 0.76 for circular)
- $w$ = maximum bar width perpendicular to flow (m)
- $b$ = clear spacing between bars (m)
- $v$ = velocity through the rack (m/s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\theta$ = angle of bars with horizontal (typically 60°)

> When partially clogged, multiply by a factor (often 3 for partially clogged).

### 2.1.4 Why & How — The Engineering "Why"

- **Why screen?** To protect downstream centrifugal pumps from ragging, to prevent clogging of small-bore lines, and to reduce loading on primary clarifiers.
- **How?** By physically intercepting large solids whose one dimension exceeds the screen opening — purely a **size-exclusion** mechanism.

---

## 2.2 SEDIMENTATION — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 The Four Types of Settling (Mandatory KTU Concept)

| Type | Particle Concentration | Description | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I — Discrete** | < 1000 mg/L | Particles settle as individuals; no flocculation | **Stoke's Law** |
| **Type II — Flocculent** | 1000 – 5000 mg/L | Particles coalesce during settling; mass increases, settling accelerates | Empirical curves (no single equation) |
| **Type III — Hindered / Zone** | > 5000 mg/L | Suspension settles as a blanket; clear liquid–sludge interface | Vesilind-type equations |
| **Type IV — Compression** | Very high (sludge layer) | Compression of settled sludge under its own weight | Empirical |

> For **food industry primary clarifiers**, the design is dominated by **Type II flocculent settling**.

### 2.2.2 Stoke's Law — The Cornerstone Equation (Type I)

$$
v_s = \frac{g \, (\rho_s - \rho_w) \, d^2}{18 \, \mu}
$$

Where:
- $v_s$ = settling velocity of particle (m/s)
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho_s$ = particle density (kg/m³)
- $\rho_w$ = water density ≈ **1000 kg/m³**
- $d$ = particle diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water (N·s/m² or Pa·s); at 20 °C, $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

### 2.2.3 Design Criteria for Primary Clarifiers (Food Industry)

**Surface Overflow Rate (SOR), $v_o$:**

$$
v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = design flow (m³/d or m³/h)
- $A_s$ = surface area of clarifier (m²)

**Detention time, $t_d$:**

$$
t_d = \frac{V}{Q}
$$

Where $V$ = volume of clarifier (m³).

**Weir loading rate (overflow rate at the effluent weir):**

$$
q_w = \frac{Q}{L_w}
$$

Where $L_w$ = total weir length (m).

### 2.2.4 Settling Tank Efficiency — The "100 % Removal Concept"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept — Discrete Settling Efficiency**
> For an ideal rectangular sedimentation tank with horizontal flow, **all particles with settling velocity $v_s \ge v_o = Q/A_s$ will be 100 % removed**. Particles with $v_s < v_o$ are removed in proportion to $v_s / v_o$. This gives the **ideal fractional removal equation:**

$$
\text{Fraction Removed} = 1 - \frac{v_o}{v_s}
$$

This is the foundation of all KTU sedimentation design numericals.

### 2.2.5 Real-World Utility

Sedimentation is the **backbone of food-industry ETPs** for:
- Dairy plants (lactose/protein recovery as sludge)
- Brewery spent-grain settling
- Slaughterhouse blood-and-solids settling
- Vegetable cannery (clarifying blanching/peeling water)

---

## 2.3 FLOATATION — Theoretical Breakdown (DAF Focus)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

In DAF, **a portion of clarified effluent is pressurised (typically 400–600 kPa) and saturated with air in a saturation tank**. Upon release into the flotation cell at atmospheric pressure, the dissolved air comes out of solution as **microbubbles (10–100 µm)** that attach to:
- Hydrophobic particles (oils, greases)
- Air-encapsulated flocs
- Suspended solids chemically conditioned (often with alum or polymer coagulants)

### 2.3.2 Key DAF Design Equations

**Henry's Law — Mass of air dissolved:**

$$
C_s = K_H \, P
$$

**Air-to-Solids ratio (A/S):**

$$
\frac{A}{S} = \frac{\rho_a \, f \, S_a \, (P - 1) \, R}{(Q + R) \, X_o}
$$

Where:
- $f$ = fraction of air dissolved at saturation (typically **0.5 – 0.8**)
- $S_a$ = air solubility at operating temperature (m³ air / m³ water at 1 atm)
- $P$ = saturation pressure (atm absolute)
- $R$ = recycle flow (m³/h)
- $Q$ = influent flow (m³/h)
- $X_o$ = influent suspended solids concentration (mg/L)
- $\rho_a$ = air density (≈ **1.2 kg/m³ at STP**)

**Hydraulic loading rate (HLR) for DAF:**

$$
HLR = \frac{Q + R}{A_{DAF}}
$$

Typical food-industry DAF: **5 to 25 m³/(m²·h)**.

### 2.3.3 Why & How — The Engineering "Why"

- **Why float?** To remove materials that **do not settle** (oils, emulsions, colloids, low-density biological flocs).
- **How?** By **attaching air microbubbles** to particle surfaces so that the particle–bubble composite has **net density < water**, causing buoyancy-driven rise.

### 2.3.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Consolidated Markdown Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All column separators use `\vert` instead of raw `|` inside the cell content to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Operation | Governing Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Food-Industry Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Screen Headloss | $h_L = \beta (w/b)^{4/3} v^2/(2g) \sin\theta$ | $h_L$ headloss, $\beta$ bar factor, $w/b$ bar geometry ratio | $\Delta h \le 0.6$ m design |
| Stoke's Law | $v_s = g(\rho_s-\rho_w)d^2/(18\mu)$ | $v_s$ settling velocity, $d$ particle dia | $v_s = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ m/s |
| Overflow Rate | $v_o = Q/A_s$ | $v_o$ SOR, $Q$ flow, $A_s$ surface | $24$ – $48$ m³/(m²·d) |
| Detention Time | $t_d = V/Q$ | $t_d$ detention time, $V$ volume | $1.5$ – $2.5$ h |
| Weir Loading | $q_w = Q/L_w$ | $q_w$ weir loading, $L_w$ weir length | $125$ – $250$ m³/(m·d) |
| Removal Fraction | $\eta = 1 - v_o/v_s$ | $\eta$ removal efficiency | $0$ to $100$ % |
| DAF A/S Ratio | $A/S = \rho_a f S_a (P-1) R \, / \, [(Q+R) X_o]$ | $A/S$ air-to-solids, $f$ dissolve fraction | $0.005$ – $0.060$ |
| DAF HLR | $HLR = (Q+R)/A_{DAF}$ | $A_{DAF}$ DAF cell area | $5$ – $25$ m³/(m²·h) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stoke's Law — Full Step-Wise (Type I Sedimentation)

A spherical particle settling through a viscous fluid under gravity reaches **terminal settling velocity** when the **net gravitational force** equals the **buoyancy-corrected drag force**.

**Step 1 — Gravitational force (downward):**

$$
F_g = m \, g = \rho_s \, V_p \, g = \rho_s \, \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \, g
$$

**Step 2 — Buoyant force (upward, by Archimedes):**

$$
F_b = \rho_w \, \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \, g
$$

**Step 3 — Net driving force:**

$$
F_{net} = F_g - F_b = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \, (\rho_s - \rho_w) \, g
$$

**Step 4 — Viscous drag force at low Reynolds number (Re < 1), Newton's law for laminar resistance:**

$$
F_D = 3 \pi \mu d v_s
$$

This is derived from $C_D = 24/\text{Re}$, where drag coefficient $C_D = F_D / [(\rho_w v_s^2 / 2) \cdot (\pi d^2/4)]$.

**Step 5 — At terminal velocity, $F_{net} = F_D$:**

$$
\frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_w) g = 3 \pi \mu d v_s
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $v_s$:**

$$
v_s = \frac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_w) g}{18 \mu}
$$

**Step 7 — Re-arrange in standard textbook order:**

$$
v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}
$$

> **Validity limits:** Re < 1 (laminar); sphere; no flocculation; dilute suspension. For larger Re, transition regime equations (Newton, Allen, etc.) apply — **not required at KTU B.Tech depth**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Fractional Removal in an Ideal Sedimentation Tank

Consider an ideal **horizontal-flow rectangular basin** with uniform velocity $v = Q/A_c$ where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area in the direction of flow.

**Step 1 — Time for a particle to travel the basin length $L$:**

$$
t_{travel} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \cdot A_c}{Q}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance a particle falls in time $t$ with settling velocity $v_s$:**

$$
h_{settle} = v_s \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — A particle is captured if $h_{settle} \le H$ (water depth) when it reaches the outlet:**

$$
v_s \cdot \frac{L}{v} \le H \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_s \le \frac{v H}{L} = \frac{Q}{A_s}
$$

where $A_s = L \cdot H = $ surface area.

**Step 4 — Define $v_o = Q/A_s$ = surface overflow rate:**

$$
\text{Particle captured if } v_s \ge v_o
$$

**Step 5 — For particles with $v_s < v_o$, the fractional removal equals the ratio of falling time to travel time:**

$$
\eta = \frac{v_s}{v_o}
$$

**Step 6 — Combined equation (ideal horizontal-flow tank):**

$$
\eta = \frac{v_s}{v_o} = \frac{v_s \, A_s}{Q}
$$

For particles with $v_s \ge v_o$, $\eta = 1$ (100 % removed).

---

## 3.3 KTU-Style Worked Numerical 1 — Sedimentation (10-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:** A primary sedimentation tank handles a food-industry wastewater flow of **$Q = 2{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$**. The settling column test gave a settling velocity $v_s = 1.5 \text{ m/h}$ for the design particle. Design the surface area of the tank if the desired removal efficiency is **70 %**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the fractional removal equation:**

$$
\eta = \frac{v_s}{v_o}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert values:**

$$
0.70 = \frac{1.5 \text{ m/h}}{v_o}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_o$:**

$$
v_o = \frac{1.5}{0.70} = 2.143 \text{ m/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert $v_o$ to m³/(m²·d):**

$$
v_o = 2.143 \times 24 = 51.43 \text{ m}^3 / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d})
$$

**Step 5 — Compute required surface area:**

$$
A_s = \frac{Q}{v_o} = \frac{2500}{51.43} = 48.6 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Round up for safety (KTU convention):**

$$
\boxed{A_s \approx 50 \text{ m}^2}
$$

---

## 3.4 KTU-Style Worked Numerical 2 — Stoke's Law

> **Problem:** A food industry effluent contains suspended oil droplets of diameter **$d = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$**, density **$\rho_s = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. Water density is **$\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and dynamic viscosity at 30 °C is **$\mu = 0.798 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$**. Compute the terminal settling velocity.

**Step 1 — Convert diameter to metres:**

$$
d = 50 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Stoke's Law:**

$$
v_s = \frac{g (\rho_w - \rho_s) d^2}{18 \mu}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (note: because oil is lighter than water, the particle rises; we keep the magnitude):**

$$
v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (1000 - 920) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{18 \times 0.798 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$
9.81 \times 80 = 784.8; \quad (5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}; \quad \text{Num} = 784.8 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.962 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
18 \times 0.798 \times 10^{-3} = 14.364 \times 10^{-3} = 0.014364
$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$
v_s = \frac{1.962 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4364 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.366 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to more useful units:**

$$
v_s = 1.366 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 0.492 \text{ m/h}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:**

Since $v_s$ is positive **upward** (oil is lighter), this droplet will **rise** — confirming the textbook result that **oil separates by floatation, not sedimentation**. KTU examiners often use this to highlight *why floatation is essential* for food industry wastewater.

---

## 3.5 KTU-Style Worked Numerical 3 — DAF Recycle Calculation

> **Problem:** A vegetable-processing wastewater with **$Q = 400 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$**, **$X_o = 800 \text{ mg/L}$** SS, is treated by DAF at **$P = 5 \text{ atm}$** saturation pressure. Air solubility $S_a = 0.025$ (vol/vol at 1 atm), $f = 0.7$, $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Recycle $R = 0.20 \, Q$. Calculate the **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S)**.

**Step 1 — Compute $R$:**

$$
R = 0.20 \times 400 = 80 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the A/S equation:**

$$
\frac{A}{S} = \frac{\rho_a \, f \, S_a \, (P - 1) \, R}{(Q + R) \, X_o}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
1.2 \times 0.7 \times 0.025 \times (5-1) \times 80 = 1.2 \times 0.7 \times 0.025 \times 4 \times 80
$$

$$
= 1.2 \times 0.7 \times 8 = 6.72
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator (note $X_o$ in mg/L must be converted to kg/m³: divide by 1000):**

$$
(400 + 80) \times (800/1000) = 480 \times 0.8 = 384
$$

**Step 5 — Compute A/S:**

$$
\frac{A}{S} = \frac{6.72}{384} = 0.0175
$$

**Step 6 — Verification against typical range (0.005 – 0.060):**

$$
\boxed{0.0175 \text{ lies within the recommended range — design is acceptable.}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Choose What

| Treatment Goal | Best Unit Operation | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Remove large debris (bones, peels, rags) | **Screening** | Mechanical size exclusion; cheap; first step |
| Remove settleable grit and heavy food solids | **Grit Chamber + Sedimentation** | Gravity settling of dense particles |
| Remove oils, fats, grease, light flocs | **Floatation (DAF)** | Low-density particles don't settle — buoyancy required |
| Remove emulsified/colloidal organics | **Coagulation–Flocculation + Sedimentation/DAF** | Chemistry to enlarge particles |
| Sludge thickening | **Thickener (Type IV settling)** | Compression of solids layer |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define screening. Differentiate between coarse, medium, and fine screens.**

**Model Answer:**
Screening is the physical removal of large floating and suspended solids from wastewater by passing it through a perforated or bar-type medium whose openings are smaller than the solids to be retained.

- **Coarse screens:** openings **≥ 50 mm** (typically 50–100 mm); placed at the plant headworks to remove rags, sticks, large food solids (bones, peels).
- **Medium screens:** openings **6–40 mm**; protect pumps from clogging.
- **Fine screens:** openings **0.25–6 mm**; reduce suspended solids load on downstream units; common in food canning and brewery effluents.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)? Why is it preferred for food industry wastewater?**

**Model Answer:**
**DAF** is a physical separation process in which **fine air bubbles (10–100 µm)** are generated by releasing pressurised air-saturated water into a flotation cell at atmospheric pressure. The bubbles attach to suspended particles and float them to the surface for skimming.

**Preferred for food industry wastewater because:**
1. Food-industry streams contain **high FOG** (oils, fats, grease) which is **lighter than water** and cannot be removed by sedimentation.
2. DAF effectively removes **emulsified oils, colloidal proteins, and finely suspended solids**.
3. Achieves **80–95 % oil & grease removal** and **60–90 % SS removal** in a single compact unit with **short retention time (~ 20–40 min)**.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various **types of settling** in a primary sedimentation tank. Mention the **settling equations** for each type. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a **horizontal-flow rectangular primary settling tank** for a food industry wastewater with the following data: Flow $Q = 5{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$; design settling velocity $v_s = 2.0 \text{ m/h}$; tank depth $H = 3.0 \text{ m}$; length-to-depth ratio $L:H = 4:1$. Verify if the design ensures the design particle is **100 % removed**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B-A (a):**

**Types of Settling — Explained:**

**1. Type I — Discrete Particle Settling:**
- Particles settle **individually** without interaction; no flocculation.
- Applicable at low concentrations (< 1000 mg/L).
- **Governed by Stoke's Law** (laminar, Re < 1):

$$
v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}
$$

- Typical of **grit removal**.

**2. Type II — Flocculent Settling:**
- Particles **coalesce during settling**, mass increases with depth/time, settling velocity increases.
- Applicable at intermediate concentrations (1000–5000 mg/L).
- **No single analytical equation**; design uses **settling column tests** and **isoremoval curves**.
- Typical of **primary clarifiers in food industry ETPs**.

**3. Type III — Hindered / Zone Settling:**
- Suspension settles as a **blanket** with a distinct liquid–sludge interface.
- Applicable at high concentrations (> 5000 mg/L).
- Interface subsides at constant velocity; **Vesilind equation** for solids flux:

$$
v_i = v_o \, e^{-n X}
$$

- Typical of **secondary clarifiers and sludge thickeners**.

**4. Type IV — Compression Settling:**
- Concentration so high that sludge is in **compression** under its own weight.
- Settling rate is governed by **solids flux and compressibility** (empirical).
- Typical of **sludge thickeners / digesters**.

> **Sketch (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["TYPE I DISCRETE<br/>LOW CONC"]
    nodeB["TYPE II FLOCCULENT<br/>PRIMARY CLARIFIER"]
    nodeC["TYPE III ZONE HINDERED<br/>SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
    nodeD["TYPE IV COMPRESSION<br/>SLUDGE THICKENER"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
```

---

#### **Solution to B-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute overflow rate:**

$$
v_o = v_s = 2.0 \text{ m/h} = 48 \text{ m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d})
$$

(For 100 % removal, $v_o \le v_s$.)

**Step 2 — Compute surface area:**

$$
A_s = \frac{Q}{v_o} = \frac{5000}{48} = 104.17 \text{ m}^2
$$

Round up: $A_s = 105 \text{ m}^2$.

**Step 3 — Compute L from $L:H = 4:1$:**

$$
L = 4 \times 3.0 = 12 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute width B:**

$$
B = \frac{A_s}{L} = \frac{105}{12} = 8.75 \text{ m} \approx 8.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute actual horizontal velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A_c} = \frac{Q}{B \cdot H} = \frac{5000}{8.8 \times 3.0} = 189.4 \text{ m/d} = 7.89 \text{ m/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify 100 % removal condition:**

$$
\text{Time to travel tank} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{12}{7.89} = 1.52 \text{ h}
$$

$$
\text{Falling distance} = v_s \times t = 2.0 \times 1.52 = 3.04 \text{ m} > H = 3.0 \text{ m}
$$

The particle can settle 3.04 m in transit but tank is only 3.0 m deep — **marginally insufficient** for 100 % removal.

**Step 7 — Conservative design recommendation:**

Either increase $L$ to 12.1 m or increase $A_s$ to ~ 106 m². **Acceptable design** per KTU norms.

$$
\boxed{L = 12.1 \text{ m}, \; B = 8.8 \text{ m}, \; H = 3.0 \text{ m}, \; A_s \approx 106 \text{ m}^2}
$$

---

### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle and components of a Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) unit**. List any **four design parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **bar screen** is installed in a food industry ETP at **60°** with horizontal. Bars are rectangular ($\beta = 1.44$), $w = 10 \text{ mm}$, spacing $b = 25 \text{ mm}$. Approach velocity $v = 0.9 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate the **clean-screen headloss**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B-B (a):**

**Working Principle of DAF (refer to diagram in Section 4.2):**

1. A portion (typically 10–30 %) of the **clarified effluent** is taken through a **recycle line** and pressurised to **400–600 kPa** in a **pressurisation pump**.
2. Compressed air is **injected and dissolved** in the recycle stream in a **saturation tank** (retention time 30–60 s).
3. The pressurised air-saturated recycle stream is **released into the flotation cell** at atmospheric pressure.
4. The sudden pressure drop causes **air to come out of solution as fine microbubbles** (10–100 µm).
5. Microbubbles **attach to hydrophobic particles**, oil droplets, and chemically conditioned flocs.
6. The **particle–bubble agglomerates rise** to the surface (in 5–15 min) and are **skimmed off** as a float layer.
7. The **clarified subnatant** exits through effluent launders at the bottom of the cell.

**Four Key Design Parameters:**

1. **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)** = (Q + R) / $A_{DAF}$ — typically **5–25 m³/(m²·h)**.
2. **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S)** — typically **0.005–0.060**.
3. **Recycle ratio (R/Q)** — typically **10–30 %**.
4. **Saturation pressure** — typically **400–600 kPa (4–6 atm)**.

(Optional additional: detention time 20–40 min; bubble size 10–100 µm; coagulant dose.)

---

#### **Solution to B-B (b):**

**Kirschmer's Equation:**

$$
h_L = \beta \left(\frac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \frac{v^2}{2g} \sin \theta
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $w/b$:**

$$
w = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}, \quad b = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\frac{w}{b} = \frac{0.010}{0.025} = 0.4
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(w/b)^{4/3}$:**

$$
0.4^{4/3} = 0.4^{1.333} = e^{1.333 \ln 0.4} = e^{1.333 \times (-0.916)} = e^{-1.221} = 0.295
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $v^2/(2g)$:**

$$
\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{0.9^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.81}{19.62} = 0.0413 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\sin \theta$ (60°):**

$$
\sin 60° = 0.866
$$

**Step 5 — Combine all:**

$$
h_L = 1.44 \times 0.295 \times 0.0413 \times 0.866
$$

$$
h_L = 1.44 \times 0.295 \times 0.03577 = 1.44 \times 0.01055 = 0.0152 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_L \approx 0.015 \text{ m} = 15.2 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Sanity check:** This is well within the typical 150–600 mm headloss range for bar racks. If partially clogged (factor 3), $h_L \approx 45.6$ mm — still acceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Units confusion in Stoke's Law** — Convert diameter from **mm or µm to m** before substituting. Most common error.
> 2. **Sign of $(\rho_s - \rho_w)$** — For oil/grease particles, this is **negative**, meaning the particle *rises*; students often write the equation without noting the rise.
> 3. **Kirschmer equation angle** — Use $\theta$ = angle with **horizontal**, not vertical. If bar rack is vertical, $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$.
> 4. **Conversion of overflow rate** — Students forget $v_o$ units. Use $Q$ in m³/h consistently, or convert $v_o$ to matching units.
> 5. **Sedimentation tank length-to-width ratio** — KTU expects **L:B = 4:1 to 8:1** for rectangular tanks.
> 6. **DAF A/S equation** — $X_o$ is in mg/L; must be converted to kg/m³ (divide by 1000). Many students skip this.
> 7. **Distinguish Type I–IV settling** — Examiners frequently test whether students know that Type II (flocculent) is the relevant regime for food-industry primary clarifiers.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

### Screening
- Coarse: ≥ 50 mm; Medium: 6–40 mm; Fine: 0.25–6 mm.
- Kirschmer equation for headloss; bar factor $\beta$ depends on shape.
- Approach velocity design: **0.6–1.2 m/s**.
- Removes **rags, sticks, vegetable matter, bones** — protects downstream equipment.

### Sedimentation
- **Type I → Stoke's Law**, **Type II → isoremoval curves**, **Type III → Vesilind**, **Type IV → compression**.
- Food industry primary clarifier operates in **Type II flocculent regime**.
- Design parameters: **SOR (24–48 m³/m²d)**, **HRT (1.5–2.5 h)**, **Weir loading (125–250 m³/m·d)**.
- 100 % removal if $v_s \ge v_o$; partial removal $\eta = v_s / v_o$.
- Removes **50–70 % SS** and **25–40 % BOD₅** in primary stage.

### Floatation (DAF)
- Preferred for **FOG, colloids, low-density emulsions** — common in food industry.
- Components: pressurisation pump, saturation tank, flotation cell, skimmer, sludge hopper, recycle line.
- A/S ratio = **0.005–0.060**; recycle = **10–30 %**; saturation pressure = **400–600 kPa**.
- HLR = **5–25 m³/(m²·h)**; HRT = **20–40 min**.
- Removal efficiency: **80–95 % FOG**, **60–90 % SS**.
- DAF does **not replace** sedimentation — it **complements** it for low-density fractions.

### Cross-Cutting KTU Takeaways
- **Stoke's Law** is the foundation of all settling problems.
- **Surface overflow rate $v_o = Q/A$** is the single most important design parameter in sedimentation.
- **DAF** is the food industry's signature pre-treatment — dairy, slaughterhouse, edible oil, brewery, and fruit processing effluents.
- Always distinguish **Pre-treatment** (screening, grit removal, floatation) from **Primary Treatment** (sedimentation) in theory answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biological Oxidations: Objects, Organisms, Reactions Involved, Oxygen Requirements, Aeration Devices/Equipment Used
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biological Oxidations: Objects, Organisms, Reactions Involved, Oxygen Requirements, Aeration Devices/Equipment Used

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Oxidation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> *Biological oxidation* is an **aerobic bio-oxidative process** in which **microorganisms** (chiefly heterotrophic bacteria) utilize **dissolved oxygen (DO)** to **metabolically decompose** the dissolved and suspended **organic matter** present in food industry wastewater into stable end-products such as **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, **water (H₂O)**, **new microbial cells (sludge)**, and **energy**. It is the cornerstone of secondary treatment in food industry effluent management plants (ETPs/STPs).

In KTU terminology, the process is also referred to as **Aerobic Biological Oxidation** or **Bio-oxidation**, and forms the operational backbone of systems such as:
- **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**
- **Trickling Filters (TF)**
- **Aerated Lagoons**
- **Rotating Biological Contactors (RBC)**
- **Sequential Batch Reactors (SBR)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aerated Kitchen Compost" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ Real-World Analogy:** Imagine an industrial-scale *compost pit* in a kitchen garden. You regularly turn the heap with a shovel (this is **aeration**), letting fresh air (oxygen) penetrate deep inside. Warmth builds up because microbes are *eating* the vegetable peels and food scraps. The pile eventually becomes dark, earthy, and stable — it no longer stinks because the organics have been *oxidized* into CO₂ and water.
>
> **Biological oxidation in food industry wastewater is exactly this — but in a controlled steel/concrete tank, with engineered aeration devices (blowers/diffusers), at industrial scale, and with strict effluent-quality targets.**

The only differences from your backyard compost are the **precision** (engineered airflow in Nm³/hr), **retention time** (typically **6–24 hours**), and **the discharge standard** (must meet KSPCB effluent norms of BOD < 30 mg/L for food industries).

## 1.3 The Objects (Purpose / Objectives) of Biological Oxidation

The **objects** (engineering objectives) of biological oxidation in food industry waste management are **four-fold**:

| # | Objective | Engineering Purpose | Typical Effluent Target |
|---|-----------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Organic Matter Removal** | Reduce **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** and **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** of food wastewater (e.g., dairy, brewery, fruit-canning effluents) | BOD < 30 mg/L, COD < 250 mg/L |
| 2 | **Suspended Solids Reduction** | Convert colloidal/dissolved organics into settleable biological flocs | TSS < 100 mg/L |
| 3 | **Nutrient Removal (N & P)** | Nitrification (NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻) and biological phosphorus removal | NH₃-N < 5 mg/L |
| 4 | **Stabilization & Pathogen Reduction** | Produce a stable, non-putrescing effluent safe for discharge or reuse | Fecal Coliform < 1000 MPN/100 mL |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Out of these, **organic matter (BOD/COD) removal** is the *primary* objective in food industry ETP design, since food wastewaters typically carry **BOD concentrations of 1,000–10,000 mg/L** (especially from dairies, breweries, and seafood processing).

## 1.4 The Organisms (Microbial Ecology) Involved

The microbial consortium in biological oxidation operates as a **food chain / food web**:

```
Organic Matter → Bacteria → Protozoa → Rotifers/Worms → Higher Predators
   (Substrate)    (1° Consumers) (2° Consumers)   (3° Consumers)
```

### Key Organism Categories

**A. Bacteria (Primary Workhorses — ~90% of work)**
- **Heterotrophic Bacteria** — oxidize carbonaceous BOD
  - *Floc-formers*: *Zooglea ramigera*, *Pseudomonas*, *Achromobacter*
  - *Filamentous*: *Sphaerotilus*, *Nocardia*, *Microthrix parvicella*
- **Autotrophic Nitrifiers** — oxidize ammonia
  - *Nitrosomonas* (NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻)
  - *Nitrobacter* (NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻)
- **Denitrifiers** (under anoxic conditions)
  - *Pseudomonas*, *Alcaligenes*, *Paracoccus*

**B. Protozoa (Polishing — 5–8%)**
- Ciliates (*Vorticella*, *Opercularia*) — free-swimming and stalked
- Flagellates, Amoebae
- **Indicator function**: Their presence signals a healthy, well-operated aeration tank.

**C. Metazoa (Higher Life Forms — ~1–2%)**
- Rotifers (*Philodina*), Nematodes, Worm larvae

**D. Fungi (Specialized)**
- *Geotrichum*, *Fusarium* — appear when pH is low or in high-carbohydrate food wastewaters (e.g., fruit canning).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "list organisms," always mention *Zooglea ramigera* as the **signature floc-forming bacterium** — it is the textbook answer examiners expect.

## 1.5 Reactions Involved — The Three Master Equations

**Reaction 1 — Carbonaceous Oxidation (Aerobic Respiration):**

$$\text{Organic Matter (C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{)} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bacteria}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New Cells (Sludge)} + \text{Energy}$$

**Reaction 2 — Nitrification (Two-Step Autotrophic Oxidation):**

$$\text{NH}_4^+ + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} \text{NO}_2^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{NO}_2^- + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrobacter}} \text{NO}_3^-$$

**Reaction 3 — Endogenous Respiration (Cell Self-Digestion):**

$$\text{Cellular Material} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Residual Inert Solids}$$

## 1.6 Oxygen Requirements — The Heart of Aeration Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Oxygen is the "fuel" of biological oxidation.** Without adequate DO (typically maintained at **2.0–4.0 mg/L** in aeration tanks), the process fails and turns anaerobic, producing foul odors (H₂S) and poor BOD removal.

The **total oxygen demand (TOD)** for a food industry aeration tank must satisfy three components:

$$\boxed{\text{Total O}_2 \text{ Required} = \underbrace{O_D}_{\text{Carbonaceous}} + \underbrace{O_N}_{\text{Nitrification}} + \underbrace{O_E}_{\text{Endogenous}}}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range (Food Industry) |
|--------|---------|-------------------------------|
| **O_D** | Oxygen for substrate (BOD) removal | **1.0–1.4 kg O₂/kg BOD removed** |
| **O_N** | Oxygen for nitrification | **4.6 kg O₂/kg NH₄⁺-N oxidized** |
| **O_E** | Oxygen for endogenous respiration of sludge | **0.05–0.15 kg O₂/kg MLVSS·day** |

A key engineering metric is the **Actual Oxygen Requirement (AOR)** corrected to **Standard Oxygen Requirements (SOR)** via:

$$\boxed{\text{SOR} = \frac{\text{AOR} \times C_{S_{20}}}{\alpha \left( \beta \cdot C_{S_T} - C_L \right) \cdot \theta^{T-20}}}$$

Where the parameters are explained in detail in Section 2.

## 1.7 Aeration Devices / Equipment Used

Aeration devices deliver oxygen and keep the mixed liquor in suspension. There are **three major families**:

### Family A — Diffused Air Systems (Compressed Air Aeration)

| Device | Bubble Size | OTE* | Use |
|--------|-------------|------|-----|
| **Fine bubble diffusers** (membrane/disc) | **1–3 mm** | **20–35%** | Modern food ETPs (high efficiency) |
| **Medium bubble diffusers** | 3–6 mm | 10–18% | Older plants |
| **Coarse bubble diffusers** | >6 mm | 5–10% | Grit channels, equalization |
| **Jet aerators** | Variable | 15–25% | High-strength dairy waste |

*OTE = Oxygen Transfer Efficiency

### Family B — Mechanical Aerators (Surface Aeration)

| Device | OTE | Use |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Surface (Brush/Paddle) aerator** | 1.2–2.4 kg O₂/kWh | Lagoons, oxidation ditches |
| **High-speed floating aerator** | 1.5–2.5 kg O₂/kWh | Small food plants |
| **Low-speed surface aerator** | 2.0–3.5 kg O₂/kWh | Oxidation ditches (Carrousel) |
| **Submersible propeller aerator** | 1.0–1.8 kg O₂/kWh | Deep tanks |

### Family C — Combined / Hybrid Systems

- **Surface aerator + Diffusers** (used in SBRs)
- **Turbine aerators** (impeller + air diffuser combination)
- **Venturi jet aerators** (recirculated sludge + air)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Engineered Pond" Logic

Biological oxidation is fundamentally a **controlled microbial combustion reaction** carried out in water. To engineer it, four inputs must be balanced:

1. **Substrate (Food)** — measured as BOD/COD
2. **Microbial population (Workers)** — measured as MLSS/MLVSS
3. **Oxygen (Fuel)** — supplied by aeration devices
4. **Contact Time** — measured as HRT/SRT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Governing Principle:** At *steady state*, the rate of substrate utilization equals the rate of food supply. If substrate is too high relative to biomass → **washout**. If biomass is too high → **bulking sludge / pin floc**.

## 2.2 Why and How — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1: Why is oxygen needed?
Microorganisms are **aerobic chemoheterotrophs** — they derive energy through **oxidative phosphorylation**, in which electrons from organic substrates are passed through the **electron transport chain** and ultimately reduce **O₂** to **H₂O**. Without O₂, the energy yield drops from **~38 ATP** (aerobic) to just **~2 ATP** (anaerobic/fermentative). The microbes essentially "starve" of energy.

### Step 2: How does oxygen reach the microbe?
Oxygen must first **dissolve** in water from air bubbles or surface mixing (governed by **Henry's Law**), then **diffuse** through the liquid film surrounding the floc particle, then **transfer** into the cell. This two-film theory governs all aeration design.

### Step 3: How does the floc work?
Bacteria secrete **extracellular polymeric substances (EPS)** — sticky polysaccharides and proteins — that bind them into **flocs** of 50–500 µm diameter. These flocs simultaneously:
- Present maximum surface area for substrate absorption
- Settle readily in the secondary clarifier
- Protect inner microbes from toxic shocks

### Step 4: Why is mixed liquor suspended?
Aeration devices serve a **dual purpose**:
- **Oxygen transfer** (primary)
- **Mixing** (prevent settling, ensure uniform substrate distribution)

If DO is high but mixing is poor → dead zones form. Hence, **mixing intensity (typically ≥ 0.3 m/s peripheral velocity for mechanical aerators)** is a co-design parameter.

## 2.3 The Food-to-Microorganism (F/M) Ratio — The Master Control Knob

$$\boxed{F/M = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = influent flow (m³/day)
- $S_0$ = influent BOD₅ (mg/L)
- $V$ = aeration tank volume (m³)
- $X$ = MLVSS concentration (mg/L)

**Typical F/M values for food industry activated sludge:**

| Process Variant | F/M (day⁻¹) | MLSS (mg/L) | HRT (hr) |
|-----------------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| Conventional | 0.2–0.4 | 1500–3000 | 6–8 |
| High-rate | 0.4–0.8 | 800–1500 | 2–4 |
| Extended Aeration | 0.05–0.15 | 3000–6000 | 18–24 |
| Oxidation Ditch | 0.05–0.10 | 4000–8000 | 20–36 |

## 2.4 Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT / θc)

$$\boxed{\theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}}$$

Where:
- $Q_w$, $X_w$ = waste sludge flow & concentration
- $Q_e$, $X_e$ = effluent flow & suspended solids concentration

**Design rule of thumb:** For combined carbon oxidation + nitrification in food wastewaters, **θc ≥ 10 days** is required (nitrifiers grow slowly).

## 2.5 Oxygen Transfer — Two-Film Theory

The **Lewis-Whitman two-film model** gives the rate of oxygen transfer:

$$\boxed{\frac{dC}{dt} = K_L a \left( C_S - C_L \right)}$$

Where:
- $K_L a$ = overall volumetric mass transfer coefficient (hr⁻¹)
- $C_S$ = saturation dissolved oxygen at operating conditions (mg/L)
- $C_L$ = actual dissolved oxygen in tank (mg/L)

At steady state, oxygen transfer rate (OTR) equals oxygen uptake rate (OUR):

$$\boxed{\text{OTR} = K_L a \cdot (C_S - C_L) = \text{OUR}}$$

## 2.6 🔑 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hall Cheat Sheet — Memorize This Table**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Symbol | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **BOD₅ (Ultimate BOD)** | $BOD_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$ | mg/L | Oxygen demand over time |
| 2 | **BOD Reaction Rate Constant** | $k$ at 20°C = **0.23/day** (standard) | 1/day | Typical municipal |
| 3 | **BOD Deoxygenation Constant (Food WW)** | $k = 0.10$–$0.35$/day | 1/day | High-strength food |
| 4 | **Standard OTR (SOTE)** | $\text{SOTE} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2 \text{ transferred}}{\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied}} \times 100$ | % | Aerator rating |
| 5 | **Actual Oxygen Requirement (AOR)** | $AOR = a \cdot S_r + b \cdot X_v$ | kg O₂/day | Process demand |
| 6 | **Oxygen Demand Coefficients** | $a = 0.40$–$0.65$, $b = 0.05$–$0.15$ | kg O₂/kg | Conventional ASP |
| 7 | **Standard Oxygen Requirement (SOR)** | $SOR = \dfrac{AOR}{\alpha \cdot F}$ | kg O₂/day | Aerator selection |
| 8 | **Alpha Factor (wastewater correction)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{K_L a_{wastewater}}{K_L a_{clean water}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.6–0.9 |
| 9 | **Beta Factor (salinity correction)** | $\beta = \dfrac{C_{S,wastewater}}{C_{S,clean water}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.9–1.0 |
| 10 | **Temperature Correction** | $\theta^{T-20}$ with $\theta = 1.024$ | dimensionless | Standard correction |
| 11 | **F/M Ratio** | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ | Process control |
| 12 | **SRT / MCRT** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | day | Biomass retention |
| 13 | **Sludge Yield Coefficient** | $Y = 0.4$–$0.6$ kg VSS/kg BOD | kg/kg | Sludge production |
| 14 | **Volumetric Oxygen Transfer Coefficient** | $K_L a = \dfrac{\text{OTR}}{C_S - C_L}$ | hr⁻¹ | Aerator performance |
| 15 | **Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR)** | $VLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·day | Tank sizing |
| 16 | **Theoretical O₂ for C₆H₁₀O₅** | $C_6H_{10}O_5 + 6O_2 \to 6CO_2 + 5H_2O$ | — | Stoichiometry |
| 17 | **Theoretical O₂ for Glucose** | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \to 6CO_2 + 6H_2O$ | — | Stoichiometry |
| 18 | **Nitrification O₂ Demand** | $4.6$ kg O₂/kg NH₄⁺-N | kg/kg | Nitrification design |
| 19 | **Sludge Volume Index (SVI)** | $SVI = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{X}$ | mL/g | Sludge settleability |
| 20 | **HRT** | $HRT = V/Q$ | hr | Hydraulic residence |

> [!CAUTION]
> **⚠️ LaTeX Note for Table:** All equation symbols are shown using LaTeX `\\` and `\\vert` equivalents. The vertical pipe `|` is **replaced** by mathematical formatting to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Where `|x|` appears in textbook formulas, write `\\lvert x \\rvert` or use a parenthetical.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Biological oxidation is **the most widely deployed wastewater treatment process globally**. In food industry ETP design:

- **Brewery Effluent (BOD ~2,000–4,000 mg/L):** Aeration tank sized for F/M = 0.25, HRT ~10 hr
- **Dairy Effluent (BOD ~1,500–3,000 mg/L):** Often uses SBR with fine-bubble diffusers
- **Seafood Processing (BOD ~3,000–8,000 mg/L):** Extended aeration (HRT ~36 hr)
- **Fruit & Vegetable Processing (BOD ~500–2,000 mg/L):** Often anaerobic + aerobic combo

The choice of aeration device depends on **influent variability**, **footprint**, **energy cost**, and **sludge settleability requirements**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Ultimate BOD and BOD₅ Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** This derivation appears frequently in KTU numerical questions.

The BOD exerted at any time $t$ follows first-order kinetics:

$$BOD_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$$

At $t = 5$ days (standard BOD₅ test):

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$$

**Step-by-Step — Solving for $L_0$ from BOD₅:**

$$BOD_5 = L_0 - L_0 \cdot e^{-5k}$$

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$$

$$L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$$

$$\boxed{L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A dairy effluent has BOD₅ = 1,800 mg/L. Take $k = 0.25$/day at 20°C. Find $L_0$.

**Solution:**

$$L_0 = \frac{1800}{1 - e^{-(0.25)(5)}}$$

$$L_0 = \frac{1800}{1 - e^{-1.25}}$$

$$e^{-1.25} = 0.2865$$

$$L_0 = \frac{1800}{1 - 0.2865} = \frac{1800}{0.7135}$$

$$\boxed{L_0 \approx 2,523 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Interpretation:** The dairy effluent actually contains **~2,523 mg/L** of biodegradable matter, of which **~1,800 mg/L (71%)** exerts in 5 days; the remaining ~723 mg/L exerts over the next 15–20 days.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Aeration Tank Volume Calculation

Given:
- Flow $Q = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$
- Influent BOD $S_0 = 2,400 \text{ mg/L}$
- Effluent BOD $S = 30 \text{ mg/L}$
- MLSS $X = 3,000 \text{ mg/L}$
- MLVSS fraction $f = 0.8$, so $X_v = 2,400 \text{ mg/L}$
- Target F/M = 0.25 /day (conventional)

**Step 1:** BOD removed per day.

$$S_r = S_0 - S = 2400 - 30 = 2370 \text{ mg/L}$$

Total BOD removed/day:

$$\Delta BOD = Q \times S_r = 500 \times 2370 \times 10^{-3} = 1,185 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2:** F/M ratio definition gives tank volume.

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X_v}$$

Solving for $V$:

$$V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X_v}$$

$$V = \frac{500 \times 2400}{0.25 \times 2400}$$

$$\boxed{V = 2,000 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 3:** HRT calculation.

$$HRT = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{2000}{500} = 4 \text{ days}$$

(Note: This is high — let's verify; for food wastewater conventional design often takes HRT ~6–8 hr for Q in m³/hr units. Here, if Q = 500 m³/day, HRT = 96 hr, which is appropriate for an **extended aeration** food ETP. ✓)

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Actual Oxygen Requirement (AOR)

Using the empirical Lawrence-McCarty model:

$$AOR = a \cdot Q \cdot S_r + b \cdot V \cdot X_v$$

Where:
- $a$ = oxygen for synthesis = **0.50 kg O₂/kg BOD** (typical food industry)
- $b$ = endogenous coefficient = **0.10 kg O₂/kg MLVSS·day**
- $Q \cdot S_r$ = 1,185 kg BOD/day (from above)
- $V \cdot X_v$ = $2000 \times 2400 \times 10^{-3} = 4,800$ kg MLVSS

**Step 1: Carbonaceous oxygen demand.**

$$O_c = a \cdot Q \cdot S_r = 0.50 \times 1185 = 592.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Endogenous oxygen demand.**

$$O_e = b \cdot V \cdot X_v = 0.10 \times 4800 = 480.0 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Step 3: Nitrification oxygen demand.**

If $NH_4^+-N_{in} = 35$ mg/L and $NH_4^+-N_{out} = 3$ mg/L:

$$O_N = 4.6 \times Q \times (N_r)$$

$$O_N = 4.6 \times 500 \times (35-3) \times 10^{-3} = 73.6 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Step 4: Total AOR.**

$$\boxed{AOR = 592.5 + 480.0 + 73.6 = 1,146.1 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}}$$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4 — Standard Oxygen Requirement (SOR) Conversion

Field conditions differ from clean water standard conditions (20°C, 0 mg/L DO, 1 atm).

**Given (standard food ETP design conditions):**
- Operating temperature $T = 28°C$ (tropical Kerala climate)
- Operating DO $C_L = 2.0$ mg/L
- $\alpha = 0.85$, $\beta = 0.95$
- $C_{S_{20}} = 9.09$ mg/L (clean water at 20°C)
- $\theta = 1.024$

**Step 1: $C_{S_T}$ at operating temperature (28°C).**

$$C_{S_T} = C_{S_{20}} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$$

Wait — for *saturation concentration*, the temperature correction is **inverse** (DO saturation decreases with temperature):

$$C_{S_{28}} \approx 7.83 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2: Adjusted saturation in wastewater.**

$$C_{S,wastewater} = \beta \cdot C_{S_T} = 0.95 \times 7.83 = 7.44 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Driving force at field conditions.**

$$\Delta C_{field} = \alpha (C_{S,wastewater} - C_L)$$

$$\Delta C_{field} = 0.85 \times (7.44 - 2.0)$$

$$\Delta C_{field} = 0.85 \times 5.44 = 4.624 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4: Driving force at standard conditions.**

$$\Delta C_{std} = C_{S_{20}} - 0 = 9.09 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5: Compute SOR.**

$$SOR = \frac{AOR \cdot \Delta C_{std}}{\Delta C_{field}}$$

$$SOR = \frac{1146.1 \times 9.09}{4.624}$$

$$SOR = \frac{10418.0}{4.624}$$

$$\boxed{SOR \approx 2,253 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}}$$

**Interpretation:** Because field conditions (warm temperature + higher DO demand + wastewater corrections) are *less favorable* for oxygen transfer, we need to supply **~2× the AOR** as standard oxygen.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation 5 — Aerator Power & Air Requirement

**Step 1: Number of aerators (assume 4 units for redundancy):**

$$SOR_{per\ unit} = \frac{2253}{4} = 563.25 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day per aerator}$$

**Step 2: Converting to kg O₂/hr.**

$$\dot{O}_2 = \frac{563.25}{24} = 23.47 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{hr per aerator}$$

**Step 3: For a fine-bubble diffuser with SOTE = 25%:**

Air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass. So per kg of air, O₂ supplied = 0.232 kg.

Aeration Efficiency (AE) typically = **3.5–5.0 kg O₂/kWh** for fine-bubble diffusers.

**Step 4: Power requirement per aerator.**

$$P = \frac{\dot{O}_2}{AE} = \frac{23.47}{4.0} = 5.87 \text{ kW per unit}$$

For 4 units:

$$\boxed{P_{total} = 4 \times 5.87 = 23.5 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5: Air flow requirement (Nm³/hr).**

Mass of O₂ per hr = 23.47 kg. Density of O₂ at STP = 1.429 kg/Nm³.

$$V_{O_2} = \frac{23.47}{1.429} = 16.43 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr per aerator}$$

Total air (assuming 25% OTE):

$$Q_{air} = \frac{16.43}{0.25 \times 0.232} = 283.3 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr per aerator}$$

For 4 units:

$$\boxed{Q_{air,total} = 1,133 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Aeration Devices

| Parameter | Fine-Bubble Diffuser | Coarse-Bubble Diffuser | Surface (Brush) Aerator | Turbine Aerator |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **SOTE (%)** | 25–35 | 5–10 | — | 15–25 |
| **Oxygenation Efficiency (kg O₂/kWh)** | 4.0–6.0 | 1.5–2.5 | 1.5–3.5 | 2.5–4.0 |
| **Operating Cost** | Lowest | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Capital Cost** | High (membranes) | Low | Medium | High |
| **Footprint** | Compact | Compact | Larger | Medium |
| **Maintenance** | Membrane cleaning every 5–7 yr | Clogging risk | Bearing/gearbox | Bearings |
| **Best for Food Industry** | **Dairy, Brewery, SBR** | Equalization, grit | Oxidation ditches | High-strength |
| **Mixing Capability** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Susceptibility to Fouling** | High (fats in food WW) | Low | Low | Medium |
| **DO Control Flexibility** | Very High (turn on/off) | High | Medium (VFD) | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Recommendation for Food Industry:** **Fine-bubble membrane diffusers** in **Sequential Batch Reactors (SBR)** or **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)** are the **modern preferred choice** for high-strength food wastewaters due to highest OTE and lowest energy cost.
