> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Wastewater Treatment Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advanced Wastewater Treatment Systems (AWTS)** refers to the suite of physico-chemical, biological, and membrane-based unit operations employed *after* conventional primary and secondary treatment to remove residual suspended solids, dissolved organics, nutrients (nitrogen and phosphorus), pathogens, heavy metals, and recalcitrant compounds to meet stringent discharge or reuse standards set by regulatory authorities such as the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and the **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)**.

In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management (OEFTT834)**, AWTS is critical because food processing effluents (dairy, meat, brewery, fruit processing, seafood, edible oil refineries) contain **high BOD/COD (often > 2000 mg/L)**, **high Total Suspended Solids (TSS)**, **high Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)**, **fats/oils/greases (FOG)**, **colour**, and **variable pH** that cannot be adequately handled by conventional activated sludge alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Advanced wastewater treatment encompasses **Tertiary Treatment (Tertiary = polishing stage)**, **Membrane Separation Technologies**, **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)**, **Adsorption**, and **Disinfection**. These systems are mandatory for food industries aiming for **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** or effluent reuse in gardening/cooling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Coffee Filter" Analogy:**
> Imagine making coffee. The primary treatment is the *first filter* that removes the big grounds. The secondary treatment (biological) is like *letting the coffee sit and let microbes "eat" the dissolved coffee compounds*. But the water is still slightly yellowish and has tiny dissolved substances. **Advanced Treatment** is the *final activated-carbon filter + UV sterilizer* you attach to your water purifier — it polishes the water to drinking quality. Similarly, in food industry wastewater, advanced treatment takes the already-treated effluent and "polishes" it to a quality that can be discharged into rivers, used for irrigation, or even recycled back into the plant.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

Advanced wastewater treatment can be broadly classified into:

| Category | Examples | Target Pollutant |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Tertiary Physico-Chemical** | Coagulation-Flocculation, Adsorption, Ion Exchange | TSS, colour, residual organics |
| **Membrane Technologies** | MF, UF, NF, RO | Suspended & dissolved solids, salts |
| **Advanced Oxidation (AOPs)** | Ozonation, Fenton, UV/H₂O₂, Photocatalysis | Recalcitrant COD, colour, micropollutants |
| **Biological Polishing** | MBR, SBR, MBBR, Anaerobic Filters | Residual BOD, ammonia, nitrate |
| **Nutrient Removal** | Anoxic-Oxic (A/O), SHARON, ANAMMOX | Nitrogen, Phosphorus |
| **Disinfection** | Chlorination, UV, Ozonation | Pathogens (coliforms) |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Expected)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bench-mark effluent quality after Advanced Treatment of food industry wastewater:**
> - **COD** < **100 mg/L** (typically < 50 mg/L for reuse)
> - **BOD₅** < **10 mg/L**
> - **TSS** < **10 mg/L**
> - **Total Nitrogen (TN)** < **10 mg/L**
> - **Total Phosphorus (TP)** < **2 mg/L**
> - **Fecal Coliforms** < **100 MPN/100 mL** (for irrigation reuse)
> - **pH** between **6.5 – 7.5**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tertiary Treatment — Coagulation–Flocculation

**Principle:** Addition of chemicals (coagulants like **alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O]**, **ferric chloride [FeCl₃]**, **PAC [Poly Aluminium Chloride]**, or biopolymers like **chitosan**) destabilizes colloidal particles by neutralizing their surface charge (Zeta Potential → 0), enabling them to aggregate into larger flocs for sedimentation or flotation.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Rapid Mixing** (G = 300–1000 s⁻¹, time = 30–60 s) — disperses coagulant uniformly.
2. **Slow Mixing / Flocculation** (G = 20–75 s⁻¹, time = 20–40 min) — promotes floc growth.
3. **Sedimentation / Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** — separates flocs.

**Why it matters in food industry:** Removes fine colloids, emulsified oils, proteins, and pigments that survive secondary treatment.

## 2.2 Membrane Separation Technologies

Membranes separate pollutants based on **size exclusion** and **solution-diffusion** mechanisms. The driving force is **transmembrane pressure (TMP)**.

### 2.2.1 Membrane Classification by Pore Size

| Type | Pore Size (nm) | Removes | Typical TMP (bar) |
|------|----------------|---------|-------------------|
| **MF** (Microfiltration) | 100 – 1000 | Bacteria, TSS, Turbidity | 0.1 – 2 |
| **UF** (Ultrafiltration) | 1 – 100 | Macromolecules, Proteins, Viruses | 0.5 – 5 |
| **NF** (Nanofiltration) | 0.1 – 1 | Divalent salts, Colour, Hardness | 5 – 20 |
| **RO** (Reverse Osmosis) | < 0.1 | Monovalent salts, all dissolved solids | 10 – 80 |

### 2.2.2 Membrane Flux Equation (Critical KTU Formula)

The **permeate flux (J)** through a membrane is given by:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

Where:
- $J$ = permeate flux (m³/m²·h or LMH)
- $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa or bar)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of permeate (Pa·s)
- $R_m$ = membrane hydraulic resistance (m⁻¹)

**Modified form for fouling (Darcy's law extended):**

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \left( R_m + R_f \right)}$$

Where $R_f$ is the **fouling resistance**, which increases with operating time due to cake formation, pore blocking, and concentration polarization.

## 2.3 Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)

AOPs generate **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)**, which are extremely powerful, non-selective oxidants (Oxidation potential = **2.80 V**, second only to fluorine).

### 2.3.1 Common AOP Systems

| AOP | Key Reaction | Typical Removal |
|-----|--------------|-----------------|
| **Ozonation (O₃)** | O₃ + organics → CO₂ + H₂O | 50–80% COD, complete colour removal |
| **Fenton Process** | Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + •OH + OH⁻ | 70–95% COD |
| **Photo-Fenton** | Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ + UV → Fe³⁺ + •OH | Up to 99% COD |
| **UV / H₂O₂** | H₂O₂ + hv → 2•OH | 60–85% COD |
| **TiO₂ Photocatalysis** | TiO₂ + hv → e⁻ + h⁺ → •OH | 80–95% micropollutants |
| **Wet Air Oxidation (WAO)** | O₂ + organics (high T,P) → CO₂ | 75–95% COD |

### 2.3.2 Fenton Process Stoichiometry

The Fenton reaction involves two key steps:

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \cdot\text{OH} + \text{OH}^-$$

$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \cdot\text{OOH} + \text{H}^+$$

**Optimum operating conditions for food industry effluents:**
- pH = **2.5 – 3.5** (critical; pH > 4 causes Fe(OH)₃ precipitation)
- [H₂O₂] : [Fe²⁺] ratio = **5 : 1 to 10 : 1 (mass basis)**
- Reaction time = **30 – 90 min**
- Temperature = **30 – 40 °C**

## 2.4 Adsorption

**Activated Carbon Adsorption** is the most common polishing step. The governing equation is the **Freundlich Isotherm** (most common for food effluents) and **Langmuir Isotherm** (for monolayer adsorption).

### 2.4.1 Adsorption Isotherms

**Freundlich Isotherm:**

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

**Langmuir Isotherm:**

$$q_e = \frac{q_m \cdot K_L \cdot C_e}{1 + K_L \cdot C_e}$$

Where:
- $q_e$ = adsorption capacity at equilibrium (mg/g)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration in solution (mg/L)
- $K_F, n$ = Freundlich constants (empirical)
- $q_m$ = maximum monolayer capacity (mg/g)
- $K_L$ = Langmuir constant (L/mg)

## 2.5 Nutrient Removal (Nitrogen & Phosphorus)

### 2.5.1 Nitrification-Denitrification (Traditional)

**Step 1 — Nitrification (Aerobic, autotrophic bacteria like *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter*):**

$$\text{NH}_4^+ + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{NO}_2^- + 0.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^-$$

**Overall:**

$$\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Denitrification (Anoxic, heterotrophic bacteria like *Pseudomonas*):**

$$\text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow$$

**Carbon source needed for denitrification:** Methanol, acetate, or food-industry waste itself (cheese whey, molasses).

### 2.5.2 Biological Phosphorus Removal (Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal – EBPR)

**Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAO)** under alternating anaerobic-aerobic conditions:

$$\text{Carbon source (volatile fatty acids)} \xrightarrow{\text{anaerobic}} \text{PHA stored in PAO}$$

$$\text{PHA} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{aerobic}} \text{Excess PO}_4^{3-} \text{ uptake into poly-P}$$

**Excess sludge wasting removes phosphorus from the system.**

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m}$ | Membrane flux |
| 2 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_m + R_f)}$ | Flux with fouling |
| 3 | $R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_c + R_p$ | Resistance-in-series model |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency |
| 5 | $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | Freundlich isotherm |
| 6 | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$ | Langmuir isotherm |
| 7 | $\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Nitrification stoichiometry |
| 8 | $P_{power} = \dfrac{\gamma Q H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Pumping power (kW) |
| 9 | $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio |
| 10 | $\text{SRT} = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | Solids Retention Time |
| 11 | $\text{HRT} = V / Q$ | Hydraulic Retention Time |
| 12 | $\Delta G^0 = -n F E^0$ | Electrocoagulation (thermodynamic driving force) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** In food industry projects, AOPs combined with membrane systems (e.g., **UF + RO**, **Fenton + MBR**) are the *de-facto standard* for achieving **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)**, especially in **brewery, dairy, and distillery plants** in Kerala and Tamil Nadu, where water scarcity and CPCB effluent regulations are stringent.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Removal Efficiency and Log-Removal for Disinfection

The **% removal efficiency** is the most frequently asked KTU 2024 calculation. For any pollutant:

$$\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**For disinfection, log-removal is preferred:**

$$\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_{in}}{N_{out}}\right)$$

### Worked Example 1 (KTU Exam Standard)

> A dairy industry wastewater treatment plant produces secondary effluent with **COD = 480 mg/L** and **TSS = 95 mg/L**. After tertiary treatment (Fenton + UF), the COD drops to **38 mg/L** and TSS to **2 mg/L**. Calculate (a) COD removal efficiency, (b) TSS removal efficiency, and (c) log-removal of TSS.

**Solution:**

**(a) COD removal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{COD} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{COD} = \frac{480 - 38}{480} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{COD} = \frac{442}{480} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{COD} = 92.08\%}$$

**(b) TSS removal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{TSS} = \frac{95 - 2}{95} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{TSS} = \frac{93}{95} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{TSS} = 97.89\%}$$

**(c) Log-removal of TSS:**

$$\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{95}{2}\right)$$

$$\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}(47.5)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Log Removal} = 1.677 \approx 1.68}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Membrane Area Calculation

The required **membrane area (A)** for a given permeate flow is:

$$A = \frac{Q_p}{J}$$

Where $Q_p$ is the permeate flow rate and $J$ is the flux.

### Worked Example 2 (KTU Exam Standard)

> An RO unit in a fruit-processing plant must produce **Q_p = 50 m³/day** of reusable water. The membrane flux is **J = 15 LMH** (liters per m² per hour). Calculate the required membrane area.

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$Q_p = 50 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day} = \frac{50 \times 1000}{24} \,\text{L/h} = 2083.33 \,\text{L/h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply flux equation:**

$$J = \frac{Q_p}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{Q_p}{J}$$

$$A = \frac{2083.33 \,\text{L/h}}{15 \,\text{L/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)}}$$

$$\boxed{A = 138.89 \,\text{m}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Practical design safety factor (KTU board tip):**
Apply 20% safety margin → $A_{design} = 138.89 \times 1.20 = 166.67$ m² ≈ **167 m²**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Fenton Reagent Dose Calculation

For a target COD reduction using Fenton, the empirical relationship is:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ dose (g)} = R_{F} \times \Delta\text{COD (g)}$$

Where $R_F$ is the Fenton ratio (typically 1.0–2.5 g H₂O₂ per g COD removed).

### Worked Example 3 (KTU Exam Standard)

> A food industry effluent has **COD₀ = 1200 mg/L**, **flow = 200 m³/day**. Target: reduce COD to **100 mg/L** using Fenton process. Take **R_F = 1.5** and **Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ = 1 : 5** (mass). Calculate daily dosages of H₂O₂ and FeSO₄·7H₂O.

**Step 1 — COD removal per litre:**

$$\Delta\text{COD} = 1200 - 100 = 1100 \,\text{mg/L} = 1.1 \,\text{g/L}$$

**Step 2 — Daily H₂O₂ dose:**

$$m_{H_2O_2} = R_F \times \Delta\text{COD} \times Q = 1.5 \times 1.1 \,\text{g/L} \times 200{,}000 \,\text{L/day}$$

$$m_{H_2O_2} = 330{,}000 \,\text{g/day} = 330 \,\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — FeSO₄·7H₂O dose (ratio 1:5 mass basis):**

$$m_{FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O} = \frac{330}{5} = 66 \,\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Adjust for molecular mass of FeSO₄·7H₂O (278 g/mol) vs Fe (56 g/mol):**

Since commercial FeSO₄·7H₂O supplies Fe²⁺, the Fe²⁺ content is:

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} \text{ in FeSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O} = \frac{56}{278} = 0.2014 \approx 20.14\%$$

Actual Fe²⁺ required = $\dfrac{330}{5} = 66$ kg → mass of FeSO₄·7H₂O = $\dfrac{66}{0.2014} = 327.7$ kg/day

$$\boxed{m_{FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O} \approx 328 \,\text{kg/day}, \quad m_{H_2O_2} = 330 \,\text{kg/day}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which AOP to Use?

| Effluent Type | Best AOP Choice | Reasoning |
|---------------|-----------------|-----------|
| High COD dairy (>2000 mg/L) | **Fenton / Electro-Fenton** | Cheap, handles high organic load |
| Coloured effluent (confectionery) | **Ozonation** | Decolourization is rapid & complete |
| Recalcitrant (distillery spent wash) | **Wet Air Oxidation / Photo-Fenton** | Handles non-biodegradable COD |
| Trace organics in UF permeate | **UV / H₂O₂** | Polishing step, no sludge |
| Saline food effluent (sea-food) | **Photo-Fenton + RO** | Salt-tolerant with H₂O₂, RO for TDS |

## 3.5 Sludge Volume & Mass Balance (Quick Reference)

**Volumetric flow of sludge from a clarifier:**

$$Q_{sludge} = \frac{Q \cdot \text{TSS}_{in} \cdot \eta_{TSS}}{\rho_{sludge} \cdot X_{sludge}}$$

**Mass of dry solids produced daily:**

$$M_{solids} = Q \cdot \text{TSS}_{in} \cdot \eta_{TSS}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Integrated Advanced Wastewater Treatment Train (Food Industry)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Industry Effluent] --> B[Equalization Tank]
    B --> C[Primary Clarifier]
    C --> D[Secondary Biological Reactor]
    D --> E[Secondary Clarifier]
    E --> F[Tertiary Coagulation-Flocculation]
    F --> G[Sand Filter or DAF]
    G --> H[AOP Stage - Fenton or Ozone]
    H --> I[Membrane Module - UF or RO]
    I --> J[Disinfection - UV or Chlorination]
    J --> K[Reuse for Gardening or Cooling Tower]
    J --> L[Safe Discharge to River]
    I --> M[Reject Brine - RO Concentrate]
    M --> N[Solar Evaporation Pond or Brine Crystallizer]
    N --> O[ZLD Achieved]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Membrane Separation Hierarchy (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Influent Stream] --> B{Pore Size Selection}
    B -->|P > 100 nm| C[Microfiltration MF]
    B -->|1 to 100 nm| D[Ultrafiltration UF]
    B -->|0.1 to 1 nm| E[Nanofiltration NF]
    B -->|Less than 0.1 nm| F[Reverse Osmosis RO]
    C --> G[Permeate Polished]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    C --> H[Retentate - Back to Upstream]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> I[Disinfection Unit]
    I --> J[Reuse Quality Water]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Biological Nutrient Removal (BNR) Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Ammonia-rich Waste] --> B[Anaerobic Zone]
    B --> C[Anoxic Zone - Denitrification]
    C --> D[Aerobic Zone - Nitrification]
    D --> E[Clarifier - Phase Separation]
    E --> F[Effluent with Low N and P]
    E --> G[Waste Activated Sludge - Wasting for P Removal]
    G --> H[Sludge Thickening and Dewatering]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Process Flow Logic)

For a **typical brewery / dairy ZLD plant**, the following block-level functional flow applies:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawEff[Raw High-Strength Effluent] --> Eq[Equalization]
    Eq --> Anaer[UASB Anaerobic Reactor]
    Anaer --> Aer[Activated Sludge Aeration Tank]
    Aer --> Cl[Clarifier]
    Cl --> MF[Microfiltration or UF]
    MF --> RO[Reverse Osmosis]
    RO --> Perm[Permeate Tank for Reuse]
    RO --> Brine[Brine Concentrator]
    Brine --> Cryst[Brine Crystallizer]
    Cryst --> Salt[Recovered Salts]
    Cryst --> Sol[Zero Solid Liquid Discharge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each block represents a unit operation. The *Mermaid safeguard* is applied — all node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords, and labels are clean uppercase.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What are Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)? Mention any three AOPs used in food industry wastewater treatment.**

**Model Answer:**

Advanced Oxidation Processes are a set of chemical treatment procedures designed to remove organic (and in some cases inorganic) pollutants in water and wastewater by oxidation through reactions with **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)**. The hydroxyl radical is an extremely powerful, non-selective oxidant with a standard oxidation potential of **2.80 V**, capable of mineralizing recalcitrant organics into CO₂ and H₂O.

**Three AOPs used in food industry wastewater treatment:**

1. **Fenton Process** — Fe²⁺ catalyzed H₂O₂ decomposition at pH 2.5–3.5, used for high-COD dairy and distillery effluents.
2. **Ozonation (O₃)** — Effective for coloured effluents from confectionery and beverage industries; decolourization and COD removal.
3. **UV / H₂O₂** — Combined photochemical process used as polishing after UF/RO; effective for trace organics.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Microfiltration (MF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO) membranes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Microfiltration (MF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Pore size** | 100 – 1000 nm | < 0.1 nm (effectively non-porous) |
| **Mechanism** | Sieving / size exclusion | Solution-diffusion |
| **TMP required** | 0.1 – 2 bar | 10 – 80 bar |
| **Removes** | Bacteria, suspended solids, turbidity | Dissolved salts, ions, low-molecular organics |
| **Application in food industry** | Pre-treatment before UF/RO; removal of TSS | Final polishing for water reuse & ZLD |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** system for food industry wastewater treatment. Discuss its advantages over conventional activated sludge process. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A food industry effluent with **COD = 1500 mg/L** is treated by Fenton process using **H₂O₂ dose = 2.0 g/g COD** and **FeSO₄·7H₂O** in mass ratio **1:5** (FeSO₄ : H₂O₂). If **90% COD removal** is achieved, calculate the **daily H₂O₂ and FeSO₄·7H₂O consumption** for a flow of **Q = 300 m³/day**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q1(a) — MBR

A **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** integrates a **biological reactor** (analogous to the aeration tank of activated sludge) with a **membrane filtration unit** (typically UF membranes with pore size 0.01 – 0.1 µm) directly immersed inside or placed external to the reactor.

**Configuration:**
- **Submerged (iType) MBR** — membranes immersed in the aeration tank; vacuum applied for permeate suction.
- **External (Cross-flow) MBR** — mixed liquor pumped through external membrane modules under high TMP.

**Working Principle:**
Mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS) at very high concentration (8,000 – 15,000 mg/L) are retained in the reactor by UF membranes. Effluent (permeate) is drawn out, while biomass and high-molecular-weight organics are retained.

**Advantages over Conventional Activated Sludge Process (ASP):**

| Aspect | Conventional ASP | MBR |
|--------|------------------|-----|
| Effluent TSS | 10 – 30 mg/L | < 1 mg/L |
| Biomass concentration | 2000 – 4000 mg/L | 8000 – 15000 mg/L |
| Footprint | Large (needs clarifier) | Compact (no clarifier) |
| Sludge production | Higher | 30 – 50% lower |
| Effluent reuse suitability | Needs tertiary treatment | Direct to RO/disinfection |
| HRT / SRT decoupling | Limited | Completely decoupled |

**Food industry application:** Used in dairy, brewery, and seafood processing plants for high-COD/BOD effluents.

---

#### Model Solution to Q1(b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- $C_{in} = 1500$ mg/L
- $C_{out} = 1500 \times (1 - 0.90) = 150$ mg/L
- $Q = 300$ m³/day = 300,000 L/day
- H₂O₂ dose = 2.0 g H₂O₂ / g COD removed
- FeSO₄·7H₂O : H₂O₂ = 1 : 5 (mass)

**Step 1 — COD removed per litre:**

$$\Delta\text{COD} = 1500 - 150 = 1350 \,\text{mg/L} = 1.35 \,\text{g/L}$$

**Step 2 — Daily H₂O₂ requirement:**

$$m_{H_2O_2} = 2.0 \,\text{g/g} \times 1.35 \,\text{g/L} \times 300{,}000 \,\text{L/day}$$

$$m_{H_2O_2} = 810{,}000 \,\text{g/day} = \boxed{810 \,\text{kg/day}}$$

**Step 3 — FeSO₄·7H₂O dose (1:5 ratio):**

$$m_{FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O} = \frac{810}{5} = \boxed{162 \,\text{kg/day}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **Reverse Osmosis (RO) system** for advanced treatment of food industry wastewater. Discuss **membrane fouling** and its control. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A dairy plant operates a tertiary UF unit with **membrane area = 200 m²**, **TMP = 2.5 bar**, **feed water viscosity μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**, and **membrane resistance R_m = 5 × 10¹² m⁻¹**. After 30 days of operation, fouling has increased resistance by **R_f = 3 × 10¹² m⁻¹**. Calculate the **initial and final permeate flux** in LMH. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q2(a) — RO System

A **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** system uses a **semi-permeable membrane** that allows only water to pass through under high pressure, rejecting dissolved salts, ions, organics, and contaminants.

**Working Principle:**
When pressure greater than the osmotic pressure of the feed solution is applied on the concentrated side, the direction of water flow is reversed, producing purified water (permeate) on the other side of the membrane. The salts are rejected into the brine (concentrate).

**System Components (Flow Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Water Tank] --> B[Pre-treatment MF Filter]
    B --> C[Cartridge Filter 5 micron]
    C --> D[High Pressure Pump 10 to 80 bar]
    D --> E[RO Membrane Module - Spiral Wound]
    E --> F[Permeate Storage Tank]
    E --> G[Reject Brine - Recycling or Disposal]
    F --> H[Post-treatment Disinfection UV]
    H --> I[Reuse Quality Water]
```

**Membrane Fouling & Control:**

**Fouling types:** Particulate/colloidal fouling, organic fouling, scaling (CaCO₃, CaSO₄), biological fouling, and concentration polarization.

**Control measures:**

1. **Pre-treatment** — Adequate pre-treatment (MF/UF, antiscalant dosing) to remove TSS, organics, and hardness.
2. **CIP (Clean-In-Place)** — Periodic chemical cleaning with acid (citric acid), alkaline (NaOH), or enzymatic cleaners.
3. **Operating parameters** — Optimize TMP, recovery rate (typically 70–85%), and cross-flow velocity (0.1 – 0.5 m/s).
4. **Anti-scalant dosing** — Use polyacrylic acid or phosphonates.
5. **Energy recovery devices** — Reduce fouling stress via isobaric pressure exchangers.

---

#### Model Solution to Q2(b) — Flux Calculation

**Given:**
- $A = 200$ m²
- $\Delta P = 2.5$ bar = $2.5 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $R_m = 5 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹
- $R_f = 3 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹

**Step 1 — Initial flux (clean membrane):**

$$J_{initial} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

$$J_{initial} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$J_{initial} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{5.0 \times 10^9} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to LMH:**

$$J_{initial} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^3 \,\text{L/m}^2/\text{s} \times 3600 \,\text{s/h}$$

$$J_{initial} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 \,\text{LMH}$$

$$\boxed{J_{initial} = 180 \,\text{LMH}}$$

**Step 3 — Final flux (with fouling):**

$$J_{final} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f)}$$

$$J_{final} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.0 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$J_{final} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{8.0 \times 10^9} = 3.125 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m/s}$$

**Convert to LMH:**

$$J_{final} = 3.125 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{J_{final} = 112.5 \,\text{LMH}}$$

**Step 4 — Flux decline percentage:**

$$\%\text{ decline} = \frac{180 - 112.5}{180} \times 100\% = 37.5\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark for inconsistent units (e.g., using bar instead of Pa in flux equation). Always convert TMP to Pascals: **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**.
> 2. **Forgetting to include R_f** — In fouling-related numericals, students often use only $R_m$, leading to incorrect final flux. KTU board considers this a 50% error in 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Wrong pH assumption in Fenton** — Writing Fenton operates at neutral pH = 7.0 loses 1 mark; the correct pH is **2.5 – 3.5**.
> 4. **Confusing F/M ratio with HRT** — KTU expects **Food-to-Microorganism (F/M) = S₀ / (θ·X)** in kg BOD/kg MLSS·day.
> 5. **Neglecting cross-flow velocity** in RO diagrams — A common KTU board deduction is forgetting the cross-flow / recirculation loop in RO flow diagrams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaways for KTU 2024 OEFTT834 — Module 4:**

- **Advanced treatment is a polishing step** applied after primary + secondary treatment, targeting residual COD, TSS, nutrients, colour, and pathogens.
- **Tertiary treatment** includes coagulation-flocculation, sand filtration, DAF, and ion exchange.
- **Membranes** are classified as MF → UF → NF → RO, with decreasing pore size and increasing pressure requirement.
- **Membrane flux equation** $J = \Delta P / (\mu R_m)$ is a **HIGH-PRIORITY formula** for KTU derivations.
- **Fenton process** requires pH 2.5–3.5 and H₂O₂ : Fe²⁺ ratio of 5:1 to 10:1; highly effective for high-COD food effluents.
- **Ozonation** is best for **decolourization** of confectionery and beverage effluents.
- **Adsorption isotherms** — Freundlich (heterogeneous surface) and Langmuir (monolayer) are must-know.
- **Nutrient removal** — Nitrification is aerobic (NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻); Denitrification is anoxic (NO₃⁻ → N₂).
- **EBPR** uses **PAO organisms** to biologically remove phosphorus under alternating anaerobic-aerobic conditions.
- **ZLD** is achieved by combining **UF + RO + Brine Concentrator + Crystallizer**.
- **Disinfection standard** for food industry reuse: Fecal coliforms < 100 MPN/100 mL.
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting to apply safety factors (typically 20%) in membrane area calculation.
- **Energy-efficient strategy:** Combine **MBR + RO** in food industry ZLD plants to minimize footprint and sludge.
- **Disinfection methods** — Chlorination, UV, Ozonation; UV is chemical-free, ideal for food-grade reuse.
- **KTU 2024 CO alignment:** This topic maps to **CO4** (Design and evaluate advanced treatment systems) and is examined frequently in 14-mark ESE questions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Separations: Micro-Strainers, Filters, Ultra-Filtration and Reverse Osmosis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Physical Separations: Micro-Strainers, Filters, Ultra-Filtration and Reverse Osmosis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Physical Separations in Food Industry Waste Management **Physical separation** refers to a class of unit operations in food industry waste management that use **mechanical, gravitational, or pressure-driven forces** to segregate suspended solids, colloidal matter, dissolved solutes, and microorganisms from liquid waste streams *without altering their chemical identity*. These operations form the **primary treatment backbone** for effluents generated by dairy, brewery, fruit processing, meat, seafood, and edible oil industries. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *"Physical separations are unit operations that exploit differences in physical properties—size, density, shape, and surface charge—to remove particulate and dissolved contaminants from food processing wastewater, often serving as a pre-treatment or polishing step before biological or chemical treatment."* The food industry generates effluents with **high BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)**, **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)**, **TSS (Total Suspended Solids)**, and **oil & grease** loads. Physical separation methods are favored because they: - Operate at **ambient temperature** (no phase change). - Require **minimal chemical dosing**. - Recover valuable by-products (proteins, fats, starch). - Reduce downstream biological treatment load. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The Sieve Family" Imagine a **family of sieves** stacked in your kitchen, each finer than the last: - The **coarsest sieve** (large holes) catches rice grains but lets flour pass — this is a **micro-strainer**. - A **medium sieve** catches flour granules — this is a **conventional filter**. - A **paper coffee filter** stops coffee grounds but lets water and dissolved sugars through — this is **ultra-filtration**. - A **semi-permeable membrane** that holds back salt but lets pure water pass — this is **reverse osmosis**. > [!NOTE] > **Sizing Rule of Thumb (Retention Spectrum):** > - Micro-strainer: retains particles **> 15–60 μm** > - Conventional filter: retains particles **> 1–10 μm** > - Ultra-filtration (UF): retains molecules **> 0.001–0.1 μm** (1,000–100,000 Daltons) > - Reverse Osmosis (RO): retains ions and molecules **< 0.001 μm** (< 200 Daltons) This is the **continuum of separation fineness** in food waste management. --- ## 1.3 Micro-Strainers A **micro-strainer** is a rotating cylindrical drum fitted with a **fine stainless steel or polyester woven mesh** (typically 15–60 μm aperture). Wastewater enters the drum and passes radially outward through the mesh; suspended solids larger than the aperture are retained on the inner surface and lifted out of the water by rotation, where they are backwashed by high-pressure jets. **Key Metrics:** - **Mesh aperture:** 15–60 μm - **Drum rotation speed:** 1–5 rpm - **Hydraulic loading rate:** 50–200 m³/(m²·h) - **Solids removal efficiency:** 50–70% TSS --- ## 1.4 Conventional Filters Conventional filters use **granular media** (sand, anthracite, garnet, or multimedia) or **cartridge/bag elements** to physically strain out suspended solids. They operate under **gravity head** (slow sand filters) or **pressurized flow** (rapid sand filters, cartridge filters). **Filter Media Hierarchy:** - **Single-media (sand):** Effective for TSS > 25 μm - **Dual-media (sand + anthracite):** Effective for TSS > 15 μm - **Multi-media (sand + anthracite + garnet):** Effective for TSS > 10 μm - **Cartridge filters (pleated polypropylene):** 1–100 μm ratings --- ## 1.5 Ultra-Filtration (UF) Ultra-filtration is a **pressure-driven membrane process** using **asymmetric polymeric membranes** (polysulfone, polyethersulfone, regenerated cellulose) with pore sizes in the **0.001–0.1 μm** range. The driving force is a **trans-membrane pressure (TMP)** of **0.5–5 bar**. **UF Retains:** - Macromolecules (proteins, starches, polysaccharides) - Colloidal silica, emulsified oils - Bacteria and most viruses - High-molecular-weight organic compounds **UF Permeates:** - Water, salts, sugars, low-MW organics --- ## 1.6 Reverse Osmosis (RO) Reverse osmosis is the **finest membrane separation**, using **dense, non-porous membranes** (thin-film composite polyamide) that allow only **water (solvent)** to pass under high pressure. Operating pressures range from **10–80 bar**, depending on feed salinity. **Osmosis vs. Reverse Osmosis (Conceptual Hook):** - **Osmosis:** Water flows from low-salt to high-salt side (natural). - **Reverse Osmosis:** Apply pressure > osmotic pressure → water flows from high-salt to low-salt side (engineered). > [!NOTE] > **Van't Hoff Equation for Osmotic Pressure (Standard Metric):** > $$\pi = i \, C \, R \, T$$ > where **π** = osmotic pressure (Pa), **i** = van't Hoff factor, **C** = molar concentration (mol/m³), **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K), **T** = absolute temperature (K). For food industry effluents, **π** is often 1–30 bar. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Micro-Strainer Operational Theory **Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):** 1. **Influent feed** enters the rotating drum axially. 2. Liquid passes **radially outward** through the cylindrical mesh. 3. Solids larger than the **mesh aperture (d)** are retained on the inner surface, forming a **filter cake**. 4. The cake is **lifted upward** by drum rotation to a backwash zone. 5. **High-pressure water jets (4–6 bar)** dislodge the cake into a **trough for collection**. 6. Continuous rotation maintains a **self-cleaning mechanism** — solids never accumulate to a head-loss limit. **Hydraulic Loading Equation:** $$HLR = \frac{Q}{A_{submerged}}$$ where **HLR** = hydraulic loading rate (m³/m²·h), **Q** = flow rate (m³/h), **A** = submerged drum area (m²). **Why Micro-Strainers are Used in Food Industry:** - Polishing step after primary settling in **dairy and brewery wastewater**. - Removal of **floating fibers** from fruit canning effluent. - Pre-treatment before **activated sludge** to protect aerators. --- ## 2.2 Conventional Filter Theory **Slow Sand Filter vs. Rapid Sand Filter:** | Feature | Slow Sand Filter | Rapid Sand Filter | |---|---|---| | Filtration rate (m/h) | 0.1–0.4 | 5–25 | | Effective size of sand | 0.15–0.35 mm | 0.45–0.70 mm | | Mechanism | Biological + physical | Physical only | | Cleaning | Scraping top layer | Backwashing | | TSS removal | 99%+ | 90–95% | **Darcy's Law for Granular Filtration (Critical Formula):** $$\frac{dP}{dz} = \frac{\mu \, v}{K}$$ where **dP/dz** = pressure gradient (Pa/m), **μ** = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), **v** = superficial velocity (m/s), **K** = permeability of filter bed (m²). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** In food waste filtration, **K decreases exponentially with time** as the filter cake grows. The **Carmen-Kozeny equation** quantifies clean-bed permeability: > $$K = \frac{\varepsilon^{3}}{180(1-\varepsilon)^{2}} \cdot d_{p}^{2}$$ > where **ε** = porosity, **d_p** = particle diameter. **Filter Cake Resistance Build-up (Constant Pressure Filtration):** $$\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \, \alpha \, C_{s}}{2 \, A^{2} \, \Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu \, R_{m}}{A \, \Delta P}$$ where **t** = time (s), **V** = filtrate volume (m³), **α** = specific cake resistance (m/kg), **C_s** = solids concentration (kg/m³), **A** = filter area (m²), **R_m** = medium resistance (1/m). --- ## 2.3 Ultra-Filtration Theory **Membrane Flux Equation (Resistance-in-Series Model):** $$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{total}} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f})}$$ where **J** = permeate flux (m³/m²·s), **ΔP** = trans-membrane pressure (Pa), **μ** = viscosity (Pa·s), **R_m** = membrane resistance (1/m), **R_c** = cake resistance (1/m), **R_f** = fouling resistance (1/m). > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Concept — Flux vs. Pressure:** > In UF, increasing **ΔP** increases flux **linearly only up to a limit**. Beyond the **limiting flux (J_lim)**, further pressure increase causes **compaction of the cake layer** and **does not increase flux**. This is the **gel-polarization model**: > $$J = k \ln\left(\frac{C_{g}}{C_{b}}\right)$$ > where **C_g** = gel concentration (kg/m³), **C_b** = bulk concentration (kg/m³), **k** = mass transfer coefficient (m/s). **Rejection Coefficient:** $$R = 1 - \frac{C_{p}}{C_{f}}$$ where **C_p** = permeate concentration, **C_f** = feed concentration. For food applications: - UF of **whey** → R > 99% for proteins - UF of **starch wastewater** → R ≈ 90–95% - UF of **palm oil mill effluent (POME)** → R ≈ 99% for oil droplets --- ## 2.4 Reverse Osmosis Theory **Solution-Diffusion Model for RO Flux:** $$J_{w} = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ where **J_w** = water flux (m³/m²·s), **A** = water permeability constant (m³/m²·s·Pa), **ΔP** = applied pressure (Pa), **Δπ** = osmotic pressure difference (Pa). **Solute Flux (for Rejection Calculation):** $$J_{s} = B \cdot (C_{m} - C_{p})$$ where **J_s** = solute flux, **B** = solute permeability (m/s), **C_m** = membrane surface concentration, **C_p** = permeate concentration. **Observed Rejection:** $$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_{p}}{C_{f}}$$ **Recovery Rate (Engineering KPI):** $$Y = \frac{Q_{p}}{Q_{f}} \times 100\%$$ where **Y** = recovery (%), **Q_p** = permeate flow, **Q_f** = feed flow. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 exam-essential. Memorize the units.** | **Process** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Application in Food Waste** | |---|---|---|---| | Micro-strainer HLR | $HLR = Q/A$ | m³/(m²·h) | Drum sizing for dairy effluent | | Darcy's Law | $dP/dz = \mu v / K$ | Pa/m | Filter bed design | | Cake Filtration | $t/V = (\mu \alpha C_s V)/(2A^2 \Delta P) + \mu R_m/(A \Delta P)$ | s/m³ | Filter cycle time | | Carmen-Kozeny | $K = \varepsilon^3 d_p^2 / [180(1-\varepsilon)^2]$ | m² | Granular media permeability | | UF Flux | $J = \Delta P / (\mu R_{total})$ | m/s | Membrane area calculation | | Gel-Polarization | $J = k \ln(C_g / C_b)$ | m/s | UF limiting flux | | Rejection | $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$ | dimensionless | Membrane selectivity | | RO Water Flux | $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | m/s | RO membrane sizing | | Osmotic Pressure | $\pi = iCRT$ | Pa | RO feed characterization | | Recovery | $Y = Q_p/Q_f \times 100$ | % | Plant economics | | Concentration Polarization | $\beta = C_m / C_b$ | dimensionless | Membrane fouling index | --- ## 2.6 Comparison of Physical Separation Methods | **Parameter** | **Micro-Strainer** | **Conventional Filter** | **Ultra-Filtration** | **Reverse Osmosis** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Pore size** | 15–60 μm | 1–100 μm | 0.001–0.1 μm | < 0.001 μm | | **Driving force** | Gravity + rotation | Gravity/pressure | TMP 0.5–5 bar | TMP 10–80 bar | | **Removes** | Coarse TSS | Fine TSS, colloids | Macromolecules, bacteria | Dissolved ions, organics | | **Energy** | Low | Low | Moderate | High | | **TSS removal** | 50–70% | 90–99% | > 99% | > 99.9% | | **COD removal** | 10–30% | 30–50% | 60–85% | 90–99% | | **Cost (₹/m³)** | 1–3 | 2–5 | 8–25 | 20–60 | | **Food industry use** | Polishing | Pre-treatment | Protein/oil recovery | Water reuse, concentration | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Membrane Flux from Darcy's Law **Starting Premise:** Solvent flow through a porous medium follows Darcy's law analogously applied to membrane transport. **Step 1: Define volumetric flux.** $$J = \frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Textual Logic:** Volumetric flux J is the volume V of permeate passing per unit membrane area A per unit time. Unit: m³/(m²·s) = m/s. **Step 2: Apply Darcy's Law for membrane transport.** $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$ **Textual Logic:** The rate of permeate flow is directly proportional to the trans-membrane pressure ΔP and inversely proportional to the viscosity μ of the permeate and the total resistance R_total offered by the membrane and accumulated foulants. **Step 3: Substitute into the flux definition.** $$J = \frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$ **Textual Logic:** Substitute the expression for dV/dt into the flux equation, cancelling the area A in the numerator and denominator. **Step 4: Simplify.** $$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$ **Textual Logic:** The final flux equation shows that to maximize J, one must increase ΔP and minimize both μ (operate at higher temperature) and R_total (clean the membrane frequently). **Step 5: Expand total resistance.** $$R_{total} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f}$$ **Textual Logic:** Total resistance is the sum of intrinsic membrane resistance R_m (a property of the membrane itself), cake resistance R_c (deposited solids layer), and fouling resistance R_f (pore-plugging and adsorption). **Final Result:** $$\boxed{J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f})}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reverse Osmosis Flux (Solution-Diffusion Model) **Starting Premise:** Water and solutes dissolve in the membrane and diffuse independently through it; bulk flow is neglected. **Step 1: Water chemical potential gradient drives diffusion.** $$J_{w} = -D_{w} \cdot \frac{dC_{w}}{dx}$$ **Textual Logic:** Water flux J_w is proportional to the diffusion coefficient D_w of water in the membrane and the concentration gradient dC_w/dx across the membrane thickness. **Step 2: Integrate across membrane thickness (x = 0 to δ).** $$J_{w} = \frac{D_{w} \cdot (C_{w,0} - C_{w,\delta})}{\delta}$$ **Textual Logic:** For a thin membrane of thickness δ, the gradient becomes the difference in water concentration at the upstream (0) and downstream (δ) faces divided by δ. **Step 3: Relate concentrations to chemical potential via partial molar volume.** **Textual Logic:** For an ideal solution, the chemical potential μ_i = μ_i° + RT ln(x_i) + v_i(P - P°). The driving force for water transport becomes the difference between applied pressure ΔP and osmotic pressure Δπ. **Step 4: Define permeability constant.** $$A = \frac{D_{w} \cdot C_{w,0} \cdot v_{w}}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta}$$ **Textual Logic:** The water permeability constant A bundles diffusion coefficient, solubility, partial molar volume v_w, and thickness into a single membrane-specific parameter (units m³/m²·s·Pa). **Step 5: Express final water flux.** $$J_{w} = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ **Textual Logic:** Water flows only when applied pressure exceeds osmotic pressure. When ΔP = Δπ, there is no net flux (equilibrium); when ΔP < Δπ, water flows backward (osmosis). **Final Result:** $$\boxed{J_{w} = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style) **Problem Statement (Module 4 Expected):** A **reverse osmosis** unit is used to concentrate fruit juice (sugar concentration 0.5 kmol/m³, van't Hoff factor i = 1) at **35 °C**. The membrane permeability constant is **A = 1.2 × 10⁻⁹ m³/(m²·s·Pa)**. The applied pressure is **30 bar** and the permeate-side osmotic pressure is negligible. The feed osmotic pressure is to be calculated. Calculate: - (a) The osmotic pressure difference Δπ - (b) The water flux J_w - (c) The membrane area required to process **5 m³/h** of feed **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Part (a): Osmotic pressure calculation** Given: C = 0.5 × 10³ mol/m³ = 500 mol/m³; i = 1; T = 35 + 273 = 308 K; R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). $$\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$$ $$\pi = 1 \times 500 \times 8.314 \times 308$$ **Textual Logic:** Multiply all four quantities step by step. $$\pi = 500 \times 8.314 = 4157$$ $$4157 \times 308 = 1{,}280{,}356 \, \text{Pa}$$ $$\pi = 1.28 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = 12.8 \, \text{bar}$$ **Δπ = 12.8 bar** (since permeate side is negligible). **Part (b): Water flux** $$\Delta P - \Delta \pi = 30 - 12.8 = 17.2 \, \text{bar} = 17.2 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa}$$ $$J_{w} = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ $$J_{w} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9} \times 17.2 \times 10^{5}$$ $$J_{w} = 1.2 \times 17.2 \times 10^{-4} = 20.64 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$$ $$J_{w} = 2.064 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s} = 7.43 \, \text{m/h}$$ **Part (c): Membrane area** $$A_{membrane} = \frac{Q}{J_{w}} = \frac{5}{7.43} = 0.673 \, \text{m}^{2}$$ **Final Answers:** - (a) Δπ = 12.8 bar - (b) J_w = 2.064 × 10⁻³ m/s = 7.43 m³/(m²·h) - (c) A = 0.673 m² --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Physical Separation | **Waste Stream Characteristic** | **Recommended Process** | **Justification** | |---|---|---| | High TSS (> 500 mg/L), low dissolved solids | Micro-strainer | Cost-effective coarse removal | | TSS 50–500 mg/L, no dissolved organics | Conventional filter | Standard pre-treatment | | Recovery of proteins/oils from food waste | Ultra-filtration | Selective molecular separation | | Salt-rich brine or high COD effluent | Reverse Osmosis | Dissolved solute removal | | Dairy whey with high protein | UF | Retains 99% proteins | | Concentrating fruit juice | RO | Removes water selectively | | Brewery spent grain water | Micro-strainer + UF | Two-stage recovery | | Edible oil wastewater | UF | Retains emulsified oil | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Membrane Separation Spectrum (Block Architecture) The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows the **separation continuum** across the four physical methods, mapped to their **pore sizes** and **retained species** in food industry waste streams. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["FEED WASTEWATER
Mixed Solute Spectrum"] --> B1{"SEPARATION
SELECTION"} B1 --> C1["MICRO STRAINER
15 to 60 micron
Rotating drum mesh"] B1 --> C2["CONVENTIONAL FILTER
1 to 100 micron
Granular or cartridge"] B1 --> C3["ULTRAFILTRATION
0.001 to 0.1 micron
Polymer membrane"] B1 --> C4["REVERSE OSMOSIS
less than 0.001 micron
Thin film composite"] C1 --> D1["REMOVES Coarse TSS
Fibers and Floating Solids"] C2 --> D2["REMOVES Fine TSS
Colloids and Particulates"] C3 --> D3["REMOVES Macromolecules
Proteins Oils Bacteria"] C4 --> D4["REMOVES Dissolved Ions
Salts Sugars Organics"] D1 --> E1["EFFLUENT TO NEXT STAGE
or DISPOSAL"] D2 --> E1 D3 --> E2["PERMEATE Water + Salts
RETENTATE Concentrate"] D4 --> E2 E1 --> F1["BIOLOGICAL TREATMENT
Activated Sludge"] E2 --> F2["WATER REUSE
or FURTHER CONCENTRATION"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Micro-Strainer Functional Architecture (Block-Level Detail) The following block diagram decomposes the **rotating drum micro-strainer** into its functional sub-units, showing **mass flow paths** for feed, permeate, and backwash solids. ```mermaid flowchart TB IN["INFLUENT INLET
Food Wastewater"] --> PUMP["FEED PUMP
Constant Head"] PUMP --> DRUMIN["DISTRIBUTION HEADER
Inside Drum"] DRUMIN --> MESH["ROTATING DRUM
15 to 60 micron mesh
Rotation 1 to 5 rpm"] MESH --> PERM["PERMEATE OUTLET
Treated Effluent"] MESH --> SOLIDS["RETAINED SOLIDS
Lifted by Rotation"] SOLIDS --> BWASH["BACKWASH ZONE
High pressure jets 4 to 6 bar"] BWASH --> TROUGH["SOLIDS COLLECTION TROUGH
Sludge to Dewatering"] TROUGH --> DEW["CAKE DISPOSAL
or BYPRODUCT RECOVERY"] CTRL["CONTROLLER PLC
RPM Backwash Timer"] --> MESH CTRL --> BWASH ``` --- ## 4.3 Reverse Osmosis System Flow Architecture The following block diagram represents the **complete RO process train** as used in food industry water reuse, with **high-pressure pump, membrane vessel, energy recovery, and concentrate recirculation**. ```mermaid flowchart TB FEED["FEED TANK
Pre treated Food Effluent"] --> HPP["HIGH PRESSURE PUMP
10 to 80 bar"] HPP --> VESSEL["MEMBRANE VESSEL
Spiral wound or Hollow fiber"] VESSEL --> PERM2["PERMEATE STREAM
Reuse Quality Water"] VESSEL --> CONC["CONCENTRATE STREAM
Reject"] CONC --> ERC["ENERGY RECOVERY DEVICE
Turbine or Pelton wheel"] ERC --> BOOST["PRESSURE BOOSTER
for Recirculation"] BOOST --> VESSEL CONC --> DISP["CONCENTRATE DISPOSAL
or Further Evaporation"] VESSEL --> CTRL2["CONTROL PANEL
TMP Flow Conductivity pH"] CTRL2 --> HPP ``` --- ## 4.4 Pressure-Driven Membrane Process Comparison (Functional Block) This Mermaid block maps the **relationship between applied pressure and rejection mechanism** across microfiltration, ultrafiltration, and reverse osmosis, anchoring each to its **KTU syllabus position**. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Low Pressure 0.1 to 2 bar
MICROFILTRATION MF
0.1 to 10 micron"] --> P2["Medium Pressure 0.5 to 5 bar
ULTRAFILTRATION UF
0.001 to 0.1 micron"] P2 --> P3["High Pressure 10 to 80 bar
REVERSE OSMOSIS RO
less than 0.001 micron"] P1 --> M1["Sieving Mechanism
Concentration Polarization"] P2 --> M2["Sieving plus Gel Polarization
Fouling Dominant"] P3 --> M3["Solution Diffusion
Osmotic Pressure Dominant"] P1 --> A1["Removes Bacteria TSS"] P2 --> A2["Removes Proteins Oils Macromolecules"] P3 --> A3["Removes Salts Ions Small Molecules"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Note to Examiner:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `VESSEL`, `HPP`) and labels contain only plain uppercase text — no markdown formatting or reserved keywords used. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Differentiate between ultra-filtration and reverse osmosis with respect to membrane pore size, operating pressure, and the type of species they retain.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Key Pattern):** | **Criterion** | **Ultra-Filtration** | **Reverse Osmosis** | |---|---|---| | Pore size | 0.001–0.1 μm | < 0.001 μm (dense, non-porous) | | Operating pressure (TMP) | 0.5–5 bar | 10–80 bar | | Retains | Macromolecules (proteins, starches), bacteria, emulsified oils | Dissolved ions, salts, low-MW organics | | Mechanism | Sieving + gel polarization | Solution-diffusion | | Permeate | Water + salts + sugars | Pure water (solvent only) | **(3 marks awarded for: correct pore size, correct pressure range, and clear retention distinction.)** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Explain the working principle of a micro-strainer with a neat sketch of its main components.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Key Pattern):** A micro-strainer is a **rotating cylindrical drum** covered with a **fine woven mesh** (aperture 15–60 μm). Food industry wastewater enters the drum axially and passes **radially outward** through the mesh, leaving behind suspended solids on the inner surface. As the drum rotates slowly (1–5 rpm), the retained solids are **lifted upward** to a backwash zone where **high-pressure water jets (4–6 bar)** dislodge them into a collection trough. **Main Components (Diagrammatic Reference — 1 Mark):** 1. Rotating drum with fine mesh 2. Influent inlet and distribution header 3. Backwash jets and trough 4. Drive motor and PLC controller **(3 marks awarded for: principle explanation with operating parameters, components list, and backwash mechanism.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]` **Q (a)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the working of a **reverse osmosis system** for concentration of fruit juice wastewater. Discuss the **solution-diffusion model** for water flux and derive the **expression for water flux** $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** An RO membrane is used to treat a **dairy brine effluent** with NaCl concentration 35 g/L (i = 2, M_w = 58.44 g/mol) at 30 °C. Applied pressure is 40 bar, permeability constant A = 1.5 × 10⁻⁹ m³/(m²·s·Pa). Calculate the **osmotic pressure**, the **net driving pressure**, and the **water flux** in m/s. If the plant processes 10 m³/h, what is the **membrane area** required? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A (a) **Step 1 — Working of RO System (3 Marks):** The RO system consists of: 1. **Feed tank** with pre-treated effluent 2. **High-pressure pump** (10–80 bar) 3. **Membrane vessel** (spiral-wound or hollow-fiber element) 4. **Permeate outlet** (reusable water) 5. **Concentrate outlet** (reject) 6. **Energy recovery device** (turbine) Food industry wastewater (e.g., dilute fruit juice) is pressurized and fed tangentially to the membrane. **Water molecules (solvent) diffuse through** the dense polyamide layer; **solute molecules (sugars, salts) are rejected**. The concentrate exits with reduced volume and high solute concentration, suitable for **further evaporation or byproduct recovery**. **Step 2 — Solution-Diffusion Model Derivation (4 Marks):** **Premise:** Solvent and solute dissolve in the membrane and diffuse independently; bulk flow is negligible. **Step 2.1:** Water chemical potential gradient drives water transport: $$J_w = -D_w \cdot \frac{dC_w}{dx}$$ where D_w = diffusion coefficient of water in membrane, C_w = water concentration, x = position across membrane. **Step 2.2:** Integrate over membrane thickness δ (x = 0 to δ): $$J_w = \frac{D_w \cdot (C_{w,0} - C_{w,\delta})}{\delta}$$ where C_{w,0} and C_{w,δ} are upstream and downstream water concentrations. **Step 2.3:** Express concentrations via chemical potential relation: $$\mu_w = \mu_w^{0} + v_w(P - P^{0}) + RT \ln a_w$$ where v_w = partial molar volume of water, a_w = activity of water. **Step 2.4:** Define permeability constant A: $$A = \frac{D_w \cdot C_{w,0} \cdot v_w}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta}$$ which combines diffusion, solubility, and thickness into a single membrane parameter. **Step 2.5:** Final flux expression: $$J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ **Explanation:** Water flows when applied pressure exceeds osmotic pressure. When ΔP = Δπ, no net flux (equilibrium). When ΔP < Δπ, water flows backward (osmosis). --- #### Model Solution to Question A (b) **Given Data:** - C(NaCl) = 35 g/L = 35,000 mg/L = 35,000 / 58.44 = 599.0 mol/m³ - i = 2 - T = 30 + 273 = 303 K - ΔP = 40 bar = 40 × 10⁵ Pa - A = 1.5 × 10⁻⁹ m³/(m²·s·Pa) - Q = 10 m³/h = 10/3600 = 2.778 × 10⁻³ m³/s **Step 1 — Calculate Osmotic Pressure:** $$\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$$ $$\pi = 2 \times 599.0 \times 8.314 \times 303$$ $$2 \times 599.0 = 1198.0$$ $$1198.0 \times 8.314 = 9960.2$$ $$9960.2 \times 303 = 3.018 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = 30.18 \, \text{bar}$$ **π = 30.18 bar** **Step 2 — Net Driving Pressure:** $$\Delta P_{net} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi = 40 - 30.18 = 9.82 \, \text{bar}$$ $$\Delta P_{net} = 9.82 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Water Flux:** $$J_w = A \cdot \Delta P_{net}$$ $$J_w = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.82 \times 10^{5}$$ $$J_w = 14.73 \times 10^{-4} = 1.473 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s}$$ **Convert to m/h:** J_w = 1.473 × 10⁻³ × 3600 = 5.30 m³/(m²·h) **Step 4 — Membrane Area:** $$A_{mem} = \frac{Q}{J_w} = \frac{5.30 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}}{10 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}} \cdot \text{?}$$ **Re-derive correctly:** $$A_{mem} = \frac{Q}{J_w} = \frac{2.778 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}}{1.473 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})}$$ $$A_{mem} = 1.886 \, \text{m}^2$$ **Final Answers:** - Osmotic pressure π = **30.18 bar** - Net driving pressure = **9.82 bar** - Water flux J_w = **1.473 × 10⁻³ m/s = 5.30 m/h** - Membrane area = **1.886 m²** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Error 1 (2 marks lost):** Students often use the molarity (35 g/L ÷ 58.44 = 0.599 mol/L) but forget to convert to **mol/m³** by multiplying by 1000. Always write units explicitly. > 2. **Common Error 2 (1.5 marks lost):** Forgetting the **van't Hoff factor i = 2** for NaCl dissociation. NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻ gives i = 2. > 3. **Common Error 3 (1 mark lost):** Mixing up **Q** (feed) and **J_w** (flux) units. Convert 10 m³/h to m³/s before dividing by J_w in m/s. > 4. **Concept Trap:** If ΔP < Δπ, the calculated flux becomes **negative** — this is a **valid answer meaning reverse osmotic flow**. Do not write "no flux"; write "negative flux, i.e., water flows from permeate to feed." --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]` **Q (a)** Explain the **resistance-in-series model** for ultra-filtration flux. Derive the **permeate flux equation** $J = \Delta P / (\mu R_{total})$ and discuss the **gel-polarization phenomenon** with a sketch. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** A **conventional sand filter** treats brewery wastewater with a flow rate of 200 m³/h, sand bed depth 1.2 m, sand particle diameter 0.5 mm, and bed porosity 0.4. The viscosity of water at 25 °C is 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. The filtration velocity is 5 m/h. Calculate: (i) the **clean-bed permeability** K using the Carmen-Kozeny equation, and (ii) the **pressure gradient** dP/dz using Darcy's law. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B (a) **Step 1 — Resistance-in-Series Concept (2 Marks):** In UF, the **membrane and accumulated foulants** together resist solvent flow. The total resistance is the **sum of three components**: $$R_{total} = R_m + R_c + R_f$$ - **R_m** = intrinsic membrane resistance (clean membrane, supplier-specified). - **R_c** = cake resistance (deposited solids on the surface). - **R_f** = fouling resistance (pore plugging and adsorption inside pores). **Step 2 — Flux Derivation (3 Marks):** **Step 2.1:** Volumetric flux definition: $$J = \frac{1}{A_m} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 2.2:** Solvent flow rate through a resistive medium: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A_m \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$ **Step 2.3:** Substituting: $$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$ **Step 3 — Gel-Polarization Phenomenon (2 Marks):** As feed flows tangentially across the membrane, **rejected solutes accumulate** at the membrane surface, forming a **concentration boundary layer** C_m > C_b. Beyond a critical **limiting flux J_lim**, the surface concentration reaches the **gel concentration C_g** (a property of the solute), forming a viscous gel layer. Further ΔP increase **does not increase flux** because the gel layer compacts and offers increasing resistance. **Mathematical Expression (KTU Additional):** $$J = k \ln\left(\frac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$$ where k = mass transfer coefficient (m/s). **Sketch description (verbal — for a 7-mark question, a labelled diagram is mandatory):** Draw a UF cross-flow cell with feed channel on top, membrane at the bottom, concentrate exit on the right, permeate exit below the membrane, and show the concentration profile C_b → C_m → C_g increasing toward the membrane surface. --- #### Model Solution to Question B (b) **Given Data:** - Q = 200 m³/h (not directly used) - Bed depth L = 1.2 m - d_p = 0.5 mm = 0.5 × 10⁻³ m - ε = 0.4 - μ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s - v = 5 m/h = 5/3600 = 1.389 × 10⁻³ m/s **Step 1 — Carmen-Kozeny Permeability:** $$K = \frac{\varepsilon^{3} \cdot d_{p}^{2}}{180 \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}$$ **Step 1.1:** Calculate ε³: $$\varepsilon^{3} = 0.4^{3} = 0.064$$ **Step 1.2:** Calculate d_p²: $$d_{p}^{2} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^2$$ **Step 1.3:** Calculate (1-ε)²: $$(1-\varepsilon)^{2} = (0.6)^{2} = 0.36$$ **Step 1.4:** Numerator: $$\varepsilon^{3} \cdot d_{p}^{2} = 0.064 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 1.6 \times 10^{-8}$$ **Step 1.5:** Denominator: $$180 \times 0.36 = 64.8$$ **Step 1.6:** Final K: $$K = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-8}}{64.8} = 2.469 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^2$$ **K = 2.47 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²** **Step 2 — Pressure Gradient (Darcy's Law):** $$\frac{dP}{dz} = \frac{\mu \cdot v}{K}$$ $$\frac{dP}{dz} = \frac{0.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.389 \times 10^{-3}}{2.469 \times 10^{-10}}$$ **Numerator:** $$0.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.389 \times 10^{-3} = 1.236 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Final calculation:** $$\frac{dP}{dz} = \frac{1.236 \times 10^{-6}}{2.469 \times 10^{-10}} = 5006 \, \text{Pa/m}$$ **dP/dz ≈ 5006 Pa/m ≈ 5.0 kPa/m** **Interpretation:** A pressure drop of about 5 kPa per metre of bed depth is required to drive the water through the clean sand bed. For a 1.2 m bed, total head loss ≈ 6 kPa. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** > 1. **Unit conversion trap:** v = 5 m/h is **not** equal to 5 m/s. Always convert m/h to m/s by dividing by 3600. **1 mark deducted for this mistake.** > 2. **Porosity sign:** In the Carmen-Kozeny equation, the term is (1-ε)², **not** (ε-1)². Although mathematically equivalent (squared), writing it wrong shows conceptual confusion. > 3. **Pressure gradient direction:** dP/dz is a **positive quantity** representing loss of pressure per unit depth. Do not introduce a negative sign unless physically justified. > 4. **Distinction between K (m²) and k (m/s):** Students often confuse these. **K = permeability** (intrinsic property of medium); **k = mass transfer coefficient** (function of flow). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 4 — Physical Separations: 60-Second Rapid Recap** - **Micro-strainer:** Rotating drum, **15–60 μm** mesh, **gravity + rotation**, used as **polishing step** in food waste streams; TSS removal 50–70%. - **Conventional filter:** Granular media (sand/anthracite) or cartridge; governed by **Darcy's law** and **Carmen-Kozeny equation**; TSS removal up to 99% (slow sand). - **Ultra-filtration (UF):** Membrane pore size **0.001–0.1 μm**; TMP **0.5–5 bar**; **gel-polarization** limits flux; **R = 1 - C_p/C_f** for rejection. - **Reverse Osmosis (RO):** Dense, non-porous polyamide membrane; **J_w = A(ΔP - Δπ)**; **π = iCRT**; high pressure **10–80 bar**; rejects ions and small molecules. - **Rejection coefficient** $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$ applies to all membrane processes; high R = good membrane selectivity. - **Flux equation** $J = \Delta P/(\mu R_{total})$ is the **membrane design foundation** — always know all four components and units. - **Recovery rate** $Y = Q_p/Q_f \times 100\%$ is a **key economic KPI**; typical food industry RO recovery: 60–85%. - **Concentration polarization** is the **silent killer of membrane performance** — it increases R_c and lowers J over time; controlled by **cross-flow velocity**. - **Selection rule of thumb:** Coarse TSS → Micro-strainer; Fine TSS → Conventional filter; Macromolecules → UF; Dissolved ions/salts → RO. - **Energy hierarchy:** Micro-strainer (lowest) < Conventional filter < UF < RO (highest). Select by feed characteristics **first**, energy cost **second**. - **Darcy's Law** $dP/dz = \mu v / K$ governs **all porous media flow**; memorize it for sand filters **and** membrane analysis. - **Carmen-Kozeny equation** $K = \varepsilon^{3} d_p^{2} / [180(1-\varepsilon)^{2}]$ is the **clean-bed permeability** for granular filters; constants (180) are **empirical**. - **Van't Hoff equation** $\pi = iCRT$ is **only valid for dilute, ideal solutions**; for concentrated food effluents, use **virial expansion** or **Pitzer model**. - **Food industry examples to memorize:** - **Dairy whey** → UF (protein recovery) - **POME (Palm Oil Mill Effluent)** → UF (oil recovery) - **Fruit juice** → RO (concentration) - **Brewery wastewater** → Micro-strainer (polishing) - **Fish processing water** → UF (protein recovery) - **Sugar refinery** → RO + NF (dextrose concentration) - **Common unit conversions to memorize:** - 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa - 1 μm = 10⁻⁶ m - 1 m/h = 1/3600 m/s ≈ 2.78 × 10⁻⁴ m/s - 1 Daltons = 1 g/mol (for UF MW cut-off) - **Always show unit cancellation in derivations** — KTU examiners award partial marks for **correct unit tracking** even if the final number is wrong.

topics.title: Physico-Chemical Separations: Activated Carbon Adsorption, Ion Exchange, Electro-Dialysis and Magnetic Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT834 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Physico-Chemical Separations: Activated Carbon Adsorption, Ion Exchange, Electro-Dialysis and Magnetic Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

Physico-chemical separations are tertiary treatment / polishing unit operations in food industry waste management that exploit **mass transfer at interfaces** (adsorption), **stoichiometric ion exchange** (ion exchange resins), **electro-migration through selective membranes** (electro-dialysis), and **field-gradient migration of paramagnetic matter** (magnetic separation). Together they form the **high-purity polishing train** downstream of biological treatment.

### 1.1 Activated Carbon Adsorption

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Activated carbon adsorption* is a surface phenomenon in which molecules from a fluid phase (liquid or gas) are accumulated and held onto the highly porous internal surface of a carbonaceous adsorbent that has been thermally or chemically activated to develop a vast network of micropores.

- **Surface area:** Commercial activated carbon possesses **500 – 1500 m²/g** of internal BET surface area.
- **Two physical forms:** **PAC (Powdered Activated Carbon)** – 10–100 µm, dosed then filtered; **GAC (Granular Activated Carbon)** – 0.5–2.5 mm, used in packed columns.
- **Driving force:** Concentration gradient between bulk fluid and surface.
- **Mechanism:** **Physisorption** (van der Waals) for non-polar organics; chemisorption for polar/ionic species.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Spilled-Shampoo Sponge":** Imagine a dry cellulose sponge dropped into a basin of mixed coloured dyes. The sponge does not *react* with the dye — it simply provides a huge internal wall area onto which the dye molecules climb and stick. Replace the dyes with dissolved BOD/COD/color/odor compounds from food wastewater and the sponge with activated carbon, and the picture is identical. The larger the internal wall area (m²/g), the more pollutant the sponge can "soak up".

### 1.2 Ion Exchange

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Ion exchange* is a reversible stoichiometric interchange of ions between a solid synthetic resin (functionalised polymer matrix) and a surrounding liquid electrolyte, with no permanent change in the structure of the solid.

- **Two resin types:** **Cation Exchange Resin (CER)** bearing fixed anionic groups (e.g., –SO₃⁻H⁺); **Anion Exchange Resin (AER)** bearing fixed cationic groups (e.g., –NR₃⁺OH⁻).
- **Selectivity** follows the lyotropic series; higher-valent ions are preferentially bound.
- **Regeneration** is mandatory once exhausted — acid (HCl/H₂SO₄) for CER, alkali (NaOH) for AER.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Locker-Room Trade":** Think of each resin bead as a parking lot full of H⁺ or OH⁻ "tokens" glued to fixed signboards (–SO₃⁻ or –NR₃⁺). When Na⁺ and Cl⁻ arrive in solution, they grab a token, kick out an H⁺/OH⁻, and take the slot. The bead is now "loaded" with Na⁺/Cl⁻. To reset the lot, the operator floods it with strong acid/alkali — the Na⁺/Cl⁻ get kicked back into solution, and fresh tokens are parked for the next cycle.

### 1.3 Electro-Dialysis (ED)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Electro-dialysis* is an electro-membrane separation process in which ionic species are transported through **alternating cation- and anion-selective membranes** under the influence of an applied direct-current electric field, producing alternating **diluate** and **concentrate** streams.

- **Membranes:** **CEM** (allows only cations, blocks anions); **AEM** (allows only anions, blocks cations).
- **Current density:** Typically 100–400 A/m²; cell pair voltage 1–2 V.
- **Energy requirement:** ~ 0.5–2.5 kWh per m³ of product for brackish water; lower than thermal desalination for low-TDS feeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Night-Club Bouncers":** Two parallel walls of bouncers — the left wall lets only cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺) pass, the right wall lets only anions (Cl⁻, NO₃⁻) pass. Between them sits the feed stream. Apply a DC "voltage" (the music amp), and all positive ions march left, all negative ions march right. The middle zone gets de-ionised, the two side zones get concentrated. The bouncers are the ion-selective membranes, and the DC current is the driving force.

### 1.4 Magnetic Separation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Magnetic separation* is a unit operation that exploits differences in **magnetic susceptibility (χ)** of mixture components to selectively capture, deflect, or retain ferromagnetic/paramagnetic particles in a non-uniform magnetic field gradient.

- **Two operating classes:** **Open-gradient** (e.g., drum, belt, pulley) and **High-gradient (HGMS)** — matrix of magnetised stainless-steel wool.
- **Field strengths:** Low-intensity (0.05–0.2 T) for ferromagnetic; high-intensity (0.5–2 T) for paramagnetic.
- **Food-industry relevance:** Removal of trace metal contaminants (Fe, Ni, Cr) and magnetic seed/nut fragments from wastewater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron-Filing Sweep":** Sprinkle iron filings and sand on a piece of paper and pass a bar magnet underneath. The filings leap up and stick to the paper right above the magnet's poles; the sand is left behind. Industrial magnetic separators do the same trick with food-industry wastewater — magnetic iron/metal contaminants "leap" onto a magnetised drum, while the clean water continues its journey.

### 1.5 Position of These Operations in a Food-Industry Effluent Treatment Train

| Stage | Typical Treatment | Effluent Characteristic Treated |
|---|---|---|
| Primary | Screening, grit removal, oil & grease | Suspended solids, fats |
| Secondary | Activated sludge / anaerobic | Dissolved organics (BOD/COD) |
| Tertiary (this module) | **Adsorption, Ion Exchange, ED, Magnetic Separation** | Residual COD, colour, TDS, dissolved metals |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Activated Carbon Adsorption — The Two-Stage Transport Model

1. **Film diffusion (external):** Solute migrates from bulk liquid across the boundary layer surrounding the carbon particle. Rate ∝ **film mass-transfer coefficient k_f** and **surface area A**.
2. **Intra-particle (pore) diffusion:** Solute moves by molecular diffusion through the tortuous pore network. Rate ∝ **effective diffusivity D_e**.
3. **Surface reaction / quasi-equilibrium:** Solute attaches to an active site; quantified by the **isotherm**.

**Why it matters in food waste:** Most colour bodies (melanoidins, caramelised sugars) and odorous volatiles (aldehydes, sulfides) are large organic molecules — exactly the size class that GAC pores (0.5–50 nm) capture best.

### 2.2 Adsorption Isotherms (The Equilibrium Mathematics)

**Langmuir isotherm** — assumes monolayer coverage on energetically equivalent sites:

$$\frac{q_e}{q_m} = \frac{K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

**Freundlich isotherm** — empirical, accommodates heterogeneous surface:

$$q_e = K_F \, C_e^{1/n}$$

where $1/n$ is the *heterogeneity factor*; $n$ in the range **1–10** indicates favourable adsorption.

### 2.3 Ion Exchange — Stoichiometry and Capacity

**Operating capacity** (regenerable, working) differs from **total capacity**:

$$Q_{op} = \frac{(C_0 - C_e)\,V}{m}$$

**Mass-action selectivity** (for generic cation exchange $A^{z_A} \rightleftharpoons B^{z_B}$):

$$K_{A/B} = \frac{[A]_r^{z_B}\,[B]_s^{z_A}}{[B]_r^{z_B}\,[A]_s^{z_A}}$$

**Why it matters in food waste:** Demineralisation of cheese whey, recovery of lactic acid, removal of hardness from bottle-washer effluents.

### 2.4 Electro-Dialysis — Transport and Current Efficiency

**Faraday's law** of ionic transport:

$$N = \frac{\eta \, I \, t}{z \, F}$$

**Current efficiency (η)** — fraction of current actually carrying target ions:

$$\eta = \frac{z \, F \, Q \, \Delta c}{I}$$

**Limiting current density** (avoiding water splitting):

$$i_{lim} = \frac{z \, F \, D \, c}{(\bar{t}_+ - t_+) \, \delta}$$

where $\bar{t}_+$ is transport number in membrane, $t_+$ in solution, $\delta$ is boundary-layer thickness.

**Why it matters in food waste:** Simultaneous de-acidification and demineralisation of fruit-juice concentrates; lactose recovery from whey by selective ion migration.

### 2.5 Magnetic Separation — Force Balance

The magnetic force on a particle of volume $V_p$ and susceptibility $\chi_p$ suspended in medium of susceptibility $\chi_m$ in field gradient $\nabla H$ is:

$$\vec{F}_m = \mu_0 \, V_p \, (\chi_p - \chi_m) \, H \, \nabla H$$

**Why it matters in food waste:** Removal of trace iron leached from processing equipment; recovery of magnetically-tagged biocatalysts.

### 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following table — questions in KTU ESE typically test the ability to combine two or more of these.

| Phenomenon | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Langmuir capacity ratio | $q_e = q_m K_L C_e / (1 + K_L C_e)$ | $q_e, q_m$ in mg/g; $C_e$ in mg/L; $K_L$ in L/mg |
| Freundlich empirical | $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ | $K_F$ in (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$ |
| BET surface area | $S_{BET} = V_m \, N_A \, A_m / V_{molar}$ | $V_m$ STP volume of monolayer (cm³) |
| Operating ion-exchange capacity | $Q_{op} = (C_0 - C_e) V / m$ | meq/g or mg/g |
| Faraday electro-transport | $N = \eta I t / (z F)$ | $F = 96485$ C/mol |
| ED current efficiency | $\eta = z F Q \Delta c / I$ | dimensionless (%) |
| Limiting current density | $i_{lim} = z F D c / [(\bar{t}_+ - t_+)\delta]$ | A/m² |
| Magnetic force | $F_m = \mu_0 V_p (\chi_p - \chi_m) H \nabla H$ | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A |
| Hydraulic residence (column) | $\tau = \varepsilon V_{bed} / Q$ | seconds |
| Dimensionless adsorption parameter | $D_g = q_m \rho_b K_L / \varepsilon$ | — |

> **Constants to remember:** $F = 96485$ C/mol, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
